data.title: Britain’s biggest data centre to be powered by gas
data.description:
Britain’s largest data centre will be powered by gas because of delays connecting to the grid.

The £9bn development in Bedfordshire, which is three times the size of the UK’s existing biggest data centre, will be entirely fuelled by a gas generation station until 2034 to ensure a “reliable” supply, planning documents show.

Steve Reed, the Housing Secretary, last week gave permission for the data centre to count as a nationally significant project, meaning it can bypass the council when seeking planning permission.

A growing number of data centres in Britain are planning to rely on gas because of a decade-long grid backlog, putting the AI industry at odds with Labour’s net zero policies.

MPs have raised concerns that net zero rules are incompatible with Sir Keir Starmer’s AI ambitions.

The Quest Park site in Bedfordshire, spearheaded by property developer David Kohler, plans to have up to 720 megawatts of computing capacity. It would amount to more than a fifth of the UK’s existing data centre demand.

The site was originally proposed as a film studio before Donald Trump proposed to impose a 100pc tariff on films made outside of the US.

“Ideally it would be done by the grid, [but] we need to be creative in how we get power,” Mr Kohler said.

Quest Park is the second major data centre project that will be able to go directly to ministers for permission under new rules designed to speed up development. The other, a £2bn site in Buckinghamshire, will also be gas-powered.

Mr Reed last week said Quest Park could apply for a development consent order to be treated as a project of national significance. An on-site gas-powered generating station will also require permission.

Ofgem has said that around 140 data centres in Britain are seeking grid connections. The regulator said last week it could consider cutting power to data centres when the grid is under strain.

Mr Kohler said renewables were not appropriate for data centres, which need a constant baseload of energy to operate.

Questpit, the project’s developer, says it will create up to 1,145 permanent jobs and will require investment of up to £9.3bn and take five years to build. The site would be built on a former clay pit which closed in 2008.

The Government was recently forced to revise forecasts for how much AI will contribute to Britain’s emissions.

Having originally said it would be less than 0.05pc of the total it now says the technology could account for 3.4pc of emissions over the next decade.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Why antique dealers risk becoming a thing of the past
data.description:
Tucked beneath a Waitrose car park in the historic market town of Brackley, Northamptonshire, Brackley Antique Cellar is a sprawling emporium of antique dealers selling their wares across 30,000 sq ft of space.

Viewers of the BBC’s Bargain Hunt will recognise the site, which opened in 2000 and has featured on the show several times.

More than 200 antique dealers call the centre home but for Jim Broomfield, the managing director, there are fears for the site’s future. Labour’s tax rises have left it struggling to balance the books.

Its business rates bill, a type of council tax paid by companies, soared by 20pc last year.

This was because its rateable value – which predicts how much annual rent the site could generate – saw a significant increase, leading to a higher rates bill.

Broomfield says he was shocked by the move.

“We came back from holiday and we had paperwork saying it was going to be £70,000 and then all of a sudden, we got one for £105,000,” he says. “I’m just thinking, what the bloody hell went on?”

The reassessment has translated into a rise in the centre’s annual business rates bill from about £14,500 to £17,500 – and the explanation attached to the move only deepened his frustration.

“On the bottom of one of them, it says the reason for the increase is ‘previously incorrectly rated’,” he says. “It’s been rated like that for more than 20 years. Now they’re suddenly saying it wasn’t rated properly.”

Broomfield is far from alone.

For antiques centres – often large retail spaces occupying older and unusual buildings – rising costs have become what Broomfield describes as “death by a thousand cuts”.

Many other dealers say they are also being squeezed by a combination of rising overheads and a business rates system that can leave large, property-intensive retailers facing substantial fixed costs regardless of how much they sell.

Labour’s business rates raid is to blame.

An outcry from pubs forced Rachel Reeves to introduce an emergency carve-out for the sector. The Chancellor also promised permanently lower business rates for retail, hospitality and leisure properties with rateable values below £500,000 from April 2026.

However, other key fixtures of Britain’s market towns – such as antique halls – have fallen through the cracks, putting their futures in jeopardy.

Business rates are also a potential landmine that Andy Burnham will have to navigate if and when he takes power in Downing Street.

The former Manchester mayor has argued that Labour has “got it wrong on small businesses” and proposed abolishing business rates for the smallest firms.

But many businesses still face sharply higher bills this year after pandemic-era relief was cut from 75pc to 40pc.

Brackley Antique Cellar relies heavily on footfall and rents paid by stallholders. Broomfield says the current rates system does not properly reflect the realities of businesses trading from unconventional premises.

“We’re underground, to start with – we’ve got no shop frontage or anything,” he says. “You couldn’t be in a worse position, really.

“It has gradually got worse and worse over the last three or four years. The electricity is just killing us. The cost of that now is about £2,000 a month. It just keeps biting into the profit and in the end, you’ve got no profit.”

Jane Killigrew runs Blaby Antiques Centre, near Leicester, housing around 60 dealers. She says business rates remain one of the biggest fixed costs facing physical retailers.

“It is quite a significant cost each month and I do feel that bricks-and-mortar shops may disappear,” she says.

Her business occupies a former British Legion club that has been converted into retail space.

“It isn’t the most attractive building at all,” she says. “It’s got old leaky roofs which we have to keep patching but it’s a good use of the building.”

Killigrew says antique centres offer something online sellers cannot.

“With antiques, you need to see them, really,” she says. “They’re all different and they’ve all got certain amounts of damage so I think there’s still a place for people to come in and actually feel and touch these things.

“People get quite a lot of enjoyment from coming here. It’s a day out.”

But she fears rising costs are steadily pushing dealers online.

“People with smaller shops have definitely gone to eBay, Etsy, that sort of thing,” she says.

For many antique dealers, reform cannot come quickly enough.

David Harvey, who runs an antiques shop in Oxfordshire, described parts of the current rates system as “nutty”.

Because both of his premises sit within the same council area, only one qualifies for small business rate relief. Had the second site been located just a few miles away in Gloucestershire, both would have qualified.

“Part and parcel of being successful in retailing is that there has to be a certain amount of feel-good factor,” he says. “People are wary.”

Rather than discussing antiques, customers are increasingly focused on household budgets.

“The topic of conversation is not, ‘Am I going to buy that wonderful William and Mary chest of drawers?’” he says. “It’s, ‘Do you realise I’ve just been to the supermarket and a week’s shopping cost me way over £100?’

“It’s those things that impact people.”

Back in Brackley, Broomfield says he was determined to keep going but admits there are limits to how much extra cost can be passed on to dealers.

“We can’t keep putting the rent up as a prop,” he says. “Last year was the first time we’d put the rent up for about four years.

“After more than a quarter of a century in business, he worries about what might happen if the economics no longer add up.

“We’ve been going since 2000 – we’re celebrating 26 years this year – and you just think, will it still be going in 10 years’ time? Who knows,” he says.

“You either just pack up or you try to think of different ways of trying to keep going.”

data.title: Asda sheds 7,500 roles as debt costs top £730m
data.description:
Asda has let nearly 7,500 staff go in the last year as the costs of servicing its debt mountain hit a record high of £730m.

The job losses are thought to have occurred across the struggling supermarket chain’s sprawling operations as under-pressure bosses sought to offset the mounting costs of private equity ownership.

The interest on Asda’s debt burden surged by a fifth to £730m in 2025 from £611m the previous year, meaning the grocer has spent close to £2.2bn servicing the loans taken out to finance its £7bn takeover by TDR Capital in 2021.

The ballooning debt bill pushed pre-tax losses to nearly £1bn despite a decision to cut prices in an attempt to win back growing numbers of customers turning to the German discount giants Aldi and Lidl.

With the financial burden of the deal becoming more acute and Asda bleeding market share, the company has embarked on a sweeping cost-cutting programme. Staff numbers fell by 7.5pc in 2025, according to the group’s annual report, to around 91,400 people.

Approximately 1,000 staff are expected to lose their jobs as part of an automation plan that will result in Asda relocating its George clothing online operation to a single site in Derby, operated by DHL.

Although 250 employees will be redeployed to the new warehouse, four times as many are expected to be replaced by robots. The job cuts were first revealed by The Telegraph in January, before being confirmed this month.

The move has prompted uproar from trade unions. Rachelle Wilkins, a national officer for the GMB union, said: “A thousand people losing their jobs to be replaced by robots sounds like something from a dystopian sci-fi movie, but the warehouse industry is becoming increasingly automated and it’s impossible to stand in the way of progress.”

She added: “[Our] members are now very worried this is the thin end of the wedge. What if Asda does the same thing with its online food shopping network? Thousands of jobs could be lost.”

Hundreds of staff who oversaw Asda’s botched IT upgrade were also let go, while hundreds more jobs went when the grocer sold the Leon fast-food chain back to John Vincent, its founder, last year.

The cost of the IT overhaul has continued to weigh heavily. In its latest results, Asda revealed £284m in losses tied to Project Future, a four-year programme to disentangle its IT networks from its former owner, Walmart. The total cost of the transition stands at £1.2bn.

The sale of Leon resulted in the closure of 23 of its 68 UK branches. In its final year under Asda’s ownership, sales at Leon fell almost 4pc to £62.5m and pre-tax losses totalled £8m.

Allan Leighton, the retail veteran, was brought in to revive Asda’s fortunes in 2024, having helped save it from the brink of bankruptcy in the late 1990s. However, Asda’s share of the UK grocery market has continued to slide. It has fallen from 14.3pc at the time of TDR’s buyout to 11.5pc, its lowest level in three decades.

It was the only major supermarket that saw its sales fall during Britain’s record May heatwave and the opening weeks of the World Cup, according to figures released last week. The most recent setback brings its market share closer to Aldi, which now commands 10.7pc of the market.

In the year before TDR swooped, Asda’s annual interest bill stood at £90m.

An Asda spokesman said: “The reduction in headcount last year was due to clear and specific reasons – primarily the completion of Project Future, our IT separation from Walmart and the disposal of Leon during the period. These accounted for a reduction of several thousand roles.”

The spokesman added: “The headcount reduction is not linked to Asda’s finance costs. Our debt is largely secured well into the next decade, providing stability and visibility on financing costs. Asda is also supported by a strong balance sheet and capital structure, with £1.3bn in cash, and £2.1bn of total liquidity at the year end.”

data.title: The hidden reality behind Britain’s homegrown nuclear age
data.description:
When Rolls-Royce was chosen to build the country’s first mini nuclear power plants, Labour ministers promised the scheme would help to “revive Britain’s industrial heartlands”.

Three small modular reactors (SMRs) are expected to be built in Anglesey, Wales, by the mid-2030s – proving the concept and triggering what could become a massive global industry.

But a year later, exactly just how British those SMRs will be is turning into a thorny subject.

Senior backbench MPs have claimed there were “serious questions” for Rolls-Royce to answer after the company began a process to buy “key nuclear island components” – including reactor pressure vessels – from either South Korea or the Czech Republic last month.

Nuclear plants are usually divided into two parts: a reactor “island” housing the most sensitive nuclear equipment and a separate site where the conventional turbine sits.

The companies in the running for the nuclear island contracts are Korea’s Doosan and CEZ, the Czech state energy giant that has its own nuclear programme and is an investor in Rolls-Royce SMR.

Insiders say the lack of a British bidder was inevitable, because only a handful of businesses in the world can make the specialist equipment and because of a need to begin construction within the next five years.

The contracts with Doosan and CEZ are for detailed design and pre-production only and there is still potential for Sheffield Forgemasters, the state-owned British manufacturer of forgings and castings, to play a role, they add.

But Sheffield’s potential involvement was previously trumpeted by Rolls-Royce and the fact it is now in doubt looks like a downgrade. UK Steel, the industry lobbying group, said the award of contracts overseas was “extremely disappointing”.

Rolls-Royce insists it is committed to “maximising localisation for its customers in the UK and the Czech Republic”.

There is still plenty of value to be captured in Britain. It is understood that the reactor island makes up about 20-25pc of the SMR’s production value. Moreover, the pressure vessel represents less than 0.5pc.

Yet the company’s decision has stirred fears that even more of the work to build what was ultimately a British design could end up going overseas.

Bosses previously suggested it was possible to build the reactor pressure vessels in the UK and even shortlisted locations for a £200m factory before dropping the idea in summer 2024.

The decision to procure them abroad is now expected to trigger greater political scrutiny of the rest of Rolls-Royce’s plan.

That matters because the company is not in the driving seat when it comes to building the first SMRs.

In the UK, it is technically a contractor – a technology supplier – to Great British Energy Nuclear (GBEN), the quango that is responsible for running the Government’s SMR programme.

A person familiar with the GBEN process says sourcing reactors from inexperienced British suppliers at the beginning would “add risk needlessly”, but that Rolls-Royce will be expected to help “build UK competence” over time.

“You want to make the best choice for the first unit, but to have in place deals to build up UK partners for the future,” they add.

“We need to support the UK supply chain properly to build competence and capacity.

“It’s something the Government needs to ensure happens. They need to be monitoring important national capabilities and ensuring they grow properly.”

SMRs are seen as a potential breakthrough because of their modular nature and lower overall cost compared to larger nuclear plants.

It’s envisaged that their modules will be mass produced in factories, before being transported by truck and then clicked together like Lego pieces on site.

Rolls-Royce has predicted there could be demand for 400 SMRs globally in the coming decades, making it a potentially huge industrial programme for the UK – just as its jet engines are today.

The company estimates this could create up to 40,000 domestic jobs and £50bn of economic benefits by 2050, with the Government separately saying the GBEN project alone could create up to 3,000 jobs.

In the UK, ministers have set a target for at least 70pc of the Anglesey SMRs to be made domestically.

What else could still be done in Britain?

Although a pressure vessel factory is off the table for the time being, there is still potential for domestic companies to make other parts for the reactor island as well as for the other parts of the power plant complex.

A major signal will be where the steam turbines for the SMRs are made. Currently, these are not produced in Britain.

Rolls-Royce has selected Siemens Energy as its strategic partner, but says it expects the German giant to leverage its UK manufacturing base to create domestic jobs and opportunities.

It isn’t clear what that means in practice yet. But it’s understood that executives are pushing for the reshoring of the capability, a move that would ensure Britain produced turbines for the SMRs that Rolls-Royce sells around the world.

On top of this, Rolls-Royce has brought in Dorset-based Curtiss-Wright to make reactor protection systems.

There is also a potential second major factory for SMRs that could be worth further hundreds of millions to the British economy.

This would be where various parts and components are assembled into repeatable, finished modules that can be transported to sites.

Rolls-Royce is currently experimenting with how to do this at the Sheffield Advanced Manufacturing Research Centre, as part of a £15m package of work it is doing with the academic institution.

It has also been on the lookout for potential places to set up the proper factory. These could include locations such as Sunderland, Teesside or Deeside, in Wales, that previously lost out on the reactor vessel work, as well as others such as Carlisle; Grimsby, Lincolnshire; and Catterick and Ferrybridge in North Yorkshire, according to reports.

Rolls-Royce confirmed in April that it was looking for a site of about 20 acres and expected to pick one later this year.

There is political pressure for the factory to be in Wales, close to where the first SMRs will be built.

But here as well, bosses are involving a foreign company. Aker Solutions, an Oslo-based oil and gas supply chain company, will be the “primary module partner”.

Rolls-Royce says this will “generate project management and engineering design work” at Aker’s offices in Chiswick, west London, and Aberdeen, while oil and gas supply chain companies that currently work on projects in the North Sea may also stand to benefit.

On top of this, there will be plenty of work on more prosaic aspects of the plant such as steel structures, electrical systems, tanks, valves, pumps, cables and buildings.

It means there is still plenty to play for if Britain wants to make SMRs the export success story politicians have promised.

But as with any such investment, other factors will also be at play: energy costs, taxes, employment laws, skills and infrastructure.

Lord Vallance, the minister for nuclear, said: “Great British Energy-Nuclear is making excellent progress against its ambition for 70pc of British built content across the small modular reactor fleet, and we fully support their work with Rolls Royce to unlock UK supply chain benefits providing thousands of jobs in our community.

“This is part of our commitment to delivering a golden age of nuclear and developing world leading-nuclear expertise and UK supply chains, supporting thousands of jobs in our community.”

data.title: Autobahn melts as heatwave moves east across Europe
data.description:
Germany’s autobahn highways are breaking apart as an extreme heatwave moves east across Europe.

With temperatures in Germany reaching a record high of 41C, parts of the autobahn network are crumbling under the heat.

Sections of at least three German autobahns have broken apart, in some cases forcing the closure of roads and diversions for tens of thousands of motorists.

They include sections of the A1 motorway near Hamburg, the A10 near Berlin and the A9 in the central state of Saxony-Anhalt.

The motorway sections have broken up because the concrete lacks enough room to expand under the heat.

The phenomenon, known as a “blow up”, tends to affect older and heavily used stretches of motorway that have been repeatedly repaired.

Germany also suffered rail chaos over the weekend as the heatwave caused cancellations and overheating inside rail carriages owing to broken air conditioning units.

In Leipzig, the tram network was also shut down on Monday after melting asphalt clogged the tramlines in several locations.

As the heatwave moves east, Poland has recorded some of its highest ever temperatures, with the mercury hitting 40.5C in the town of Słubice.

In Ukraine, the country’s energy grid was buckling under temperatures in excess of 36C on Monday, with authorities in the Rivne region introducing emergency power outages to ease pressure on the grid. The summer is the peak period for repairing the network, which Russian attacks have damaged.

In France, officials are mulling over whether to change summer working hours to adapt to extreme heat and climate change.

On the heels of the country’s unprecedented heatwave, Jean-Pierre Farandou, the French minister of labour, said France needed to rethink the way workplaces and work hours operate.

This was because episodes of extreme heat will probably become the norm, he said, and “return to our country year after year”.

“Shouldn’t the country itself adapt to the world of work in advance?” Mr Farandou told news media Franceinfo on Monday. “Indeed, are 9am to 6pm working hours still appropriate?”

Mr Farandou cited Spain as an example of a country that reorganises itself around scorching summers, and said he planned to visit Madrid on a fact-finding mission to understand how it copes with 40C weather.

“We must in fact consider that France, in the summer, becomes Spain,” he said.

For example, France could adopt Spain’s “jornada intensiva” or continuous working day model, which allows businesses to shift their working hours so that employees start and end their days early, he said.

Under that model, between June and September, workdays typically change to 8am to 3pm, without a lunch break.

The World Health Organization said it had recorded more than 1,300 excess deaths in Europe since the start of the temperature spike on June 21.

data.title: Great British Bake Off studio to be bought by ITV as part of £1.6bn deal
data.description:
ITV is on the cusp of buying the studio behind The Great British Bake Off as part of a £1.6bn deal to break up the company and sell its TV channels to Sky.

The media giant is poised to acquire Love Productions, which makes Bake Off, from Sky as part of a complex deal which will see it jettison its TV channels and streaming business.

The proposed deal opens the door for Bake Off to switch from its current home on Channel 4 to ITV, although it is not yet clear whether the show will make the move.

The hit baking show originally aired on the BBC in 2010 before Channel 4 scooped it up in 2016 in a dramatic coup for the youth-focused broadcaster.

Channel 4 struck a three-year deal with Love Productions for Bake Off in 2021. However, it has since moved to a rolling basis, meaning its future is uncertain beyond its 17th series this year.

Media mergers

The move comes after ITV unveiled plans last November to sell its broadcast arm to Sky, which is owned by US giant Comcast, in a £1.6bn deal, leaving it with a remaining studios business.

On Thursday, it emerged that as part of the deal, Sky plans to transfer ownership of Love Productions, which also makes Benefits Street and The Piano, to ITV.

Talks have been held up by knotty negotiations over how the broadcaster and streaming platform ITVX will be split off from the production arm.

In addition to making programmes for its own channels, ITV sells shows to other broadcasters and streamers, such as Disney+’s Rivals and Ludwig for the BBC.

Meanwhile, Sky will be bound by ITV’s public service broadcasting obligations, including a requirement to provide news and regional programming to the ITV Network.

ITV has been struggling with the move to streaming amid a declining TV advertising market.

However, its shares have jumped since discussions were first revealed in November and were up a further 3pc on Thursday, giving it a market value of around £3.1bn.

The deal could be announced in the next two weeks, according to Reuters, which first reported the talks. It would value Love Productions at between £80m and £120m.

Sky’s swoop on ITV’s broadcasting business marks efforts by the media group to build scale to compete with deep-pocketed US rivals such as Netflix and Disney, which are increasingly drawing audiences away from traditional TV.

The acquisition of Love Productions will also bolster ITV’s lucrative studio division, which has cashed in on high demand for programming.

ITV Studios reported a 5pc rise in revenues to £2.1bn last year.

However, the future of this side of the business remains uncertain as rivals combine to gain scale.

French group Banijay and The Traitors producer All3Media, which had both explored a takeover offer for ITV Studios, agreed to merge in an $8bn (£6.3bn) deal in March.

Meanwhile, in the US, Paramount has tabled a $111bn offer for rival studio Warner Bros Discovery in a move that marks a major reshaping of Hollywood.

British broadcasters have equally sought to collaborate more closely in an effort to increase their firepower against US rivals.

However, a merger between two of the biggest players in the market is likely to draw scrutiny from Ofcom and competition regulators.

ITV and Sky declined to comment.

data.title: US-blacklisted Chinese firm to install battery stations on UK roads
data.description:
Battery-swapping stations for electric lorries are to be rolled out on Britain’s roads by a Chinese company that has been blacklisted by the US government.

The devices made by Contemporary Amperex Technology Co Ltd (CATL) will allow truck drivers to replace depleted batteries in less than five minutes, rather than waiting for them to recharge.

On Monday, CATL announced it was partnering with Octopus Energy, the UK’s biggest energy supplier, to launch so-called battery mega hubs in the UK from next year.

However, the move threatens to trigger security scrutiny. CATL was named last year on the Pentagon’s roster of “entities identified as Chinese military companies” – a US government blacklist. CATL has denied any military links.

Octopus and CATL have set up a joint venture called Swaptopus. Together, they plan to build 30 battery hubs across Europe by 2035, capable of supporting 300,000 electric trucks.

Greg Jackson, the chief executive of Octopus Energy, said: “Electric trucks already beat diesel on running costs; the challenge is keeping them moving. Battery swapping changes that. Instead of waiting for hours, trucks can be back on the road in minutes.”

Speaking at his company’s “energy tech summit” on Monday, Mr Jackson said he had been to China to try out one of the CATL swapping stations at the weekend and that their adoption of the technology was being driven by “cold, hard economics”.

Octopus also previously partnered with the Chinese wind turbine manufacturer Ming Yang, arguing that it could provide cheaper equipment and lower energy prices.

However, Ming Yang was later blocked from supplying UK wind farms after the US government raised national security concerns with British officials.

Executives at Octopus criticised the decision, warning it threatened to cost the UK “decades of cheaper electricity”.

Octopus claims the battery-swapping system would make running an electric lorry cheaper than a diesel one.

“The beauty of battery swapping is we’ll have a massive stack of batteries at these stations that we can fill with the cheapest electricity at the cheapest times,” Mr Jackson said.

Electric models accounted for only 2.7pc of the heavy-truck market for vehicles weighing 12 tonnes or more at the end of last year, according to the International Council on Clean Transportation. In China, the figure is more than 30pc and growing, according to Mr Jackson.

There, CATL already operates more than 300 battery-swapping stations, each capable of serving thousands of lorries per day.

The practice is seen as a better solution than parking and recharging for hauliers, because time spent charging can disrupt delivery schedules.

In China, electric truck operators effectively pay to lease batteries rather than own them outright. When they arrive at a swap station, it is switched out for a fully charged one, and they can continue their journey immediately.

Oscar Luo, the executive president of CATL, said the technology would also put haulage company concerns about battery health and degradation at ease, as they would always be using the most modern technology.

CATL, the world’s biggest battery maker, was labelled last year a “military-civil fusion contributor to the Chinese defence industrial base” by the US department of defence, meaning its technological breakthroughs were allegedly being shared with the military to aid modernisation.

The blacklisting prevents the company from securing any US government contracts and signals to other businesses that it is considered a security threat.

It could also pave the way for harsher sanctions later, with the Pentagon saying it “reserves the right to take additional actions on these entities”.

CATL has previously insisted it has “never engaged in any military-related business or activities” and has filed an appeal against its blacklisting.

A CATL spokesman said: “We have never engaged in any military-related business or activities, and the designation is a mistake.

“It has no legal bearing on CATL’s commercial operations in Europe, including today’s partnership with Octopus Energy, which is a commercial joint venture focused on UK freight decarbonisation and clean energy infrastructure.

“We continue to engage with the [US government] to address the false designation.”

An Octopus Energy spokesman said: “Of course the US military should decide who supplies its equipment. But civilian energy and investment are a different matter. 

“At a time when energy security and growth matter most, decisions should be based on evidence and delivery, not speculation.

“CATL are building factories in the US and Spain, supplying batteries to companies like BMW and Rolls-Royce – Octopus isn’t breaking new ground here.”

data.title: Burnham faces Supreme Court threat over £140m Manchester skyscraper loans
data.description:
Andy Burnham faces the threat of a Supreme Court fight over £140m of Manchester skyscraper loans.

On Monday, the Court of Appeal rejected a legal challenge against two loans handed out by the Greater Manchester Combined Authority (GMCA) to Daren Whitaker, a property developer, They were awarded while Mr Burnham was in charge of the GMCA.

However, the Court of Appeal’s judgment confirmed several of the concerns of Aubrey Weis, a rival developer that brought the case, and Mr Weis said he was now considering launching a further appeal to the country’s highest court.

It means the saga of the loans threatens to follow Mr Burnham to No 10, despite the ruling in his favour.

Mr Weis was contesting a GMCA committee decision in March 2024 to grant loans of £70.8m and £69.2m to companies owned by Mr Whitaker. The GMCA initially approved £140m in loans, though the developer’s companies later took smaller sums totalling £120m.

During the hearings, lawyers acting for Mr Weis claimed the loans were made on “obviously an unlawful basis”.

Mr Weis alleged that the GMCA had granted subsidies to Renaker, Mr Whitaker’s property-development company, by issuing the loans at below-market interest rates.

His lawyers said this “advantageous treatment” was unfair because other developers did not receive comparable financial support. Mr Weis runs Combined Property Control, a rival property-development business in the North West.

Mr Weis alleged that Mr Burnham’s GMCA had used a “deeply flawed” analysis to justify the loans and accused the mayor’s authority of failing to carry out basic checks on Mr Whitaker.

The GMCA claimed that a “whole due diligence process” was carried out before the loans were allocated.

Three senior judges dismissed Mr Weis’s appeal on Monday, saying there was no error of law by the earlier court that reviewed the case.

However, the Court of Appeal’s judgment confirmed several of Mr Weis’s concerns.

The Court found that the GMCA should have regarded statutory guidance on subsidy control and failed to do so, and that its setting of interest rates was “wrong”.

It also suggested that there could be grounds for a judicial review of the wider process.

The judgment said: “It may well be that a public authority that fails to follow its own policies opens itself up to a judicial review challenge to the decision to give financial assistance to an enterprise.”

On the matter of due diligence, Lord Justice Zacaroli said that while Mr Whitaker had a successful track record of repaying earlier borrowing from the GMCA, this was “not the same as correctly assessing the creditworthiness of the borrowers”.

However, ruling on those issues was “outside the scope of this appeal” as the tribunal did not have the power to make this judgment, he added.

A spokesman for the Weis Group said: “These are extraordinary findings and raise serious questions that go well beyond the two loans at issue in this case.

“Nothing that the Court of Appeal has found has done anything to dispel the feeling that there was some form of cronyism at play here involving parallel viability assessments with differing forecasts on the profitability of these Renaker schemes.

“It is noteworthy that we raised these issues with Andy Burnham before these loans were approved but received no answers.”

The spokesman added: “We are considering the judgment carefully with our legal team, including whether to seek permission to judicially appeal the wider process or appeal to the Supreme Court. All options remain open.”

A GMCA spokesman said: “Today’s judgment from the Court of Appeal confirms the findings from last year’s tribunal, which rejected the false claims that developers received favourable loans and instead found that they were made on commercial terms.

“Despite repeated legal challenges and attempts in the media to undermine our processes and our reputation, the court has upheld the decision of the expert tribunal that scrutinised all our documents and stood behind the robust measures we put in place.”

Earlier this year, Mr Burnham defended the decision to hand loans to Mr Whitaker’s company and said that the GMCA had to support Renaker because “London-based lenders wouldn’t give us money”.

The loans were used to build high-rise residential towers in Manchester known as Trinity Islands and Contour.

Mr Weis previously failed to challenge the loans at the Competition Appeal Tribunal and was appealing against the tribunal’s decision to throw out his claim in July 2025.

Mr Burnham, who has said he wants to launch “the biggest programme of council house building since the Second World War”, has pointed to his record as Manchester mayor as proof of his credibility to lead the country.

Mr Weis was contacted for comment.

data.title: Burnham ‘caved in to unions’ in final act as mayor
data.description:
Andy Burnham brokered a deal before he left office as mayor of Greater Manchester that handed bus drivers a bumper pay rise.

Mr Burnham, who is expected to become prime minister next month, has been accused of “capitulating to union demands” over the deal.

The pay settlement for Manchester bus drivers, worth up to 56 per cent over three years, is one of the highest in modern times.

It was voted through earlier this week, when Sharon Graham, the Unite general secretary, praised it as a “huge win” and credited union threats of strike action for securing the deal.

The pay rise for around 600 Bee Network bus drivers came after months of negotiations between the union and their employer, Go North West. They will receive pay rises of between 23 and 56 per cent over three years.

Transport for Greater Manchester (TfGM) – which delivers transport services and policy on behalf of the mayor – brokered the deal, and is now in discussions with the operator about contributing financially to the pay settlement.

Richard Holden, the shadow transport secretary, said the pay deal should “serve as a warning”, adding that Mr Burnham would be “no different to any other tax-raising Labour leader”.

“He would raise your taxes to pay for more handouts to Labour’s union paymasters and in benefits to people who can work but don’t need to under Labour,” he said. “Labour are the party of welfare and the unions, as Burnham has already shown by his record in Manchester.”

One transport industry source said the move was a “capitulation to union demands” and raised concerns about the knock-on effect of such a large pay rise. “Given that wages are 50-70 per cent of underlying bus costs, this will increase network costs by 5-10 per cent at a stroke. If you give increases of this order of magnitude, there is the problem of the domino effect,” he said.

He explained this is where a pay rise leads to demands from other public sector workers for similar increases.

Mr Burnham has described the franchised Bee bus network as a “blueprint” for Britain.

His close ally Louise Haigh, the former transport secretary, held discussions with Mr Burnham about how the Bee network could “be replicated across the country”.

But figures show that the network, which Mr Burnham decided to bring under his control in 2021 with a franchising arrangement, is heavily reliant on taxpayer subsidies.

Last year, Manchester’s bus network was the most heavily taxpayer-subsidised in the country, aside from London.

The amount of subsidy Manchester’s buses have received – both from central and local government – has increased sharply since the Bee network was launched.

According to official figures, its net subsidy rose to a record high of £83.7m in 2025, more than double the amount it received just two years previously.

“Andy Burnham has spent years selling the Bee Network as his blueprint for Britain, but the books tell a different story,” Mr Holden said.

“Fares cover less than half the cost, reserves are being raided, and council tax payers across Greater Manchester are picking up the tab to keep Andy’s dream afloat.”

Launching the network in 2023, Mr Burnham said that Greater Manchester was the “first place in England to retake control of its buses after nearly 40 years of deregulation”. By January 2025, all bus services had been bought under his control under a franchise model.

The latest transport revenue report by Greater Manchester Combined Authority (GMCA) shows that gross costs exceed £700m, but passenger fares cover little more than 40 per cent of that.

The remaining 60 per cent, some £481.7m, is met entirely by public money. A mixture of central government grants, council tax precepts, levies on local authorities, raided capital budgets, and financial reserves are being used to balance the books.

Deal ‘addressed historic disparities’

A spokesman for Transport for Greater Manchester said: “We inherited a bus system that was shrinking and in decline – with the network almost 40 per cent smaller than in the 1970s. Now, we’re running our buses in a way that puts people and communities first.

“Passenger numbers are up by 24 per cent, bus frequencies and punctuality are hugely improved, and fares have been made more affordable, with hundreds of new buses ordered from UK manufacturers. A recent national, independent survey saw Greater Manchester’s bus network well ahead of the rest of country when it comes to passenger satisfaction with value for money.”

The spokesman pointed out that it is not surprising that Greater Manchester receives the second-largest subsidy after London, as it is the second-largest public transport network behind the capital, and added that the increase in levels of subsidy during 2024 and 2025 reflects the increase in services brought under local control through franchising.

Union sources said the pay deal with bus drivers addressed historic pay disparities between drivers predominantly in Bolton and their colleagues elsewhere in the network.

data.title: Metro Bank forced to refund man scammed seven times in one month
data.description:
Metro Bank has been ordered to refund a man who lost more than £31,000 to an investment scam seven times in the space of a month.

The man, who was scammed on seven separate occasions, including twice on the same day, was judged to be entitled to approximately £7,467 from Metro Bank of the £31,297.29 that he lost.

According to an Ombudsman report, between Feb 19 and March 13, 2025, the complainant – referred to as Mr M – made seven separate payments to a company that purported to be an investment firm but was actually a scammer.

During that period, Mr M handed the scammer remote access to his account, allowing them to take out a loan, open an international bank account in his name, and withdraw thousands of pounds.

When Mr M attempted to withdraw the “profits” he thought he had made, he was informed he would have to pay another fee of £13,000 to do so. It was at this point that he informed the Metro Bank branch from which the funds had originated, which then persuaded him that he had been scammed and no further payments should be made.

Metro Bank was subsequently hit with a complaint from Mr M, who argued that the bank should have prevented him from making the seven payments.

An investigator judged that Metro Bank should pay 50pc of the third payment – worth £4,940.28 of the £9,880.57 – and 25pc of the four payments that followed, but the high street bank did not agree with these conclusions.

Charlie Newton, writing on behalf of the Ombudsman, said Mr M “really should have questioned whether what he was doing was legitimate”.

However, Mr Newton argued that Metro Bank should have been monitoring accounts and any payments made to help prevent fraud and scams. The bank could have also taken additional steps or provided further warnings before processing a payment, he added.

While Mr M’s Metro Bank account was new and therefore lacked a payment history to provide a point of comparison for the seven payments, the Ombudsman ruled the bank should have intervened by the third payment “given that it represented a second large payment within a short period of time”.

The high street bank also should have provided a warning to Mr M about investment scams such as social media adverts that offer “too good to be true returns”, the Ombudsman’s report read.

Metro Bank was consequently ordered to refund 50pc of transaction three, and 25pc of transactions four, five, six and seven, along with 8pc annual interest from the date the transaction took place to the date of the settlement.

Metro Bank was contacted for comment.

data.title: Revealed: the best savings accounts on the market right now
data.description:
Regardless of why you’re saving – for a house, a holiday, your children’s future or as a buffer to cover emergencies – you’ll want to make sure your money is working as hard as possible.

To help you get the most out of your savings, Telegraph Money has launched a range of “best buy” tables showing the highest rates available across a variety of savings accounts. These tables update automatically every day, with data provided by Savings Data Limited.

It’s more important than ever to make sure your savings are receiving a competitive rate. Inflation in May measured 2.8pc, but this is expected to rise thanks to higher energy prices kicking in this month. You’ll want your savings to earn more than this to protect the value from being eroded by rising prices.

Our tables include the best cash Isa rates – whether you want an easy-access, notice, fixed-rate or variable rate account – and we also list the best Lifetime Isas if you’re saving for your first home or retirement.

If you have already maxed out your Isa, or want to save outside of the tax-free wrapper, we track the best easy-access accounts and interest-paying current accounts.

Those who prefer to save monthly should refer to the table of top-paying regular savings accounts, while savers happy to lock their money up for longer in pursuit of higher returns could benefit from our lists of the best fixed-rate bonds and notice accounts.

We also track the best Junior cash Isas and the best children’s savings accounts for parents putting money away for their family’s futures.

In this article, we will cover:

  • The best fixed-rate bonds
  • The best variable savings account rates
  • The best current account rates
  • The best Isa rates
  • The best children’s accounts
  • How to choose the right account for you

Why do rates matter?

Seeking out an account with a more competitive interest rate can make a huge difference.

For example, take a savings account paying a relatively low 0.75pc. A saver with £50,000 in this account will earn around £375 a year in interest. Moving the deposit to an account with a better rate can mean a difference of thousands. Take an account at the higher end offering 4.75pc – you’d yield £2,427 a year, an extra £2,052.

Cash loses value over time if it is left in an account that pays less than the inflation rate. At the current inflation rate of 2.8pc, a pot of £1,000 left in a zero-interest account would be worth just £972.76 after a year in real terms, and just £871.03 after five years. Our inflation calculator can show how your savings are affected.

What is a savings account?

Unlike a current account, which you’ll use to pay your bills and for everyday spending, a savings account is designed to be the place where you can stash your spare money away, where it will hopefully grow over time.

There are several different types of savings accounts – some offer variable rates, which can change at any time, while some require you to commit to locking your cash away for a fixed period.

In theory, the more inconvenient an account is, the more interest you should earn – but it’s not always the case.

The best fixed-rate bonds for 2026

Fixed-rate bonds tend to pay the highest rates on the market. Savers need to weigh up whether to take a fixed deal in order to get guaranteed interest for a longer term, or take a chance on a variable rate that could rise over the length of the term.

The best variable savings account rates for 2026

Variable-rate accounts tend to come with more flexibility than fixed-rate deals; often, you can make as many withdrawals as you like without taking a hit on interest, but some specific deals have their own restrictions.

In general, easy-access accounts let you take out money whenever you like; regular saver accounts require you to make deposits regularly; while notice accounts allow you to make withdrawals, but only if you give your provider a prescribed amount of notice.

By clicking through to each of our guides you can find out today’s best rates.

Easy-access savings account

Regular savings account

Notice savings account

The best current account rates for 2026

In some cases, current accounts will pay interest on the balance of money they hold, which can be useful if you don’t want the hassle of transferring money between multiple accounts.

The best Isa rates for 2026

Cash Isas work in a similar way to savings accounts, except that all interest you earn is tax-free – and you’re restricted to depositing up to £20,000 in each tax year. Income tax is payable on savings interest earned outside of an Isa, above an allowance of £1,000 for a basic-rate taxpayer and £500 in the higher-rate band.

Lifetime Isa

Savers who are under 40 and putting money away to buy a home or fund their retirement can open a Lifetime Isa. As well as the tax-free savings growth, the Government rewards savers with a bonus of 25pc, up to £1,000 a year.

The best children’s accounts

If you’re under the age of 18, or saving on behalf of a youngster, you can open a children’s account. Many account providers will let children add and withdraw money from the age of seven, so it can be a good way to teach financial skills early in life.

Children also get their own Isa allowance of £9,000 a year, which means an adult saving on behalf of a child in a Junior Isa can put this extra amount away on top of their own £20,000 allowance.

What is happening to savings rates?

Hundreds of savings accounts currently offer a rate that beats CPI inflation, which measured 2.8pc in May, according to the latest figures from the Office for National Statistics.

Savings rates have risen slightly in recent months, as the economic impacts of war in Iran reversed expectations for further Bank Rate cuts this year. However, should inflation rise as a result, savings rates may still be unable to keep up.

The most competitive rates can be quick to disappear – so if you spot an account that looks good, you may need to act fast.

Alice Haine, of online investment platform Bestinvest by Evelyn Partners, said: “For now, the best savings rates are continuing to outstrip inflation, giving savers who hunted out the top deals a healthy real return on their nest eggs. Locking in a top rate now before the best deals disappear could be a sensible strategy for those with cash languishing in an account delivering dismal returns to ensure their money is working as hard as possible.”

Use our calculator to see how much your savings account is earning you – and how much more interest you could get if you switched.

What is the difference between an Isa, bond, savings and a current account?

A current account is a transactional account that typically pays no interest but gives you a lot of flexibility in how often you access your money.

With a savings account, the bank pays you interest for keeping your money and therefore imposes some restrictions on how many withdrawals you can make.

A fixed-rate bond is a savings account with a fixed term, usually between one and five years. Until the duration of the bond is up, you cannot withdraw your funds, but in exchange for the commitment you will typically benefit from a higher rate.

The difference between an Isa and other types of savings account is that there is no tax charged on the interest. Everyone can save up to £20,000 a year tax-free in an Isa, or £9,000 for children.

How to choose the right account for you

The first thing to consider is whether you might need access to the funds in an emergency. A current account or an easy-access savings account will give you this flexibility.

However, you will get a higher rate if you are willing to lock away your funds for a set period (for example, in a bond). Generally, the longer the period, the higher the rate – however, this is not the case at the moment. Fixed-rate accounts with one and two-year terms are far higher than those with five-year rates.

The other thing to consider is whether you are at risk of exceeding your personal savings allowance. This is £1,000 for a basic-rate taxpayer and £500 for a higher-rate taxpayer. If you earn more than this in interest outside of an Isa, then you will have to pay income tax.

Use our savings tax interest calculator to work out whether you could breach your allowance, and if you should get an Isa.

data.title: BBC in row with journalists over 1pc pay rises
data.description:
The BBC has been plunged into a row over journalists’ pay as Matt Brittin, the new director-general, pushes ahead with sweeping job cuts.

The National Union of Journalists (NUJ) has rejected the BBC’s offer of a 1pc pay rise for staff, which it said fell well below inflation and would amount to a real-terms pay cut.

Union bosses said the offer failed to recognise pressures on staff, who they said are being asked to produce more with less while facing increasing job insecurity.

It comes as journalists brace for fresh cost-cutting that will lead to almost 2,000 employees – or 10pc of the workforce – losing their jobs.

The broadcaster has already announced that 550 roles will be cut in an effort to save £160m this year.

Radio 4’s The World Tonight and Midnight News are among the programmes that will be axed, while Amol Rajan will not be replaced when he leaves the Today programme.

Addressing staff last week, Mr Brittin described the cuts as “unavoidable”.

“Pretty much every organisation globally is dealing with an unprecedented level of uncertainty,” he said.

But Mr Brittin, a former senior executive at Google, faces a battle with unions over the pay deal.

Laura Davison, the NUJ general secretary, said: “This offer falls far short of what our members deserve and would leave many worse off in real terms.

“BBC journalists and media workers continue to deliver accurate, independent, locally relevant and universally accessible journalism in increasingly difficult circumstances.

“The BBC must invest in the talented and experienced staff who make its public service journalism possible.”

The union has agreed to independent conciliation talks through Acas, which it hopes will lead to an improved offer for its members.

The current offer comes after months of negotiations with the NUJ, alongside fellow unions Bectu and Unite.

Under the proposals, employees would receive a 1pc pay rise with a minimum £500 increase for all eligible staff.

However, bosses said there would be no extra performance-linked pay increases, with funds instead directed to the across-the-board pay award. Bosses on the BBC’s executive committee will see no pay rises this year.

In an email to staff, the BBC said: “We are currently facing difficult financial realities that require careful decision-making. The pay review represents a significant investment, and we must ensure any decisions are responsible and sustainable for the future.

“Despite several months of discussions, we have not been able to reach agreement with the joint unions on the offer set out below and the next steps will be to seek independent conciliation with Acas.”

Alongside job cuts, the BBC is also exploring ways to boost its commercial revenues as it seeks to plug a hole in its finances caused by falling income from the licence fee.

Jonathan Munro, the interim chief executive of BBC News, last week told staff the corporation’s TV news channel would prioritise international viewers in an effort to drum up more advertising revenues overseas.

data.title: Miliband’s energy bill levies holding back net zero
data.description:
Labour’s green levies on energy bills are holding back Britain’s net zero push, government advisers have warned.

Extra charges on energy bills to subsidise the growth of wind and solar plants championed by Ed Miliband have made power too expensive, the Climate Change Committee (CCC) said. 

It has caused consumers to turn away from the heat pumps and electric vehicles (EVs) needed to cut emissions, the committee added.

The CCC said electricity should be made cheaper by removing the levies, which would then help the UK hit its emission-reduction targets.

Nigel Topping, the CCC chairman, said: “The transition to clean electricity is not happening fast enough. Government support to accelerate the shift to electric vehicles and heat pumps is critical.

“At this moment of political uncertainty, any weakening of current positions risks slowing these transitions, undermining investment and the long-term consistency businesses need.”

The panel has recommended that the Government shift the cost of green levies away from bills – potentially meaning the cost would be added to general taxation.

Such a plan would be controversial. Green levies already add £11bn a year to power bills, but this is set to rise to £18bn by 2030, according to the Office for Budget Responsibility.

That increase will be driven largely by new wind farms and other renewables approved by ministers switching on and becoming eligible for subsidies.

The CCC report includes sharp criticisms of government policies, which are steered by Mr Miliband.

The report said: “While there have been some steps taken to reduce the costs of electricity, the Government’s plan for electrification lacks ambition … electric van sales remain off track and the growth in heat pump installations slowed significantly last year.”

The CCC’s other key recommendations included accelerating uptake of EVs, persuading more people to ditch gas boilers for heat pumps and boosting industrial electrification.

However, cutting the price of electricity is the key change needed to open the way for the rest, it said.

The Energy Secretary’s net zero push depends on households ditching gas in favour of low-carbon electricity.

However, green levies on energy bills have made UK power so expensive that electricity consumption has plummeted.

Household power consumption is down from 126 terawatt hours (TWh) in 2005 to 95 TWh last year.

The CCC said electricity is now four times as expensive as gas in terms of the energy each provides – the biggest cost gap in Europe – and said this is the key reason why UK heat pump uptake is so slow.

The Government has already moved some green levies – known officially as “policy costs” – from bills to tax.

The Renewables Obligation, a levy used to subsidise early wind farms, was adding about £7bn a year to bills, but Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, moved 75pc of the domestic component on to taxation at a cost of about £2.3bn at her last Budget.

Claire Coutinho, the shadow energy secretary, said the priority had to be on cutting the price of electricity rather than racing to net zero.

“It defies common sense to shut down our own industry in the name of climate change, just to import more goods from countries like China, which are still powered by coal,” she said.

Mike Hawes, the chief executive of the Society of Motor Manufacturers and Traders, said: “The dependence on electrification to deliver our climate goals is self-evident, but the pathway cannot be built on blind optimism.

“Reducing surface transport emissions is essential, but energy prices remain too high, production costs uncompetitive, charging infrastructure at best uneven and natural demand too low.”

A Department for Energy Security and Net Zero spokesman said: “The main driver of high energy costs are global gas prices, which have also pushed up electricity prices.

“We are driving forward the biggest public investment in home electrification in history through our £15bn warm homes plan, cutting bills, upgrading homes and lifting families out of fuel poverty.”

data.title: BP’s deputy chief exits in latest shock resignation
data.description:
The deputy chief executive of BP has quit her job in yet another shock resignation at the troubled oil major.

The FTSE 100 giant confirmed on Tuesday that Carol Howle had “elected to retire” just three months into her role and following a 26‑year career with the company.

Another senior figure, HR vice president Kerry Dryburgh, is also leaving after 16 years.

They are the latest executives to leave in what has been an extraordinary period of upheaval at BP, with new chief executive Meg O’Neill attempting to lead a major turnaround.

Albert Manifold was dismissed as chairman by the board last month less than a year into his role over “serious concerns” about his behaviour. Mr Manifold disputes the allegations.

In a statement, Ms O’Neill said: “I want to thank Carol and Kerry for their outstanding commitment and contribution to BP and wish them every success for the future.

“Carol led the company through a critical transitional phase as interim and then deputy chief executive. With her departure I have chosen not to replace the deputy chief executive role.

“We have significant actions under way to streamline the organisational model and we have a focused leadership team in place.”

The fresh upheaval at BP is likely to heighten scrutiny of the company’s governance.

The Telegraph revealed in May that investors were calling for changes on the board because of the handling of the situation surrounding Mr Manifold.

Dame Amanda Blanc, the senior independent director who is also the boss of insurer Aviva, is leading a search for his replacement.

The company also confirmed this month that William Lin, a BP lifer and head of the company’s gas and low-carbon business, was leaving.

Meanwhile, Ben Mathews, the company secretary, is on medical leave for stress, adding to the sense of turmoil at the top of the £74bn oil company.

As news of the leadership changes broke on Tuesday, shares in BP fell by 1pc.

BP said it had long been intended that Ms Howle’s role would only be temporary, saying that she now planned to travel.

However, the company made no mention of this when she was appointed. In fact, it announced that the company’s strategy and sustainability team would now report to her and that she would oversee “long-term strategy development beyond our 2027 targets”.

Ms O’Neill said at the time: “I’m looking forward to working closely with her to deliver a simpler, stronger, more valuable BP to maximise value for our shareholders.”

data.title: Labour threatens to intervene in Paramount’s Warner Bros takeover
data.description:
Lisa Nandy has threatened to intervene in Paramount’s takeover of Hollywood studio Warner Bros over the effect of the $110bn (£83bn) deal on media plurality.

The Culture Secretary said she was “minded to intervene” in the deal on public interest grounds, raising the prospect of an investigation by regulator Ofcom.

The deal would combine Paramount, which owns Channel 5 and CBS News as well as Nickelodeon and Comedy Central, with Warner’s slate of properties including CNN, TNT Sports and the Cartoon Network.

It would also combine two of Hollywood’s biggest film studios and the owners of the Paramount Plus and HBO Max streaming services.

Ms Nandy said her concerns related both to the market for news media – a reference to each company’s news channels – and to avoiding concentration of media ownership.

The Culture Secretary acknowledged that laws about plurality of media ownership did not apply to streaming or video-on-demand services.

However, she insisted this should still be considered given that streaming now dominates viewing habits, adding that she may push through laws to allow TV streaming mergers to be investigated if there are concerns about the combination of Paramount Plus and HBO Max.

Ms Nandy has written to the two companies giving them a week to respond, at which point she could issue a public interest intervention notice ordering Ofcom to examine the deal on media plurality grounds.

The matter is being separately investigated by the Competition and Markets Authority. The planned merger has triggered concerns that fewer films could be released, leading to less choice for audiences.

The competition watchdog is also likely to examine the effect on jobs after Paramount pledged to cut $4bn in costs. Warner Bros employs around 4,000 people in the UK.

Paramount has pledged to release 30 films per year in cinemas with a 45-day release window. It has also said the two studios will continue to license their films and TV shows to other streaming services and remain active buyers of shows from other producers.

If Ms Nandy chooses to use the full extent of her powers, it could mean the deal is not cleared by UK authorities until next year. Paramount has said it expects the deal to be completed by the end of September.

The Culture Secretary said: “I am conscious that the proposed acquisition is global in nature. In reaching this decision, my focus has been, and will remain, on the UK public interest and the range of services available to UK audiences.”

A Paramount spokesman said: “We are grateful for the continued constructive engagement with all interested government bodies and relevant authorities, including in the UK.

“We are confident that our proposed transaction does not pose any media plurality issues in the UK and remain confident in our stated transaction timeline.”

The US Department of Justice has already cleared the deal, although it still faces state opposition and clearance from the American communications regulator.

Paramount is led by David Ellison, a supporter of Donald Trump and the son of Larry Ellison, the tech billionaire.

Staff at CNN have raised concerns about the deal, as have Hollywood stars such as Jane Fonda and Ben Stiller.

data.title: Burnham is a closet neo-liberal with perfect economic timing
data.description:
Don’t tell the Parliamentary Labour Party, but Andy Burnham is not as Left wing as he pretends.

His “business-friendly socialism” is a populist tilt to appease residual Corbynistas, the Left’s grievance industry, and London enclaves of shrill wokery.

One thing he most assuredly has is the gift of economic timing, or at least good luck. But whether he is a genuine strategist or adds up to little more than a sweet-talking, wave-surfing, chaser of shifting political fashions is a legitimate question.

Britain touched bottom some time ago and is slowly adjusting to two decades of economic shocks. We are still at peak media catastrophism, but that is a lagging indicator.

Sir Keir Starmer can reasonably feel robbed. The rebound last year was blighted by Donald Trump’s tariff war; and blighted this year by the Iran war and the ensuing jump in global borrowing costs. Green shoots wilted in the UK housing market.

Instead it is Burnham who may snatch the dividend of a powerful economic renaissance that is gradually gathering force and which could deliver the highest growth rates for a generation.

The next secular cycle could plausibly make Britain the surprise star of the developed world by the late 2020s, so long as Burnham establishes market credibility with root-and-branch reform of the welfare system – not half-baked, Starmer-style tinkering to meet the fiscal rule.

He might do just that, according to his Mancunian economic mentor Jim O’Neill. “I think Andy smells correctly it is the right thing to do,” he said.

The party is sufficiently chastened by its poll collapse to obey orders next time. “Andy is the saviour, and so he’s got a lot of people who want to have their lives extended as MPs, who will basically back him on whatever he wants to do,” he told LBC Tonight with Andrew Marr.

Big caveat: this is a task for an iron man of unusual ruthlessness, and one might ask whether Burnham isn’t more of a thin-skinned snowflake craving adoration.

“You have to make it absolutely clear that you are putting your office at stake, that you are prepared to call new elections or to resign if your parliamentary group is not behind you,” said Göran Persson, the Social Democrat premier who turned around Sweden when his country was in far worse fiscal shape than the UK today.

“Any sign that you might waver will doom the programme. If there is the slightest dissension between your ministers, if you cannot keep your team together, you will find yourself on a very slippery slope,” he said.

Got that, Andy?

The supercharger for economic growth is artificial intelligence. The UK has the world’s third-largest AI industry, without the handicap of the EU’s suicidal AI Act. It scores well on diffusion.

Its high gearing toward services, life sciences and technology makes it a good fit for quantum gains in AI productivity, already visible in a 1.6pc rise in the past year on the new real-time information (RTI) measure.

The UK is now on the accelerating upward leg of the AI “J-Curve”, tracking America with slight lag. Optimists think it could lift UK growth rates to 2-3pc, drastically improving fiscal dynamics and the political mood.

Burnham’s Britain is sitting on the coiled springs of a multi-year credit boom. Households have deleveraged massively, paying down £600bn of mortgage debt. Companies have retrenched. Total private debt has dropped from 185pc to 133pc of GDP.

The scale overwhelms the modest rise in public debt. Today, the UK is one of the least indebted countries in the developed world.

The Bank for International Settlements says the combined private and public debt-to-GDP ratios are: Japan (354), France (324), Canada (315), Netherlands (302), China (300), US (251), Italy (232), UK (221) and Germany (196).

All it will take to unleash a fresh cycle of credit expansion is lower inflation and a revival of animal spirits.

Both are on the way because the UK energy crisis of the past four years is essentially over – for reasons that should console both sides of the culture war.

We are moving from a global gas shortage to a structural glut. Excess supply will soon be flooding the market. European gas futures for 2028 are trading at €26 (£22) per megawatt hour, around the long-term average in real terms.

The UK is over the hump in the switch to more efficient electrotech.

Battery storage is now so cheap that it makes wind and solar unbeatable. Few noticed that Elon Musk’s Tesla teamed up last week with NatPower to launch a £3bn-£4bn investment in battery projects in the UK and Italy.

Fewer yet noticed a week earlier that the Japanese are to invest £9bn in infrastructure linked to the UK’s roll-out of offshore wind, now so vast that the UK will be a net exporter of electricity within five years.

The benefits will accrue progressively. If the Tories and Reform think energy costs will be a millstone around Burnham’s neck three years hence, they are in for a rude awakening.

Will Burnham blow it by lurching Left? I strongly doubt it. What he calls Manchesterism is muscular use of the state to sharpen markets, break cartels and drive economic growth. It has nothing in common with ideological anti-capitalism.

“Andy is not going to nationalise things for the sake of it,” said Prof Michael Jacobs, a former Downing Street adviser to Gordon Brown and now one of Burnham’s economic whisperers.

“What he will do is go after monopolies that have no real competition and are making profits they don’t deserve. The energy distribution networks are ripe for that,” he said.

Burnham will give the National Wealth Fund borrowing powers along the lines of Germany’s KfW, its €540bn investment bank, opening the way for a blitz of dirigiste investment, off-books and outside the fiscal rule. Will the bond vigilantes swallow it? Yes, so long as they have claims on the underlying assets.

Arya Chanda argues on the conservative platform Centre Write that Burnham’s Manchesterism has echoes of The Good Society, the seminal defence of managed markets published by Walter Lippmann in 1937.

Lippmann was a “conservative liberal” horrified by the collapse of reasoned civility during the interwar era, so like our own era, if you swap radio propaganda for today’s nihilistic social media.

Laissez-faire capitalism had failed in the Great Depression. Lippmann sought to head off the alluring false solutions of the Fascist Right and the Communist Left with regulated market liberalism and a respect for social stability.

Chanda says the more he has researched Burnham’s time as mayor – including his New York-style back-to-basics police campaign against anti-social behaviour – the more he has concluded that the whole Left-wing pitch is a feint.

“Manchesterism has successfully dressed neoliberalism in the rhetoric of socialist solidarity and Mancunian pride. If Burnham does to the country what he has done in Manchester – perhaps with a Chancellor Streeting alongside him – ‘Singapore-on-Thames’ stands the best chance it has ever had of becoming a reality,” he wrote.

Up to a point, Lord Copper. I doubt that Burnham deserves much of the credit for turning Greater Manchester into a flourishing hub of the knowledge economy, with an average growth rate of 3.1pc a year since 2015.

But at least he did not stand in the way. If he does not stand in the way of the coming national recovery, that alone will be something to celebrate. Perhaps, mirabile dictu, he might even do some good.
