data.title: How the heatwave melted Britain’s power network
data.description:
It was supposed to be a quiet time of year for the people who oversee Britain’s energy network.

Instead, a surprise heatwave, geopolitical turmoil and sheer bad luck have exposed the cracks in the country’s power system.

Before Friday evening, grid chiefs issued their second electricity margin notice of the week – an alert that effectively signals things are becoming a little too close for comfort.

The notice urges generators to squeeze every last bit of capacity they can out of their plants.

It followed a similar warning on Tuesday evening, as sweltering temperatures prompted households and businesses to turn on fans and air conditioners to keep cool.

To bolster supplies, the National Energy System Operator (Neso) was eventually forced to call upon gas-fired power stations and European neighbours – at significant cost.

That’s despite less than a month ago from Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, boasted that the country was producing “more clean power than ever before”.

He has repeatedly argued that shifting away from oil and gas would get Britain off the “roller coaster” of fossil fuel prices that drives up costs.

So what went wrong?

Normally, the summer months are seen as quiet because warm weather means people use less electricity and gas for heating.

But this week, grid operators have been grappling with a cocktail of misfortunes – not least the exceptionally hot weather.

Extreme heat batters Britain’s electricity system from two directions because it pushes up demand while also squeezing supply, explains Tom Edwards, of energy consultancy Cornwall Insight.

What also makes things harder are the consumption patterns. Electricity usage usually peaks reliably at breakfast time and then again in the evenings as people get home from work.

But when it is this hot, households and businesses turn on fans and air conditioners to keep cool throughout the day. Some also keep them on through the evening and night, when there’s less solar power being generated.

At the same time, high temperatures make all kinds of equipment throughout the network less efficient: from gas plants to electricity transmission lines and even solar panels.

“We’ve actually seen a lot of gas plants issue unavailability notices, saying they can’t do full output,” explains Edwards. “It’s just too hot for them to run at full efficiency.”

Similarly – and perhaps more ironically – solar farms also become less efficient. A typical panel’s efficiency drops by 0.3pc to 0.5pc for every degree that the temperature rises above 25C.

Transmission lines are not immune either. These cables – which act as high-voltage motorways, moving electricity up and down the country – heat up when in use. High ambient temperatures reduce the amount of power they can safely carry, forcing grid controllers to dial it down.

The final bit of bad luck this week is nuclear outages. Power station bosses usually take advantage of the summer months to refuel reactors, with Heysham in Britain and French plants across the Channel currently offline.

All these things combine to squeeze the system further, as plants produce less power and the amount that can be moved around also shrinks.

Because Britain is in the middle of a major energy transition – shifting its power system from one dominated by large, centrally located coal and gas plants to one that is highly diffuse and based on wind and solar power – there is also less spare generation that can be tapped because the necessary grid upgrades haven’t been completed yet.

For example, grid chiefs were switching off wind farms on Tuesday and Wednesday because the network was too congested to accept their power.

“Everybody is getting pushed to the limit,” says Tony Jordan, the co-founder of Auxilione, an energy consultancy. “So you have to call on all the generators you can.”

This is precisely what Neso did on Wednesday and Friday. In what’s known as an electricity margin notice, the grid operator urged plant owners to “make any additional generation capacity they may have available”.

It also warned they could be called upon to operate outside their normal usage limits, in an emergency, to bolster supplies.

Neso has insisted the notice does not signal blackouts are imminent and instead aims to signal that “we would like a greater safety cushion between power demand and available supply”.

Yet things are clearly tighter than normal. This week marked the first time that such notices had been issued during the summer months. They are usually reserved for the winter – and even then, they tend to be used rarely.

Intervening to boost generation isn’t cheap either.

Gas prices were already higher than usual after the US-Iran war plunged the Middle East into chaos. In the UK this week, for example, gas has been selling for about £1 per therm compared with about 78 pence per therm a year ago.

That means fuel costs for gas plants were already higher and because gas generation is being reduced under Mr Miliband’s clean power mission, plant owners now demand higher payments to switch on.

“That’s because they’ve effectively been told they don’t have a long-term future because renewables are going to take over,” says Jordan. “So there is less investment in new plants and the existing ones want to make their money when they have the chance.”

As a result, Neso was forced to pay top dollar for power from gas plants and from interconnectors with European countries.

For electricity imports, it paid about £1,400 per megawatt-hour on Wednesday – roughly 20 times the average power price in June last year.

Heatwaves are not going away – the Met Office has said temperatures could peak above 45C by the middle of the century – so how can Britain’s energy system adapt?

One way is to build more flexible energy storage.

Ofgem said on Friday that it had approved 16 new such schemes, including a pumped hydro dam in Scotland and a compressed air facility in the North East.

These will be able to provide power at short notice, helping to lessen reliance on importing electricity from abroad.

However, to an extent, the particular set of circumstances this week is not something grid operators should be expected to plan for, says Jordan.

“You could build extra capacity,” he says. “But it would have to be paid for. And you’ve got to ask: what are the chances?

“If you asked people to pay £50 or so extra each year to be prepared for something that might happen for a few days, I think most would probably say no.”

However, Edwards of Cornwall Insight believes it might be time for Britain to start planning properly for higher summer demand.

“We haven’t historically planned around thinking about summer loads from cooling demand, and that’s going to need to change as we factor it into our lives,” he says.

“How do we keep cool if there’s going to be Mediterranean temperatures regularly in the UK and we need to cool our buildings and homes?

“The system we’ve got is built for a UK climate that might not always be the case in the future, so we need to think about how to have the same level of reliability in summer as we do in the winter.”

data.title: Best fixed-rate bonds for July: Today’s latest rates
data.description:
Fixed-rate bonds are savings accounts that lock away your funds for a set period of time, paying a guaranteed rate of interest throughout this term.

They can be a shrewd move in times when savings rates are on the way down – as they are now – since the rate you fix at will be unaffected until the term ends.

However, you’ll need to be sure you won’t need the cash in the meantime. Many accounts simply won’t let you access your savings until the term is over, while those that do allow early withdrawals will usually deduct your interest as a penalty.

This guide will cover:

  • How we determine the best rates
  • The best fixed-rate bonds in July
  • Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a fixed-rate bond
  • Premium Bonds vs fixed-rate bonds
  • Fixed-rate bond FAQs

What are fixed-rate bonds?

Fixed-rate bonds may also be referred to as fixed-rate savings accounts, or even fixed-term deposits – depending on the provider. In essence, they are savings accounts that pay a set amount of interest, and last for a set amount of time.

They commonly span from nine months to five years and, in theory, the longer the term you commit to, the higher the interest rate you receive.

However, this is not always the case – during times when rates are expected to fall over the longer term, savings providers will increase rates for shorter-term bonds in a bid to avoid paying over the odds in future.

You will often need to pay in a lump sum at the start of the term, rather than making regular deposits, so these accounts tend to suit those with larger amounts to save.

The interest can be paid annually, quarterly or monthly, depending on your provider. In some cases, interest may only be paid at the end of the term – if you have a large amount saved over a number of years, receiving all the interest in one go could mean you will exceed your personal savings allowance and have to pay tax on your returns.

How we determine the best rates

The Best Buy tables show the best savings rates widely available in the market. This means certain accounts are excluded, including those that require minimum deposits of more than £25,000 or are available only to local or existing customers.

The data in these tables is provided by Savings Data Limited and is compiled using automated tracker tools and updates from savings providers. Savings Data Limited then manually checks the information and enters it into a database that feeds the live tables, which update every day.

The savings accounts shown are protected by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS). The information in this article is intended for information purposes and should not be taken as endorsement or advice.

The best fixed-rate bonds in July

Use the table below to explore your options for a fixed-rate deal.

For the top rates where you can access your cash whenever you like, see our guide to the best easy access accounts and the best current accounts.

Alternatively, our guide to the best notice accounts can show you the top accounts that require you to wait a little while before your money is released.

While you’re saving, you might be keen to shield your money from a potential tax bill – our guide to the best cash Isas can help you find a tax-efficient account.

Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a fixed-rate bond

Rachel Springall, of the analyst Moneyfactscompare.co.uk, said: “Picking the right fixed bond term entirely depends on someone’s circumstances and how soon they wish to access their pot. There are bonds with fixed terms of just a few months, so these might be useful for someone who has a big lump sum but may need to use it before the year is over, such as a deposit for a home.

“There are fixed bonds available that pay interest monthly, which may be suited to customers looking to supplement their income.”

She also warned savers to consider the personal savings allowance (PSA) when it came to accounts that were not Isas, as there could be tax to pay on the interest you earn.

Fixed-rate bonds are worth considering by anyone who wants certainty in their returns – this can be particularly handy in times of volatility.

While it may be tempting to stick to the high street brands you’re familiar with, it’s not always the route to the best accounts.

“As it stands, some of the top rates available are from challenger banks, which should not be overlooked,” Ms Springall added.

Premium Bonds vs fixed-rate bonds

While Premium Bonds and fixed-rate bonds might sound similar, they are very different:

  • Premium Bonds are only provided by National Savings & Investments (NS&I), while fixed-rate bonds are offered by a number of savings providers.
  • Premium Bonds aren’t savings accounts; you don’t get paid interest. Instead, each £1 bond is entered into a monthly prize draw, where you could win between £25-£1m. Fixed-rate bonds pay a set amount of interest for the duration of the term.
  • Premium Bonds prizes are paid tax-free; savings interest from fixed-rate bonds is taxable unless it’s a fixed-rate cash Isa.

Fixed-rate bond FAQS

You might have to pay tax on the interest your fixed-rate bond earns if it exceeds your tax-free allowance, unlike the money stored in an Isa.

This allowance depends on your tax bracket; basic-rate taxpayers can earn £1,000 in interest tax-free but the limit is £500 for higher-rate taxpayers and no personal savings allowance for additional-rate taxpayers.

Those with incomes of less than £17,570 may be able to benefit from the additional starting rate for savings, which gives those on lower incomes up to £5,000 as an extra tax-free savings allowance.

If you usually pay tax by self-assessment, you will be asked to detail the amount of savings interest you have earned over the tax year. If you pay tax via Pay As You Earn (PAYE), any tax on savings interest is usually calculated automatically and your tax code may be adjusted if you have tax to pay.

You must submit a tax return if you earn more than £10,000 in savings interest – regardless of whether or not you usually file one.

This will depend on what rates are available at the time you are looking for a bond, as well as whether the account suits your circumstances.

The best way to determine how “good” an interest rate is, is to see how it compares with other similar types of bond on the market. If it can easily be beaten by other providers, it might be worth looking elsewhere.

Our table of the best fixed-rate bonds above shows some of the top deals currently available.

NS&I’s Premium Bonds are not fixed-rate bonds – and they work slightly differently to other types of savings accounts.

They do not pay any interest, instead every £1 you have invested is assigned its own Premium Bonds number, which is entered into a monthly prize draw, whether you could win between £25 and £1m. Our guide to Premium Bonds explains how they work in more detail.

NS&I does offer fixed-rate bonds, including Guaranteed Growth Bonds and Guaranteed Income Bonds – see our guide to NS&I savings accounts to find out more.

data.title: Virgin Media O2’s £2bn bid to rival BT faces competition investigation
data.description:
The competition watchdog has launched an in-depth investigation into Virgin Media O2’s £2bn bid to take on BT in the broadband market.

In a complex deal, VMO2’s parent companies – Liberty Global and Telefónica – plan to take control of Netomnia, which is the second-largest “alt-net” in the UK, covering around three million homes.

The transaction has been struck through the companies’ joint venture Nexfibre, which is also backed by the French private equity firm InfraVia.

The move has fired the starting gun on an expected wave of consolidation in Britain’s fragmented “alt-net” market and would strengthen VMO2’s position as the main challenger to incumbent BT by creating a combined full-fibre network reaching roughly eight million homes.

The Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) has said it will fast-track an investigation into the takeover to a second, more granular phase, after VMO2 bosses warned any delay would create uncertainty and threatened to pull some of their investment.

The CMA’s decision to examine it follows vocal opposition from CityFibre, the UK’s third-largest broadband provider, which has warned the deal would “significantly reduce competition” and re-establish a “cosy duopoly” of BT and VMO2.

Simon Holden, the CityFibre chief executive, said on Wednesday that the deal would “remove a successful challenger and reduce choice for consumers”.

He added: “With 80pc overlap between the two networks, the deal raises significant questions and the CMA is right to take an in-depth look at its impact on UK digital infrastructure and the competition that policymakers, regulators and the alt-nets are working so hard to establish.”

VMO2’s owners have hit back at the claims, with Mike Fries, the Liberty Global boss, dismissing them as “sour grapes”.

Speaking at a conference in London earlier this year, Mr Fries threatened to scale back investment if regulators gave in to criticism and blocked the deal, saying any such move would undermine Labour’s pledge to kickstart economic growth.

Both Liberty Global and Telefónica have also warned that a lengthy regulatory review risked derailing the takeover as the funding could fall through.

The fast-tracking means the CMA investigation will pass straight to the detailed phase two without the need for an initial review. A deadline has been set for Dec 15.

The investigation will be led by a panel of independent experts, while the watchdog said it would also work closely with media regulator Ofcom.

Rajiv Datta, the chief executive of Nexfibre, said: “We requested a fast-track to phase two to get to the right answer faster, ensuring due process while recognising urgency. We look forward to continuing our constructive engagement with the CMA.

“This deal would create the scaled, sustainable alternative to the BT Openreach monopoly, something the UK market still lacks. Every day of delay reinforces the incumbent’s advantage and slows the progress of genuine competition.”

A CMA spokesman said: “This decision is not a finding of any competition issues and it allows the independent inquiry group leading this investigation to make a decision faster than under the usual timetable.”

VMO2’s takeover of Netomnia comes at a moment of turmoil for Britain’s alt-net challengers, which have built their own full-fibre broadband networks to challenge BT’s dominance.

However, rising interest rates and slower-than-expected uptake have pushed many to the brink of collapse, with a sweeping wave of consolidation now expected across the market.

Separately, Getty Images pulled out of its $3.7bn (£2.8bn) merger with rival Shutterstock after the CMA intervened in the deal.

The watchdog said Shutterstock would have to sell its editorial business to secure approval, citing concerns that combining the two photo agencies would push up prices for UK media outlets.

Getty’s board has now voted to terminate the deal after the extended deadline of July 6, according to a filing.

data.title: Halifax to disappear from high street
data.description:
Halifax is set to disappear from British high streets.

The building society brand is being scrapped after 173 years and all customer accounts will be rebranded to Lloyds over time.

Lloyds Banking Group, which has owned Halifax since 2009, said there was nothing that customers needed to do and they would be contacted about the changes.

The banking giant confirmed the move after reports in May said it was considering phasing out Halifax as a standalone brand.

It is understood that the decision was rooted in efforts to simplify the group’s portfolio, with the distinction between Halifax and Lloyds seen as becoming less prominent in recent years.

Halifax was founded in West Yorkshire in 1853 and granted its first mortgage, before growing to become one of the UK’s largest building societies.

Jas Singh, the chief executive of consumer relationships at Lloyds Banking Group, said: “As Halifax changes to Lloyds, our Halifax customers will keep everything they know and love today – the same fantastic app design, the same friendly faces in our branches – even the same sort code and account number.

“But as Lloyds customers, they’ll get the best innovation and experiences we offer.”

No job cuts announced

Lloyds said it remained committed to the town of Halifax and the wider Yorkshire and Humber region, where some 3,000 staff are based at its Trinity Road office.

No job cuts are being announced as part of the shake-up, and Halifax branches will either be rebranded to Lloyds or shifted to a Lloyds branch nearby throughout 2027.

Earlier this year, Lloyds announced 95 more bank branch closures, in addition to 49 already earmarked to shut this year. It has closed 1,470 sites in the past decade, according to consumer group Which?.

Under the latest round of closures, 53 Lloyds, 31 Halifax and 11 Bank of Scotland sites will shut between May this year and March 2027.

This is in addition to an ongoing closure programme that will shut 49 sites by October.

The Financial Conduct Authority states that banks must assess customer impact when closing branches, and provide reasonable alternatives for cash access.

More than 6,300 branches have closed in the past decade. Currently, 40 out of the UK’s 650 constituencies do not have any branches, while 88 are down to their last one, according to figures from Which?.

data.title: Forget Italy – this Cumbrian couple are changing the UK pasta market
data.description:
When 18-year-old Imogen Royall met her future husband Matt Kenyon in a grimy nightclub in 2010, she was locked in a deep struggle in her relationship with food – to the point that she could hardly bear to eat in front of him. So it’s remarkable that the couple now run an award-winning pasta company whose British-made casarecce and conchigliette are stocked in more than 600 delis, farm shops and supermarkets across the country.

Growing up, Royall’s family bounced from Oxford to London to Sheffield before settling in the Lake District, and she describes their household as “traumatic”. From as young as six she remembers her control-based approach to food creeping in. “I became fixated and obsessed with monitoring my food, or not eating,” says Royall.

As she grew up, the glorification of stick-thin bodies in programmes such as America’s Next Top Model didn’t help. Nor did “the covers of those awful magazines at the time, like Sugar”, Royall says. “Being a teenager in the 2000s, carbs were demons.”

It was her desire to appear “normal” when starting to date Matt, a 21-year-old builder from Windermere, that forced Royall to confront her fears.

“At some point, I just wanted to be with my boyfriend and not have a panic attack halfway through a meal,” she says now. “I wanted to be able to enjoy things and connect with somebody.” 

Royall recalls a specific dinner in their early courtship that changed everything. They were at the Wheelhouse Crab and Oyster Bar in Falmouth: “We were eating loads of seafood and had to destroy this crab. That was the scariest food for me. But we had bibs on, and we were making a mess and just enjoying it. It was a massive turning point – I found joy in food.”

Recounting her story from her sun-streamed office in their Cumbria home, Royall, 33, says her younger self could never have imagined she’d start a carbs-based company with her now-husband – let alone one stocked next to the Ottolenghi sauces in the temple of middle-class nourishment that is Waitrose.

Established in 2022, Northern Pasta Co started as a farmer’s market stall in Kendal, where the couple sold their 100 per cent British-grown spelt pasta. It soon became a staple at independent grocers, finally earning a listing at Waitrose in April. It is one of a crop of prospering homegrown pasta brands, among them Yorkshire Pasta and Devon’s Fresh Flour Company, trying to provide Britons with one of their best-loved foods, the lion’s share of which is imported from Italy.

The idea struck when Royall was working in a bakery during the Covid-19 pandemic. “I learnt that we grow ancient grains in the UK; they were in the sourdough we sold. But pasta is my favourite carb, and I’d always seen it on the shelves looking bright yellow and really unappealing,” she recalls. “So I thought, ‘why can’t we do something about that? Why can’t our British farmers show up in another area of the supermarket?’”

“It’s such a simple product, just flour and water,” says Kenyon, 35, calling in from the factory, a 20-minute drive from their front door. With Kenyon’s extended family living in Italy, the couple married in Tuscany in 2018, serving fresh pasta at their nuptials. But it wasn’t until they were back in their kitchen during Covid – and inspired after briefly living on the island of Hvar in Croatia, where they saw the residents eating seasonally and locally – that they decided to make pasta together.

Royall and Kenyon have always tried to eat as sustainably as possible, so prioritising regenerative farming came naturally. Northern Pasta Co’s spelt is grown by two Cotswolds-based millers who are “hugely passionate” about soil health, says Royall. “A customer has the power to impact the UK landscape by buying a bag of pasta. That’s so exciting,” she adds.

Spelt, an ancient wheat, has been grown in Britain since the Bronze Age, and many find it easier to digest than modern wheat. For a tummy-troubled nation obsessed with probiotics, the idea that eating pasta needn’t be naughty – or painful – is welcome. Plus, Royall and Kenyon argue that their pasta is beautifully nutty and flavoursome, while the shapes, including casarecce, gigli and radiatori, are extruded through bronze dies, a 17th-century Neapolitan method that provides more texture, so it can better grip on to sauce.

“We’ve become so detached from joy in our food with all these smart watches and devices,” says Royall, who is clearly no longer afraid of tucking into a bowl of carbs. “There’s a lot of ‘shoulds’ and ‘shouldn’ts’. We wanted to tap into the joy of food. The Italians do it so well.”

Speaking of our continental neighbours – has this thoroughly British company received any heat for producing an Italian staple? “I don’t think there’s a rivalry,” says Kenyon. “We’ve never tried to pit ourselves against Italian pasta. But my auntie takes it back to Italy when she visits, so that’s saying something”. High praise also came in the form of a Great Taste label, but the fortune-flipping moment came in 2023 when Royall and Kenyon were featured on James Martin’s Saturday Morning.

“It was pretty scary,” recalls Royall. “We had 100 bags of pasta, went on the telly, then had 6,000 orders over a weekend. I’d never seen anything like it.” Kenyon adds: “Our capacity was 100 bags per day. Filling those orders was insanity. The pasta dryer takes 16 hours, and when that time was up, I’d go back and make more – even if it was 2am.”

Kenyon now oversees a team of seven in their new Lancashire factory, where they churn out 1,500 bags each day. “An insanely hard worker,” Royall says, addressing her husband. “You started working as a builder at 16 and figured out how to build a house. Even if you don’t know how to do something, by the end of the day, you will have done it.”

Their respective strengths play out in the running of the business. “I’m an emotional person, so I do the marketing,” Royall says, referencing the recipes and videos she posts for their 20,000-strong Instagram audience, as well as the cookbook she released earlier this spring. Wisely, they don’t shy away from being the face of the brand. Both in their 30s, with what seems like an effortlessly healthy glow, their aspirational yet accessible image has helped grow a following that’s invested in their entrepreneurial journey.

But most couples wouldn’t dream of going into business together, and navigating overlapping domestic and professional spheres can’t have been simple. “When we started off, we did everything together. It wasn’t great. You’re just watching over each other with a magnifying glass, and [thinking] ‘they’re not quite doing it right’,” laughs Royall. “To be completely transparent, I think we made pasta [for the business] together twice,” adds Kenyon.

Remembering those first dinners together when Royall struggled to eat, Kenyon addresses her directly. “How far you’ve come with it is incredible.” With their kitchen creation now available on shelves across the country, their story is incredible indeed.

Northern Pasta Co Pasta for the People by Imogen Royall is out now (Murdoch Books, £20)

data.title: Japan’s stock market is the ultimate picks and shovels play
data.description:
Ask any investor where the best stock market returns have been achieved in recent years and they will almost certainly mention the US. Most likely they will talk about tech stocks and the “magnificent seven”. The more engaged might give a nod to India. More recently they are likely to have noticed the fireworks in Korea.

The market they are very unlikely to place at the top of the leaderboard is Japan. Forty years on from the Japanese stock market and property boom, Asia’s most developed market still struggles to shake off its reputation as a deflationary demographic disaster.

It is the market I have always struggled to interest our investors in. Which is a shame. Because in the past decade and a half, it has been a fantastic performer.

If you had invested £100 in the FTSE 100 in the summer of 2012, you would have around £180 today. Not terrible, but an unexciting annualised return. By contrast, the same £100 invested in the US during that 14-year period would be worth £531, nearly three times as much.

No surprise there. The post-financial crisis recovery years were dominated by big, successful US tech stocks. What might surprise you, however, is that £100 invested in Tokyo over the same period is today worth £532. By the narrowest of margins, Japan has pipped the US to the post.

Japan has been the best-performing slice of my pension, un-flashily delivering the goods year after year. I think it will continue to do so. For a variety of reasons, the stars are well aligned for Japan. And I am no longer in a small minority of eccentric Japanophiles. The broader investment community is cottoning on.

Part of the case for Japan has been well rehearsed. The escape from 30 years of deflation – in which GDP barely grew, wages stagnated, and companies sat on their cash in the absence of anything better to do with it – is now firmly in the rear-view mirror.

Now the country is benefiting from a healthy level of inflation at around the Bank of Japan’s target, positive wage growth, and consequently improving domestic demand.

A second tailwind that is well understood is the positive impact of corporate reforms. Encouraged by the government and Tokyo Stock Exchange, the country’s leading companies have implemented these with surprising alacrity.

Better governance is showing up in the unwinding of complex cross-shareholdings, more mergers and acquisitions, and notably in growing levels of share buybacks and dividends. More than two-thirds of companies are increasing their payouts to shareholders, a new record.

What is new, and has the potential to keep the Japanese market bubbling, is the country’s exposure to the world’s big capital expenditure themes. Japan is the ultimate picks and shovels play, not just on artificial intelligence (AI), but on reindustrialisation, reshoring, automation and higher defence spending.

In a new boring-is-good world in which capital-light Silicon Valley hands the baton to a power-hungry “real stuff” economy, Japan offers investors a broad-based, high-quality, diversified, sensibly priced opportunity. And unlike Korea, it is not a two-horse race.

Japan has deep experience and expertise in many of the sectors that will underpin the AI revolution and the broader pendulum-swing to a hard-assets economy: factory automation, precision machinery, power equipment, defence electronics, industrial materials, telecom networks and construction.

Names that you will have heard of: Kawasaki, Mitsubishi, NTT. Others that you may not have: Keyence, Softbank, Fanuc.

The top-performing sectors so far in 2026 have been metals, electrical appliances and precision machinery, and they play on AI investment via optical fibre for data centres and memory chips. Machine tool orders are at a record high, driven by demand from both the US and China.

But the winners are broader than this. The third-best-performing sector is banks, a play on domestic reflation and higher interest rates, with another quarter-point increase looking likely next week. The sustainability of returns is reflected in the more subdued returns year to date of the broad-based Topix index, compared with the narrower, tech-exposed Nikkei 225 index.

Japan has a long history of disappointing overseas investors. A colleague of mine used to joke that it was never too late to short Japan. He was wrong, but even now, overseas investors have invested less in Japan than you would expect, given its weight in the global indexes.

Slowly but surely, that is changing. Having dipped in and out of Japan, treating it as a tactical trade, longer-term investors are now seeking a permanent exposure to the country’s shares.

Domestic investors, too, are learning to love their home-grown opportunities, with about half of holdings in the Japanese equivalent of our Isa now represented by local shares. Around a fifth of household assets are invested in equities – a notable shift from the traditional Japanese preference for bank deposits and government bonds.

It is still easy to find reasons to be sceptical about Japan. The country’s ageing population is in long-term decline; it has the highest level of government debt in the developed world; its exports are vulnerable to a strengthening in the yen. But Japan is slowly becoming a normal developed economy again, with rational levels of inflation, interest rates and bond yields.

Above all, Japan is well-placed for a resurgent bricks-and-mortar world in which wealth creation reverts to the manufacture of high-quality real stuff that you can touch. No one does that as consistently well as the Japanese.

Tom Stevenson is an investment director at Fidelity International. The views are his own.

data.title: Best notice savings accounts for 2026: Today’s latest rates
data.description:
If you have a notice account, you’ll be required to notify your bank or building society a specified number of days ahead of making a withdrawal, detailing how much you want to withdraw. This notice period, set by the bank, has to pass before your funds are released.

Notice periods can range from seven days to 180, but the most common are 30, 60 and 90 days. It’s important to choose an account with a notice period that suits you, as it can be a long wait to get your money.

In theory, your patience is rewarded with higher interest rates than you would get with easy- or instant-access savings accounts. But this isn’t always the case, so you should always do some research before committing to a new account.

In this guide, Telegraph Money addresses:

  • How we determine the best rates
  • The best notice accounts for July
  • Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a notice savings account
  • How do notice accounts work?
  • Are notice accounts worth it?
  • Notice accounts FAQs

How we determine the best rates

The Best Buy tables show the best savings rates widely available in the market. This means certain accounts are excluded, including those that require minimum deposits of more than £25,000 or are available only to local or existing customers.

The data in these tables is provided by Savings Data Limited and is compiled using automated tracker tools and updates from savings providers. Savings Data Limited then manually checks the information and enters it into a database that feeds the live tables, which update daily.

The savings accounts shown are protected by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme. The information in this article is intended for information purposes and should not be taken as endorsement or advice.

The best notice accounts for July

Use the table below to discover the best notice savings accounts available.

If you decide you don’t want to wait to withdraw your cash, see our guide to the best easy-access accounts. Opting for one of the best current accounts is another way to earn interest while maintaining full access to your money.

Or, if you’d prefer to lock in a guaranteed rate for the next 12 months or more, don’t miss our guide to the best fixed-rate bonds.

For a host of tax-efficient savings options, make sure you check our guide to the best cash Isas.

Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a notice savings account

Caitlyn Eastell, of analysts Moneyfactscompare.co.uk, said: “Typically, notice accounts pay a higher rate of interest than easy-access accounts with the benefit of being able to add funds in the account once it’s been opened.

“But an important factor to consider is many providers won’t allow any withdrawals before the end of the notice period and, on the rare occasion earlier access is allowed, may result in a loss of interest.”

Mark Hicks, of investment and savings platform Hargreaves Lansdown, said: “They’re one of the more complicated structures in the savings market. Quite often people don’t realise that they cannot get money out immediately, so they’re saving into a notice account and all of a sudden, they urgently need the money.”

How do notice accounts work?

Notice accounts work in a very similar way to easy-access accounts; the difference comes when you want to withdraw money.

Instead of requesting a withdrawal that’s paid out instantly, instead you’ll only receive the money after the requisite notice period – as mentioned, this is commonly 30, 60 or 90 days but there are all sorts of options on the market.

Once the notice period is over, your money will be released.

Are notice accounts worth it?

Notice savings accounts can be worth it if you can plan your savings withdrawals well in advance. In theory, you should receive higher interest rates to make up for the inconvenience, and can usually have unlimited access to your funds – granted, after a bit of a wait.

You can also make ongoing deposits. Unlike some accounts that only accept a single initial deposit, notice accounts allow you to continue adding to your savings whenever you like. Generally, savings will still earn interest during the notice period, but this is worth checking.

For new savers, the notice period may help with money management and preventing impulse buying.

Notice accounts FAQs

Yes, you can withdraw money from the account, but you’ll need to wait until the notice period passes to receive it. This commonly varies from 30 to 180 days.

Some accounts may impose limits on the number of withdrawals that can be made, while others may have a minimum deposit requirement you’ll need to bear in mind – it is worth checking the account’s terms and conditions to find out.

The biggest disadvantage of a notice account is having to wait for a set amount of time before being able to access your funds, which could be an issue if you need money at short notice.

data.title: Factory bosses prepare to shift jobs abroad as ‘patience runs out’
data.description:
Factory bosses have issued a plea to the Government to alleviate the cost burden on industry and prevent an exodus of manufacturers.

Without urgent government action to bring down energy bills and employment costs, the deindustrialisation of Britain will accelerate in the coming months, costing thousands of jobs across the country, businesses warned.

A quarter of the UK’s manufacturing base is threatening to shift operations overseas, and more than one in four fear they could run out of cash in the next 12 months if ministers don’t do more to temper soaring costs.

A slew of recently expanded government relief schemes will fail to halt the crisis sweeping through heavy industry, manufacturers said.

The stark findings came in a survey of British industrial companies jointly conducted by Make UK, the manufacturing lobby group, and the Trades Union Congress.

“Despite a promise in last year’s Industrial Strategy of ‘bold action’ to bring down energy costs, nothing has yet been delivered to support businesses. Patience has run out,” Make UK said.

Stephen Phipson, chief executive of Make UK, said: “The time for talking is over. The time for action is now. Britain faces deindustrialisation unless manufacturers get relief from high energy prices.

“Electricity and gas in the UK are far too expensive, and it’s costing our country steeply. We cannot afford to be delayed by political upheaval or by further consultations,” he added.

Mr Phipson urged the Government to “step in and act now” to expand the British Industrial Competitiveness Scheme (BICS), which helps lower factories’ power bills. The programme needs to apply “to all of the manufacturing industry,” and its delivery must be sped up, he said.

Ministers have broadened the scope of BICS from 7,000 eligible companies to 10,000. Businesses will receive a 25pc reduction in electricity bills from April 2027 via a one-off payment. It will also be backdated by a year.

But the industry believes it doesn’t go far enough and has complained about the nearly one-year wait for support. Make UK wants to see it expanded to 130,000 companies across the entire manufacturing sector.

Paul Nowak, the general secretary of the Trades Union Congress, said: “BICS is an important step towards tackling the punishing cost of energy for manufacturers. But with Donald Trump’s reckless war in Iran continuing to hammer energy bills, the scheme needs to be expanded further to protect jobs and keep factories and plants running.”

He added: “We also need a long-term plan to support workers in manufacturing industries and bring bills down for good through energy efficiency and decarbonisation. That’s how we can reduce the impact of shocks and secure the future of UK industry.”

Make UK said the need for further support was given greater urgency because energy prices in Britain were already the highest in the G7 before the conflict in the Middle East and had increased further since the outbreak of war in the region.

Almost half of the survey’s respondents have experienced a further increase in their energy bills since the start of the conflict in the Middle East, with six in 10 passing this rise on to customers.

According to the Chemicals Industry Association, Britain experienced a near-40pc fall in UK chemical production between 2021 and 2024, with 25 site closures.

data.title: Burnham accused of ‘smoke and mirrors’ over Manchester bus claims
data.description:
Andy Burnham’s much-lauded Manchester bus revolution has been a costly “smoke and mirrors” flop, the boss of Britain’s biggest private bus firm has claimed.

In the run-up to Thursday’s Makerfield by-election, Mr Burnham has touted the Bee Network as proof of his ability to deliver on radical policies, saying he had delivered a transport system Manchester deserves.

It was unveiled by the Greater Manchester Mayor in 2018 and took over the running of buses from the private sector in 2024. Mr Burnham has cited the model as a blueprint for possible nationalisations were he to become prime minister.

However, Ralph Roberts, the chairman of McGill’s Buses, said the network was reliant on cash from taxpayers.

“You have to be aware that the buses in Greater Manchester are a smoke-and-mirrors operation existing in huge handouts from central government,” he said.

Transport for Greater Manchester (TfGM), which runs the Bee Network, is to receive a £226m subsidy from the Government in the current financial year.

A spokesman argued that this offered better value than the more than £1bn granted to Transport for London (TfL). The Bee Network serves a population of around three million, while TfL serves a core population of nine million.

Mr Roberts said that the public were being given the wrong impression of the scheme.

Mr Burnham said last month, when announcing the extension of the Bee Network to cover local trains, that he had delivered “a public transport system of the kind this city deserves and it is already bringing huge benefits for our residents; making it easier to get around but also lowering the cost of public transport”.

Mr Roberts said: “There has been some level of change on the ground in terms of changing between the bus and the tram on one ticket. But it hasn’t delivered anything transformational.

“What the person in the street would have been looking for would be earlier buses, later buses, more buses, services in areas that are currently not served.

“Instead they’ve been sidetracked with non-essential things such as branding. You tie up all of your manpower with incidentals when you need to focus on the essentials.”

Greater Manchester’s buses have had mediocre ratings in customer satisfaction surveys undertaken by Transport Focus, ranking 26th out of 43 local authorities in England last year.

Between 13pc and 16pc of Bee Network journeys were deemed unsatisfactory at its three operators, compared with just 3pc at Glasgow-based McGill’s.

Mr Roberts said: “The industry warned them about what they were doing and they didn’t listen and they ploughed on and the cost has run away with itself.

“It’s easy to deliver a grandiose scheme when someone’s shovelling money at you and you don’t have to pay for it yourself. But as we well know the UK Government’s credit card is maxed out so those bailouts are coming to an end.”

Auditors for TfGM failed to sign off on accounts for the 2025 fiscal year after it missed the deadline for publishing statements. They described the failure as a “significant weakness” in governance.

The authority said that as a pioneer of bus franchising it was subject to “novel contractual arrangements” that complicated the accounting process. This was the reason for it missing audit deadlines, it said, and there was no suggestion that accounts were incorrect.

A further report said TfGM had disclosed “a significant level of uncertainty” because of bus and trams revenues “not growing in line with pre-pandemic forecasts”, which would compel it to use £31m of local reserves even after seeking savings of £16m.

Mr Roberts said that the Bee Network would already have run out of cash were it not for Labour’s victory in the 2024 general election, which he claimed had prolonged subsidies.

“That will not continue,” he said. “Next financial year they will struggle and probably the burden will fall on the Greater Manchester taxpayers. Or they are going to have to cut costs and services to the point that they are worse than they were to start with.

“People don’t see the losses because they have been insulated from that. But the bubble will burst.”

TfGM said Manchester’s buses were more reliable than before franchising, with a greener and more accessible fleet and services that run to more places more often. It said the Bee Network had recorded the biggest increase in passenger satisfaction in the country in the latest Transport Focus survey.

A spokesman said fares were never sufficient to fund public transport networks, with around 50pc of its costs covered by takings and a further quarter through local taxation.

Mr Roberts said that Mr Burnham’s touting of the Bee Network as an example to other cities as Labour seeks to replace the privatised network introduced under Margaret Thatcher was particularly galling.

The company has opposed plans to move to a similar system in Strathclyde, which could affect its operations in the area.

Mr Roberts added: “Other places think, ‘Oh well, it’s worked in Manchester, the buses are still running, people are not revolting, so why don’t we do it?’

“They all have the idea that bus companies are making huge profits. Manchester has now found out that that wasn’t the case, they’ve realised, well hold on, these profits don’t exist. It’s all about control and political dogma.”

Authorities in Strathclyde, he said, were “solely focused on Manchester”, while South Yorkshire “is openly saying we’ll buy the depots and all of the buses, but we’ll buy them with free money from central government that they don’t have to pay back”.

Under the franchise model, councils take charge of bus fleets and depots, and hire operators to provide a service at a fixed price.

Mr Roberts said: “We’re not compensated, we just get a fair price for our assets, but that’s not the value of a business. None of that is paid for.

“As happened in Manchester, some business will vanish. They just don’t exist any more, some of the smaller ones, because they didn’t win any of the contracts.”

data.title: Hacked off Hugh Grant pushes Andy Burnham to curb free speech
data.description:
In a sweltering Westminster basement at the peak of last week’s heatwave, Hugh Grant, looking relaxed in shorts and loafers, was in an optimistic mood.

“As we switch leadership of the Labour Party, switch prime ministers, there’s a chance finally to get something done,” the actor and press regulation campaigner told a gathering of like-minded activists.

“All I say to whoever is the new prime minister is when you’re weighing up whether to do the right thing for the people against a massive corporation or to cosy up to those giant corporations for your own political career, bear this in mind: those media organisations are going to pour a bucket of s--- over you whatever you do. Whether you support them or whether you don’t.”

Grant, who also goes by the online handle @HackedOffHugh in reference to the well-funded campaign group Hacked Off, is not a lone voice of optimism in these circles as Andy Burnham prepares for power.

For 15 years, Hacked Off has demanded state regulation of Fleet Street. It still wants ministers to push ahead with the second part of the Leveson Inquiry into the culture and ethics of the press, which was launched in the wake of the phone hacking scandal at Rupert Murdoch’s newspaper empire.

Yet their calls have met resistance across successive governments. Matt Hancock, the culture secretary at the time, confirmed in 2018 that the second phase had been scrapped. Sir Keir Starmer in his first month in power ruled out a Leveson revival.

Grant, who was accompanied by the former X Factor judge Tulisa Contostavlos at last week’s event, salted his optimism with a frustrated lament for the outgoing Prime Minister.

“If ever there was a case in point, it’s Keir Starmer, who last week was at the Murdoch summer party pretty much wearing a bucket of s---,” Grant said.

“So this is a chance for someone new to be – in my opinion – a statesman instead of a politician, to govern instead of just playing Westminster snakes and ladders, to protect the public rather than protect vested interests. So let’s hope this is a hopeful moment – it just might be.”

Hope on hope. As Team Burnham prepares to sweep into No 10, is @HackedOffHugh really on the cusp of a historic triumph over his tabloid tormentors in the form of state control of Britain’s free press?

‘Burnham may be prepared to listen’

What is certain is that press regulation is just one item on a lengthy list of unresolved policy questions facing Burnham. Given the scale and urgency of his challenges on the economy, welfare and defence, the ancient hatreds of the London media bubble might seem like one for the “King of the North” to stick on the back burner.

Yet he has already made time for the Hacked Off campaigners on his road to No 10.

Grant, who in 2003 memorably played a lovelorn Tony Blair cipher in Love Actually, and fellow press critic Steve Coogan were welcomed by Burnham in Makerfield, in the late stages of the former mayor of Manchester’s by-election campaign.

In public the pair contributed a bit of stardust. It seems unlikely that they did not deliver private entreaties to take action against the press.

Writing on X this week, the former Sun editor David Yelland, now a public relations adviser and supporter of state press regulation, said there was “real disquiet among the editor class” about Grant’s proximity to Burnham.

“I know there was intense lobbying of the Starmer team for Leveson 2 – it was fruitless. Burnham may be prepared to listen,” he said.

He certainly listens to Anneliese Midgley, a 2024-intake MP for Knowsley and former trade union political operative. She was a key figure in Burnham’s by-election campaign and is tipped for chief whip or a No 10 role in his government.

Happily for Hacked Off, Midgeley is also a close friend of Lord Watson, a key figure in the political response to the phone hacking scandal back in 2011.

He received his own payout from the Murdoch empire, alongside Prince Harry, in an out-of-court settlement last year. Midgeley has taken up Watson’s mantle in the Commons and in September called for a debate about renewed allegations from the investigative journalist Nick Davies, who originally exposed the phone hacking scandal.

Nathan Sparkes, the chief executive of Hacked Off, told The Telegraph: “People are very disappointed in Keir Starmer’s record on this, really disappointed, and hopeful that a change of administration would be more open to keeping their commitments.

“We are definitely hopeful. Frankly, Keir Starmer in his two years has done nothing on regulation despite the promises on that, and has kept to the Tory position of not re-establishing Leveson part two.

“There’s no more that he could have really offered to newspaper owners as far as we’re concerned. So in that sense it can’t get worse.”

There’s plenty of encouragement for Hacked Off in Burnham’s political past, too. He has been a proponent of Section 40 of the Crime and Courts Act 2013, legislation designed to force news publishers to join a state-backed regulator.

Under the measures, publishers would have been liable to pay all legal costs in libel or privacy lawsuits, even if they won, unless they were a member of such a regulator.

The laws were ultimately formally repealed by the Tories under the Media Act 2024, before they were ever brought into force.

As shadow home secretary under Jeremy Corbyn’s turbulent Labour leadership, Burnham was a vocal supporter of the proposed second stage of the Leveson Inquiry.

It was envisaged as an investigation of the relationship between the press and the police, which has become unrecognisably distant since the phone hacking scandal engulfed Rupert Murdoch’s titles in 2011.

Burnham said in 2016 that part two of Leveson was “non-negotiable”, seeing it as intrinsically linked with campaigning on the 1989 Hillsborough disaster, which was subject to a police cover-up that was promoted in parts of the press.

Byline Times, an online publication that campaigns for press regulation, reported that Burnham said in a chance encounter in March that “I haven’t given up on [Leveson part two]” and would do it “if he had the influence”.

Well, he has now. The writer of the Byline Times report, Adrian Quinn, a press regulation campaigner of the University of Leeds, said: “I take him at his word – he said he would and we shook on it.”

Changing media landscape

Celebrities including Grant, Sienna Miller and JK Rowling lined up to testify about their treatment at the hands of what they portrayed as a rapacious British press.

Rupert Murdoch and his younger son James were grilled about their knowledge of illegal practices at their newspapers, as well as the subsequent cover-up.

Executives, including Murdoch’s closest British lieutenant, Rebekah Brooks, and Paul Dacre, then editor of the Daily Mail, gave evidence. Even David Cameron, the former prime minister, was questioned on his dealings with the press.

The result was a 2,000-page report that eviscerated the culture of the press, concluding that journalists had caused “real hardship and, on occasion, wreaked havoc with the lives of innocent people”.

Lord Justice Leveson’s key recommendations included the scrapping of the industry-funded Press Complaints Commission and formation of a new regulator underpinned by statute.

The press fought against any form of state involvement, viewing it as anathema to Britain’s history of free and independent journalism. Publishers mounted an ultimately successful campaign to repeal Section 40, the law written to force them to submit.

The regulator Impress, which is recognised by the Government and underpinned by a Royal Charter, has failed to secure the buy-in of any major publisher. Meanwhile most of Fleet Street is signed up to the Independent Press Standards Organisation (Ipso), a self-regulatory body, which they fund.

Regardless, the news business has completely transformed since Leveson’s report was published in November 2012, and not only in its dealings with the police.

Tabloids in particular, the target of most of the criticism in Lord Justice Leveson’s opus, are nowhere near the cultural or financial force they were a decade ago. The bundle of crime, celebrity gossip, sport, humour, soft pornography and games which built their power has unravelled in a digital explosion of choice.

Only quality journalism – broadsheet, in print parlance – which prioritises information over entertainment, has been able to build a sustainable digital future through subscriptions.

Meanwhile the aggressive and frequently illegal news-gathering techniques that fuelled scoops at the News of the World have been neutered by more powerful forces than Leveson’s press regulation.

Privacy laws and social media networks that allow celebrities to feed authorised images and stories direct to their public have turned the world ruled by Murdoch’s Sunday tabloid juggernaut into a distant foreign land.

Indeed social media and the warped priorities its algorithms impose are now the big concern in Whitehall, where it is increasingly blamed for fraying Britain’s social fabric in a more dangerous way than gossip about Grant ever could.

The power of social media

The scene is set for an almighty free-speech clash under Burnham, in which the British news publishing business is a relatively minor concern.

After the Southport riots in 2024, Ofcom raised concerns about the crucial role social media played in spreading and amplifying misleading and divisive material.

Officials have also highlighted its role in recent unrest in Belfast and in clashes between Hindus and Muslims in Leicester in 2022.

In each case the suspected influence of malign foreign forces has compounded concern in Whitehall.

Now Labour has a plan to fight back. Lisa Nandy, the culture secretary, another close ally of Burnham, published plans this week to force tech giants such as YouTube, Facebook and X to prominently display news from public-service broadcasters including the BBC, ITV and Channel 4.

Predictably, platforms which build their advertising businesses on serving up whatever is most likely to keep users engaged are ready to oppose the plans.

“The UK’s creator economy is a global success story because of one simple idea: on YouTube, viewers decide what they want to watch,” says David Wheeldon, YouTube’s head of public policy for Europe. “Videos become popular because they connect with their audience, not because we’re required to prioritise them.”

He adds: “Prominence rules seek to distort that – forcing YouTube to prioritise government-picked channels over whatever viewers actually came to watch. That’s not fair on users, creators or the wider journalism ecosystem.”

Matthew Sinclair, the senior director at tech trade body CCIA, which is funded by tech giants, including Google and Meta, brands the new proposals as a “radical departure” from previous rules aiming to ensure broadcasters’ apps are easy to find in the streaming era.

“It would mean users were served content less relevant to their interests and would be unfair to the many smaller creators working to find an audience,” he said.

“British media has often won an audience around the world, and the broadcasters should work to make content that attracts new viewers in Britain and abroad, not ask the Government to dig them a regulatory moat here in the UK.”

The move would inarguably represent a significant new incursion by the state into influencing speech online and would be sure to further escalate hostilities with the Trump administration. The president has already welcomed Burnham by branding him “extremely liberal”.

Trump’s officials are increasingly attacking UK digital regulations as an assault on free speech, amid lobbying by the technology industry. Senior figures including JD Vance have also argued that curbs on social media are a sign of growing authoritarianism.

This weekend a spokesman for the majority-Republican House Judiciary Committee told The Telegraph: “For years, we have warned that UK and European tech regulators are working to censor American speech.

“The UK’s Online Safety Act empowers the Government to pressure American social media platforms into suppressing so-called ‘disinformation’, ‘misinformation’ and ‘hate speech’.

“Now regulators have gone even further, deciding what news outlets people see. Big government will never be satisfied and its censorship efforts will keep expanding.”

It is a critique with some grounding in history. Woven through Labour is an ancient thread of grievance about the media. It argues that the party has been less electorally successful than it otherwise would have because the most popular and influential newspapers tend to oppose socialism.

To this mindset, which is by no means prevailing in Labour, greater state control of the press has a siren appeal that is now extending to social media.

Newspaper proprietors were the enemy, and they are now joined by tech executives and especially Elon Musk as he wades in the dark waters of the digital hard Right.

Sir Keir Starmer’s official biographer, the former journalist Tom Baldwin, late last year reported concern inside No 10 that the press and social media were conspiring to make Britain ungovernable and perpetually angry.

He wrote: “A casual flick through the newspapers last weekend reveals a Mail columnist whose every word is coated in foam-flecked rage. He called Rachel Reeves a ‘liar’ eight times, before declaring the real question to be decided after her budget is ‘whether the chancellor will have to resign, or if she’s heading to jail’.

“That pales in comparison to what you find below the line in the comments section, where one reader opines that ‘jail is not enough’ for her. It gets madder still in the sewers of social media, where mention of Starmer is followed by smears involving ‘Jimmy Savile’, ‘paedos’ and ‘grooming gangs’.

Nandy’s social-media prominence proposals appear to be partly the result of such concern. She signalled they may offer a new route for Burnham to impose state-backed regulation of the press.

In response to a question from Jeremy Corbyn in the Commons this week, Nandy also said she met the families of victims of press intrusion “very recently”.

She added: “We have always acted with care in this space to ensure that we protect a free and fair press, but he [Corbyn] is right to say that action is needed to better protect people in this space, and this Government are determined to take that action.”

Nandy’s green paper on social media, which carried the tellingly grandiose subtitle “a new strategic direction for UK media”, said the Government will examine “whether the benefit of prominence should be explicitly linked to further responsibilities for news providers”.

They added: “This could include greater transparency, effective complaints processes, more stringent standards in AI content use, promoting media literacy or adherence to evolving best practices.”

Ian Murray, the media minister, this week refused to rule out the rumoured possibility that prominence for publishers in social media will be made conditional on their acceptance of state-backed regulation.

‘Entirely the wrong battle’

The potential battle with Burnham comes at a painful moment for traditional news publishers, which are grappling with a sharp decline in online readership as changes to Google search and social media algorithms upend traffic.

This has been exacerbated by the rollout of AI overviews in Google that summarise material from news outlets, removing the need for readers to click through at all.

Research by the Reuters Institute found that traffic to news websites from Google’s search engine plunged by a third in 2025. Industry bosses said they expected traffic to fall a further 43pc in the next three years.

For advertising-funded news groups such as Reach, which owns the Mirror and Express as well as dozens of regional titles, the impact has been devastating. The company reported its biggest loss in more than a decade last year, which it blamed on a collapse in referrals from Google.

For some newspaper executives, the timing appears opportunistic – if not downright exploitative. With many publishers on their knees, the offer of greater prominence online will simply be too valuable to turn down. Submitting to tougher regulation may seem like a small price to pay in exchange.

Nevertheless, newspaper bosses are concerned about this potential Trojan horse in the Government’s new measures.

“It’s entirely the wrong battle for the wrong time – things have moved on and I would hope that he [Burnham] understands that,” says one industry source.

“If the work started by the green paper were to be hijacked to advance a press regulation agenda it would completely undermine the Government’s aim of combating misinformation, which requires a strong and vibrant free press to counter and debunk falsehoods before they take hold.”

Nigel Huddleston, the shadow culture secretary, also criticised the idea of imposing state-backed regulation through the back door, warning that “more government intrusion and regulation is unlikely to be the answer”.

But Alex Davies-Jones, the Labour MP who resigned as a Home Office minister last month in protest against Starmer’s leadership, said at this week’s Hacked Off event: “We are going through a transition right now of government, of prime ministers, and I am hopeful because things have to change. It has been far too long, but we have to keep going.”

The new momentum for Hacked Off’s long quest for state-backed regulation of the press has come at a good moment for the group.

It is awaiting the judgment in Prince Harry’s phone hacking claim against the publisher of the Daily Mail, which had been a focus for the campaign.

During a bruising 11-week courtroom drama, Prince Harry and a roster of other high-profile claimants, including Sir Elton John and Liz Hurley, accused the Daily Mail of engaging in a “clear, systematic and sustained” campaign of unlawful information gathering that included bugging and phone hacking.

The newspaper group denied the allegations, with its lawyers arguing that Prince Harry’s friends were a “good source of leaks”.

Victory for the prince over the Daily Mail could breathe new life into Hacked Off and an old story by confirming phones were hacked at another Fleet Street publisher. However the defeat could risk a mortal wound for the campaign, albeit it is well funded.

Now though, it has Burnham, “if he has the influence”.

As for @HackedOffHugh, he declined to discuss the issues with The Telegraph offstage. “Sorry mate, got a plane to catch.”

data.title: Private jets are green, EU court rules
data.description:
Private jets are a green investment, the European Union’s second-highest court has ruled.

Production of the aircraft cannot be classified as bad for the environment and should not be excluded from a roster of activities deemed suitable for green investments, the EU General Court said on Wednesday.

The ruling followed a complaint from plane-maker Dassault Aviation at the European Commission’s decision to bar private jets from the bloc’s sustainable finance taxonomy list.

It comes as a blow to campaigners who have dismissed the planes as playthings of the ultra-rich and multinationals that should be banned because of their outsized contribution to carbon emissions.

Private jets have been singled out by climate campaigners as an obvious target to be axed by an aviation sector that is struggling to cut emissions at the same pace as other industries.

At a 2024 protest against the expansion of Farnborough Airport, Britain’s biggest business jet hub, campaigner Greta Thunberg said that private jet users were “sacrificing people and the planet to maintain their extreme lifestyles”.

The EU General Court annulled a regulation issued by the commission in 2023 specifically excluding private jets from the list of economic activities qualifying as environmentally sustainable.

Paris-based Dassault, which makes the Falcon range of business jets as well as the Rafale fighter, had claimed the exclusion was unlawful.

The court found that the carbon footprint of the aircraft related to their operation, rather than resulting from the manufacturing process, on which the list was intended to advise.

It said the commission was also wrong to bar the jets on the grounds of their higher CO2 output per passenger kilometre when compared with commercial airliners, trains, buses and cars, which could never offer the same degree of speed and flexibility.

It added that the EU’s executive arm had failed to consider the fact that the aircraft could be flown on sustainable fuel derived from sources including used cooking oil.

Campaign group Possible last month called for private jets to be banned during the fuel crisis triggered by the Iran war and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

It said that grounding the 100,000 private flights from the UK each year would provide enough fuel to fly 730,000 families of four from London to Malaga and back.

Other groups have targeted the Cannes Film Festival, claiming that the 750 private jet flights there released emissions comparable to 14,000 airline passengers flying from Paris to Athens.

Dassault said it welcomed the ruling and that the commission had “blatantly failed to consider the specific characteristics of business aviation and its role”.

The commission can appeal against the ruling within two ​months.

data.title: Sky’s US owner to spin off media business ahead of ITV takeover
data.description:
The US owner of Sky is to break itself in two, casting its British arm into the unpredictable maelstrom of media mergers being propelled by the decline of traditional television.

Comcast said it will spin off the combination of NBCUniversal and Sky as a separately listed company within a year, leaving behind its more profitable and stable broadband empire.

NBCUniversal includes the major Hollywood studio Universal and its associated theme parks, including a planned £6bn investment in a UK site in Bedford.

However, separated from Comcast and its differences with NBCUniversal more exposed, Sky is likely to be seen as a potential spin-off itself, even as it closes in on the £1.6bn takeover of ITV’s broadcasting business.

The global entertainment industry is undergoing significant upheaval as Paramount seizes control of its larger rival, Warner Bros Discovery, with the assistance of Gulf states and the Trump administration. Warner was itself spun out by Comcast’s broadband rival, AT&T, only four years ago.

Like Warner, an independent NBCUniversal will be seen as a takeover target. Speculation is certain to surround Netflix, which offered to take control of Warner this year, as well as Amazon and Apple. In such a big picture scenario, Sky would likely be cast off as a potential competition obstacle.

Alex DeGroote, a media analyst, said: “I suspect it is the first step towards either a disposal or some form of fairly major change [for Sky].” He added: “There’s a sense in the UK that Sky’s not as valuable as it was when Murdoch sold it.”

Shares in Comcast jumped by almost a quarter in pre-market trading as investors welcomed its planned break with the challenging media business.

Potential buyers for the pay-TV pioneer include major private equity firms such as KKR, which is advised by Jeremy Darroch, the former Sky chief executive and European operators such as London-listed Canal+. The planned addition of the heavily regulated ITV broadcast business would probably bring scrutiny to any transaction.

Mike Cavanagh, Comcast’s co-chief executive, who will lead the standalone NBCUniversal, denied that the break-up was preparation for a future sale, telling investors: “Our plan for NBCUniversal and Sky is to build and invest for growth.”

Comcast acquired Sky at the nosebleed price of £30bn in 2018, but has found the growth required to justify its investment hard to deliver. Brian Roberts, the chairman and controlling shareholder, has gradually curbed disclosure of Sky’s financial performance to Wall Street investors who were mostly sceptical of the takeover from the outset.

Comcast wrote down its investment by $8.6bn (£6.4bn), or around a quarter, in 2022, as the streaming revolution ate into Sky’s more lucrative satellite business.

Traditional entertainment companies are racing to gain heft and cut costs to take on the tech giants, or in some cases deliberately allowing for what amounts to a managed decline as audiences dwindle. Comcast spun off its most challenged traditional channels as a separate business, Versant, at the turn of the year, for instance.

Sky, which has been losing exclusivity over films and television as streaming services have proliferated, has sought to reinvent itself as an aggregator, able to help consumers navigate the confusing array of options. Its telecoms services, and particularly mobile, have proved easier going.

Sky has increased investment in its own production capabilities, but its primary focus on its consumer subscription relationships sets it apart from the rest of NBCUniversal, with its intellectual property and advertising-supported network television.

NBCUniversal also operates a subscription streaming service, Peacock, but has struggled to compete with Netflix and Disney.

Comcast declined to comment on speculation about Sky’s future, but a source close to the company stressed that the separation would take at least a year to complete. The company said it will retain a stake of almost 20pc in the new company and that Mr Roberts will remain involved.
