data.title: How to choose the best children’s savings accounts for 2026: Today’s latest rates
data.description:
A children’s savings account works in a very similar way to an adult one. You can open many of these accounts for children under the age of 18 with as little as £1. Seeing the balance grow is a good way to teach children financial skills as many accounts allow children over the age of seven to add and withdraw money themselves.

You can choose between an easy or instant-access, fixed term or a regular savings account. Easy or instant-access accounts allow you to withdraw or deposit money at any time, but they usually have lower interest rates. Regular savings accounts can require you to save every month and the withdrawals may not be as straightforward. They generally pay higher interest, but this can be reduced if you miss a monthly deposit.

In this guide, we will cover:

  • How we determine the best rates
  • The best children’s savings accounts in July
  • Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing an account for your child
  • How to open a children’s savings account
  • Children’s savings account vs Junior Isa
  • Children’s savings accounts FAQs

How we determine the best rates

The Best Buy tables show the best savings rates widely available in the market. This means certain accounts are excluded, including those that require minimum deposits of more than £25,000 or are available only to local or existing customers.

The data in these tables is provided by Savings Data Limited and is compiled using automated tracker tools and updates from savings providers. Savings Data Limited then manually checks the information and enters it into a database that feeds the live tables, which update daily.

The savings accounts shown are protected by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme. The information in this article is intended for information purposes and should not be taken as endorsement or advice.

The best children’s savings accounts in July

The table below highlights the best savings accounts you can open for your children.

For more ways to save for your child, check out our guide to the best Junior cash Isas.

If you’re looking for a new home for your own savings, you can find the best tax-efficient options in our guide to cash Isas, while our pick of easy access savings accounts can reveal the best options if you want to add and withdraw money whenever you like.

Alternatively, you may be able to find higher rates if you’re willing to lock your cash away for a year or more – the top rates are in our guide to the best fixed-rate bonds.

Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing an account for your child

  • What type of account suits you best?
  • What’s the interest rate?
  • Is it suitable for your child’s age?
  • Is it easy to manage?
  • Are there any restrictive terms for deposits and withdrawals?

Caitlyn Eastell, of analyst Moneyfactscompare.co.uk, said: “There are many children’s savings accounts to choose from, ranging from easy access, regular savers or Junior Isas. They are a good option for parents wishing to save for their child’s future.”

But she reminded savers of the tax implications: “It is worth noting that children are still liable to pay tax and have the same personal allowance as adults and once the child reaches a certain age, usually 18, the account will automatically roll into an adult savings account, where they will have full control.”

Mark Hicks, of stockbroker Hargreaves Lansdown, said: “Children’s accounts are brilliant but are not advertised enough. I think the UK still has a big problem in that financial education, particularly in schools, is not on the agenda. So getting a child an account and encouraging them to save from a young age is incredibly important.”

What makes a good children’s savings account?

  • A good savings rate: This might seem obvious, but the money you save for your children will grow and compound at a much faster rate if you can make sure the interest rate is competitive. If possible, it should at least match inflation – otherwise the money will be losing value in real terms.
  • Easy to manage: Consider whether you’ll need the option to make withdrawals, how you want to open and manage the account, and whether there are decent options when your child gets older – for instance, does the provider offer accounts for teenagers to help them manage their money?
  • Suits your savings plans: Many children’s accounts can be opened with £1, and may have fairly low maximum deposits. However, these won’t be suitable if you have a more significant amount to save.
  • Suitable for your child’s age: Watch out for minimum and maximum age requirements as this may affect when you can start saving for your child, and how long you can save for.

How to open a children’s savings account

  1. Choose an account: Weighing up the factors explained above, choose a provider and account that suits you and your child.
  2. Check the small print: Just in case there are any restrictions or details that aren’t obvious, comb the terms and conditions for anything that might make you change your mind.
  3. Go with your opening method of choice: Once you’re happy, go and open the account. Some providers let you open an account online or you may have to visit a branch or do it by post. Providers may have their own rules for how old a child needs to be to open an account under their own name, and young children, often under eight, typically need a parent to sign and set one up on their behalf. You may need to provide documents such as a birth certificate for the child and your own ID.
  4. Start saving: You may be able to save into your child’s account via online payments or bank transfers. If your child receives money from friends and family, such as on their birthday, you may also be able to get them involved by taking them to a branch to make a cash deposit. Be sure to take note of any deposit restrictions.

Managing your child’s account

Some accounts let children manage the account themselves from a certain age, depending on whether they were made a signatory when it was opened.

In some cases, even if the child can manage the account, they may need to have a parent or guardian with them when they visit the bank to request changes, or withdraw money.

Children’s accounts usually convert to an adult savings account once the child turns 18, but there is more flexibility before that and you may be able to access the funds if needed.

Are child’s savings accounts taxed?

Children don’t usually have to pay tax on savings interest – but it’s not because they can’t be taxed. Since children don’t usually have any other forms of income, the amount earned from savings interest rarely exceeds the combined amount of their personal allowance and personal savings allowance (PSA).

But if a parent is contributing money into the account and it earns more than £100, this will go towards their own personal savings allowance.

If you are worried about this, another type of child savings account is the Junior Isa (Jisa).

This has a separate annual £9,000 allowance to the adult Isa that can be put into a cash or stocks and shares Isa for children to access after the turn 18. All growth is tax-free – for both children and parents.

Children’s savings account vs Junior Isa

  • Product choice: There are generally more children’s savings account to choose from than Junior Isas, and interest rates can be higher for savings accounts.
  • Tax: Interest or investment growth in Junior Isas is tax-free, but interest earned in a children’s savings account is taxable.
  • Access: No money can be withdrawn from a Junior Isa. With these accounts, the money is locked away until the child becomes an adult. At this point, the account will convert to an adult Isa, and they will get full control of it. Many children’s savings accounts will allow withdrawals, which can be a useful way to teach children about the value of money.
  • Type of account: Children’s savings account just save in cash, but with a Junior Isa parents can choose to save in cash or invest with a stocks and shares option for their child.

Children’s savings accounts FAQs

Grandparents may be able to open a savings account for their grandchildren, as long as they bring proof of identity, such as a birth certificate. You usually need permission from the parents.

The cash from a grandparent won’t be taxed at the point it was given, though it could be caught by inheritance tax depending on when the gift was made.

Parents and step-parents can become liable for tax on their child’s savings interest, so it’s something to keep an eye on.

If over £100 interest or dividends is earned on the money you give a child, the interest will be taxed as if it were yours. This does not apply to Junior Isas or child trust funds.

This rule only applies to parents – friends and grandparents can give as much as they like without worrying about the interest.

As for the possibility of children paying tax on savings interest, that could also happen – but it’s much less likely. As with adults, children are liable for tax and have a personal allowance of £12,570, plus a £5,000 starting savings allowance and a £1,000 personal savings allowance. If annual income, including interest, exceeds the personal allowance, then income tax will be due.

This depends on the account and the age of the child. Generally, if your child is under eight the account is held in trust by the adult who opened it. Some accounts allow the parent to remain the signatory until your child is 16.

Many accounts let children take control of their savings from the age of seven, meaning they can deposit and withdraw money.

As a parent, you can open a savings account for your child from when they’re born – doing so means you can get saving for them straightaway, so their money can grow and compound over the longest time possible.

Certain accounts will have specific age restrictions – particularly those that offer some form of access for your child. These can be great when your children get a bit older, commonly from around age seven, as a way to teach them about saving and the value of money. Providers will be able to advise what age restrictions are applicable for specific accounts.

data.title: Housebuilder behind ‘flat-pack’ net zero homes on brink of collapse
data.description:
A housebuilder constructing “flat-pack” net zero homes is scrambling to find a buyer after it was left on the brink of collapse.

Agile Property and Homes, which erects prefab “eco-homes” made of materials such as straw and timber, has lodged an intention to appoint administrators after soaring building costs hammered its finances.

To stave off collapse, the Oxfordshire-based company is in advanced discussions with a potential buyer, according to the group’s proposed administrators.

It said a sale of the business was imminent although no deal had been agreed.

Agile develops social and affordable housing, partnering with local authorities, NHS trusts and charities on building homes.

The business presents itself as a pioneer of building flat-pack homes made of straw and timber, fitted with triple-glazed windows, heat pumps and solar panels.

The homes can be built in as little as a day and be ready for occupation within six weeks, according to its website.

Its building system was created with funding from the former Department for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy, the Technology Strategy Board, now known as Innovate UK, as well as the European Commission.

Craig White, its chief executive, told industry publication Inside Housing last year that it had “the proven model to tackle the UK’s housing crisis at low cost and speed”.

Its projects included an initiative sponsored by the Ministry of Justice and the housing department to provide “eco-homes” built by prison inmates, for more than 400 people in temporary housing.

‘The sector is struggling’

Andy Pear, one of the proposed administrators at Moorfields Advisory, said Agile has been hit by a host of financial pressures affecting the industry, including soaring build costs.

Mr Pear pointed to sharply rising input costs – such as energy, labour and raw materials – after years of inflationary pressure, recently made worse by the war in Ukraine.

He said: “The sector is struggling due to cost inflation. Inputs into the business have gone up in price substantially. That’s also created affordability issues for the whole property market.”

The business built its pipeline around government-backed affordable housing schemes, but has been caught in a wider slowdown in delivery.

The Government is falling short of its ambition to build 1.5 million homes by 2029.

To reach its target, it needs to build 300,000 homes per year, but just more than 150,000 a year will be built between now and March 2028, down from 189,000 last year, according to Savills.

“The planning system is broken, delays in getting planning permission have got worse and there’s a massive skills shortage in the UK,” said Mr Pear.

“There are all of these structural issues across the UK’s housing sector, which the Government hasn’t really dealt with.

“It will be a call to action for a new Prime Minister to get this sorted out, because it’s going to get worse before it gets better.”

Agile was contacted for comment.

data.title: Trains at their most popular in a century... but still arriving late
data.description:
Passengers are taking the most railway journeys in a century despite punctuality hitting its worst level since Covid, new statistics show.

Passengers made 1.83 billion journeys by rail between last April and March this year, the Office of Rail and Road (ORR) said.

This is the highest level of railway usage since 1920, when there were 2.18 billion journeys, according to official records.

The number has increased by 6 per cent on the 1.73 billion journeys recorded in the previous year, and is a significant jump from the 1.75 billion in 2018-19 – the previous record after 1920.

Yet more trains missed their scheduled arrival times last year than at any point over the past five years.

Senior rail figures have urged the Government to plan for “consistently growing numbers of rail users”, while the ORR has called for train operators to have a “relentless focus” on timeliness.

During 2025-26, only two thirds (66.7 per cent) of trains across Britain arrived on time, meaning within one minute of their scheduled arrival.

That figure is at its lowest since 2019-20, the year before the Covid pandemic, when 64.97 per cent of trains arrived on time.

ORR analysts said that opening new railway infrastructure during the year, as well as changes in the types of tickets people were buying, all contributed to last year’s journey figures.

The number of railway journeys has soared during Labour’s ongoing drive to end competition between private companies on the railways by placing all passenger trains under direct state control.

Journey numbers took off as nationalisations started with South Western Railway in May last year.

A “massive boost” to the year’s journey figures came from London’s Elizabeth Line, where passenger numbers “increased substantially”, the ORR said. Up to 800,000 journeys per day are made on the Underground line.

Even though passenger numbers are rising, Heidi Alexander, the Transport Secretary, has said she wants to cut taxpayer-funded subsidies for the railway industry.

On Wednesday, she told Parliament’s transport committee: “We probably do need to reduce public subsidy. Therefore, we do need to match the capacity to demand on services, and I think sensible operational decisions have to be made on those things.”

Her remarks were widely interpreted as a suggestion that less busy train services could be reduced or cut.

Rail subsidies were £11.9bn in 2024-25, whereas combined public-sector spending on roads was £12.8bn over the same period. There are more than 262,000 miles of roads in the UK, compared with about 10,000 miles of railways.

Referring to Thursday’s passenger journey figures, Darren Caplan, the chief executive of the Railway Industry Association, said: “The present DfT [Department for Transport] figures support the ORR’s report, showing current passenger numbers at around 110 per cent of pre-Covid levels, including the Elizabeth line, and 100 per cent if not included.

“We will clearly need more capacity in the future, and to plan for consistently growing numbers of rail users, both passengers and freight.”

Graham Richards, the ORR’s director of planning and performance, said: “As more people are using the railway, it’s important to have a relentless focus in ensuring trains are punctual and reliable as possible.”

data.title: The railway station taking longer to build than the Golden Gate Bridge
data.description:
A Labour authority has spent more time building a two-platform railway station than it took to construct the Golden Gate Bridge.

After more than four years and £28m, the station in south-west Leeds has surpassed the construction of the suspension bridge – and it remains unfinished.

Work first began on the White Rose railway station in March 2022 and was due to be completed in early 2024 at a cost of £26.5m.

But after the entire budget of the “basic” infrastructure project was spent in two years, the station was 70pc completed, according to a West Yorkshire combined authority (WYCA) assessment.

Workers downed tools in March 2024. A dispute between a key landowner and WYCA was left unresolved for nearly two years, until the deadlock was broken when £1.4m in additional funding was approved by the combined authority members in January.

However, the construction of the station has not yet restarted. A spokesman for WYCA confirmed that they “hoped” to see workers return next month.

Opponents of the project have described the delays to the site as “typical” of Britain’s inability to build anything.

The station – which is being built on an existing railway line between Morley and Cottingley – is located next to the White Rose Office Park and close to the White Rose Centre, a major two-storey shopping complex.

At around 150m, the station is just 5pc of the length of the 1.7 mile (2,737m) Golden Gate Bridge – and is located just 800m from an existing station, less than a 10-minute walk away.

Iain Clasper-Cotte, the managing director at FaberExposize UK, a specialist wide-format print business based in the industrial estate, said the delays had led to his company “struggling” to find staff because of the lack of an easy commute.

He said: “It’s crazy. I find it kind of impossible to understand how you decide to build a train station and then you don’t complete it. It’s like a folly.

“How can you possibly spend that much on two strips of concrete and a crappy stairway, which is all it looks like to me?

“I think it’s a great case study of how rubbish we are in the UK at building infrastructure – we can’t build anything.”

In October, Tracy Brabin, the Labour mayor of West Yorkshire, described the station as an “ambitious project”, which the authority “wouldn’t necessarily do in the same way again”.

The WYCA project has been delivered in partnership with Munroe K, the developer and owner of the White Rose Office Park, which contributed £4m. Spencer Rail Engineering has been the principal contractor for the construction.

Alan Lamb, a Conservative councillor of WYCA, told The Telegraph: “It’s really frustrating, and it really throws into question the capability and capacity of the WYCA to deliver big schemes when they’ve struggled to do just one railway station.

“Is it any wonder that the Government keeps delaying the mass transit scheme in West Yorkshire given that they haven’t managed to get a station over the line yet?”

He added: “It’s like you’ve run out of money building a house three quarters of the way through, but you’re so far in that you’ve no choice but to keep going.”

‘City deprived of accessible transport’

Mark Sewards, the Labour MP for Leeds South West and Morley, said: “I am incredibly frustrated at the delays, and people across Leeds and West Yorkshire are right to feel frustrated, too. The city and wider region has been deprived of accessible transport for far too long.

“Work on the White Rose Station must resume as quickly as possible, because better connectivity is absolutely central to improving our communities and supporting local growth.”

A WYCA spokesman said: “White Rose Rail Station is a major priority for West Yorkshire, and we are committed to working with our partners to see it open as soon as possible.

“We approved £1.4m earlier this year to get the site up and running again, all legacy issues have now been resolved and we hope to see activity back at the site next month.”

data.title: Dozens of former WH Smith stores to close in fresh blow for high street
data.description:
Dozens of former WH Smith stores across Britain are poised to close after the High Court signed off a sweeping restructuring of the business.

TG Jones, the new name for former WH Smith branches, has won court approval for its rescue plan, paving the way for 150 closures.

The High Court backed a deal on Wednesday to slash rents and close underperforming stores. Landlords will be given the option of either accepting rent cuts or terminating TG Jones’s leases, effectively shutting the stores.

Lawyers for TG Jones told the court on Monday that the “working assumption” is that around 300 of its 450 stores would stay open as its proceeds with its plan.

It marks a fresh blow for Britain’s already embattled high streets, which have suffered a succession of retail failures in recent years.

Some of the stores affected will have been staples of their high street for decades, given that WH Smith has traded for well over two centuries. Its first shop opened in 1792.

In his speech this week on returning to Parliament, Andy Burnham, the presumptive next prime minister, vowed to revitalise high streets and make them “the new symbol of British renaissance”.

The ruling clears the way for TG Jones to unlock a £15m loan from its private equity owner, Modella Capital, on top of £10m already advanced in April.

If the deal had been rejected, the retailer faced the imminent prospect of tumbling into administration under the weight of its debts. Lawyers acting for TG Jones told a judge that the company faced an £8m shortfall in tax, rent, supplier and payroll bills by the end of the week.

The retailer’s future had hung in the balance after Mr Justice Hildyard delayed his ruling on Monday, telling the High Court the matter was “too complex” to decide without further time to review transcripts of earlier meetings.

That delay left TG Jones scrambling to pay £14.1m of bills due on Tuesday, with a further £3.1m owed to suppliers on Friday.

TG Jones – the fictitious name given to WH Smith’s 450 former high street shops after Modella bought it for an initial £10m last year – employs around 4,700 staff.

Responding to the court approval, Alex Willson, chief executive of TG Jones, said: “The plan protects the substantial core of the store estate and makes TG Jones a stronger, more sustainable business.

“We are incredibly grateful to all the colleagues, partners and stakeholders who engaged constructively throughout the process, and to Modella Capital for its continued financial commitment.”

Multiple groups of landlords and creditors voted against the rescue plan but the retailer secured support from lenders and the bulk of its suppliers.

Because of the way the rescue plan is structured, a judge can sanction it even where some classes of creditors object – provided at least one class backs it and the court is satisfied the plan treats its opponents fairly.

Suppliers that TG Jones no longer intends to work with face having their debts written off entirely, while so-called non-core suppliers will recover less than half of what they are owed upfront, and may face a wait of more than three years to recover the remainder.

Toymakers, card companies and publishers are among those affected by the restructuring.

Modella has blamed TG Jones’s troubles on its separation from the WH Smith brand. WH Smith refused to license the brand when Modella bought the high street shops, forcing it to rebrand them. TG Jones has also been hit by a broader downturn in consumer confidence, its private equity owners argue.

Tom Smith KC told the court on Monday that the business was “highly distressed” after a “long-term sales decline”, pointing to high inflation, the shift to online shopping, rising labour costs and higher taxes.

However, landlords previously told The Telegraph they viewed the restructuring as a “stitch-up”, while industry sources described it as “the most aggressive retail restructuring plan the UK high street has ever seen”.

Creditors have been left unhappy by arrangements put in place by Modella to extract payments from the TG Jones business.

data.title: French and Chinese invest in British bike maker Brompton
data.description:
Folding bike-maker Brompton Bicycles has secured millions of pounds from Chinese and French investors as it grapples with a post-pandemic decline in cycling.

The British city-bike business confirmed it had sold a 10pc stake to French sports kit retailer Decathlon, while Shanghai-based investment group BA Capital had taken a 5pc share in the business, for a reported total investment of £18m.

The investment will allow Brompton to expand its sales globally, the company said, after losses widened last year and it was forced to pause plans for a new factory in Kent.

The company’s founder, Andrew Ritchie, a 79-year-old inventor, will remain Brompton’s biggest shareholder, while it insisted the brand would retain its “handcrafted-in-London heritage”. The deal will also allow Brompton’s early investors and employees to cash out some of their shares.

Brompton bikes were invented in 1975 by Mr Ritchie, who developed the distinctive three-part fold, which allows the bike to be transformed into a small locked package in seconds.

Its current range of bikes and e-bikes ranges from £999 foldable models to its £4,500 titanium-framed T Line bicycle.

The privately owned bike maker has produced more than 1.2 million bikes and has expanded its electric range, a key growth driver in Europe and North America.

The investment comes as the well-known folding bike brand struggles to deal with dramatically decreased levels of demand for bicycles following the highs of Covid.

Brompton’s sales dipped about 1pc last year to £121.5m, while its losses widened from £1m to £2m in the year to March. The business sold 78,530 bikes during the period, down 7.5pc year-on-year.

In January, it confirmed it had put plans for a £100m factory in Ashford, Kent, on hold, admitting it needed to see a “meaningful recovery in market conditions” before pursuing the project.

Will Butler-Adams, Brompton chief executive, said: “As demand for smarter and more sustainable urban transport continues to grow, we see a tremendous opportunity ahead.

“Decathlon Pulse and BA Capital bring complementary expertise, global networks and a shared belief in our mission.”

Franck Vigo, the chief executive of Decathlon Pulse, said the businesses shared “a long-term vision of sustainable urban mobility”.

New investor BA Capital, which is best known for backing the maker of viral soft toys known as Labubu dolls, said its investment would help Brompton expand its “exceptional cycling experiences” to buyers in China.

Michael Zhang, a managing partner at BA Capital, claimed “experience-driven consumption” in China was boosting interest in cycling and “outdoor lifestyles” among Chinese consumers.

TG Jones, the new name for former WH Smith branches, has won court approval for its rescue plan, paving the way for 150 closures.

The High Court backed a deal on Wednesday to slash rents and close underperforming stores. Landlords will be given the option of either accepting rent cuts or terminating TG Jones’s leases, effectively shutting the stores.

Lawyers for TG Jones told the court on Monday that the “working assumption” is that around 300 of its 450 stores would stay open as its proceeds with its plan.

It marks a fresh blow for Britain’s already embattled high streets, which have suffered a succession of retail failures in recent years.

Some of the stores affected will have been staples of their high street for decades, given that WH Smith has traded for well over two centuries. Its first shop opened in 1792.

In his speech this week on returning to Parliament, Andy Burnham, the presumptive next prime minister, vowed to revitalise high streets and make them “the new symbol of British renaissance”.

The ruling clears the way for TG Jones to unlock a £15m loan from its private equity owner, Modella Capital, on top of £10m already advanced in April.

If the deal had been rejected, the retailer faced the imminent prospect of tumbling into administration under the weight of its debts. Lawyers acting for TG Jones told a judge that the company faced an £8m shortfall in tax, rent, supplier and payroll bills by the end of the week.

The retailer’s future had hung in the balance after Mr Justice Hildyard delayed his ruling on Monday, telling the High Court the matter was “too complex” to decide without further time to review transcripts of earlier meetings.

That delay left TG Jones scrambling to pay £14.1m of bills due on Tuesday, with a further £3.1m owed to suppliers on Friday.

TG Jones – the fictitious name given to WH Smith’s 450 former high street shops after Modella bought it for an initial £10m last year – employs around 4,700 staff.

Responding to the court approval, Alex Willson, chief executive of TG Jones, said: “The plan protects the substantial core of the store estate and makes TG Jones a stronger, more sustainable business.

“We are incredibly grateful to all the colleagues, partners and stakeholders who engaged constructively throughout the process, and to Modella Capital for its continued financial commitment.”

Multiple groups of landlords and creditors voted against the rescue plan but the retailer secured support from lenders and the bulk of its suppliers.

Because of the way the rescue plan is structured, a judge can sanction it even where some classes of creditors object – provided at least one class backs it and the court is satisfied the plan treats its opponents fairly.

Suppliers that TG Jones no longer intends to work with face having their debts written off entirely, while so-called non-core suppliers will recover less than half of what they are owed upfront, and may face a wait of more than three years to recover the remainder.

Toymakers, card companies and publishers are among those affected by the restructuring.

Modella has blamed TG Jones’s troubles on its separation from the WH Smith brand. WH Smith refused to license the brand when Modella bought the high street shops, forcing it to rebrand them. TG Jones has also been hit by a broader downturn in consumer confidence, its private equity owners argue.

Tom Smith KC told the court on Monday that the business was “highly distressed” after a “long-term sales decline”, pointing to high inflation, the shift to online shopping, rising labour costs and higher taxes.

However, landlords previously told The Telegraph they viewed the restructuring as a “stitch-up”, while industry sources described it as “the most aggressive retail restructuring plan the UK high street has ever seen”.

Creditors have been left unhappy by arrangements put in place by Modella to extract payments from the TG Jones business.

data.title: Third of new London homes deemed unsellable
data.description:
One third of new homes in London failed to sell to private buyers last year, laying bare the scale of the housebuilding crisis about to be inherited by Andy Burnham.

New figures show developers gave up on selling 34pc of new homes in London last year after they failed to find a purchaser, according to CBRE analysis of Molior data. This is up from 25pc in 2025.

Rather than being sold to private buyers as planned, the homes were instead offloaded to investment funds or housing associations.

The figures highlight the growing strain on London’s housing market, as a dearth of sales makes building new homes increasingly unviable for developers.

The lack of activity casts fresh doubt on Labour’s pledge to build 1.5 million homes by 2029.

Mr Burnham announced last month that he wanted to deliver “the biggest programme of council house-building since the Second World War”, indicating where he would look to spend if, as expected, he replaces Sir Keir Starmer as prime minister.

He has already suggested that he could fund such a policy by redirecting cash in the £39bn affordable housing programme to social-rent homes.

However, he has given little indication of how he might stimulate private housebuilding, which is grappling with an affordability crisis that is most acute in the capital.

Tim Craine, the founder of Molior, said: “What is going on is there are no buyers of new homes in London.

“People are not buying new homes in London in sufficient quantities. A block of flats is 100 or 150 new homes. There is no point having one or two buyers, you need to clear the shelves.

“You don’t want to get to completion and have 120 flats unsold and be haemorrhaging interest to your debt holders.”

Soaring building costs and squeezed affordability meant that the number of new homes built in England last year fell to a decade-low. This has led to some analysts warning that Labour will not reach even half of its 1.5 million homes target.

In London, the number of new homes securing planning consent has recently plunged to 20,000 – not even a quarter of the capital’s target to build 88,000 homes per year.

Developers are slashing their pipelines as the market for new homes shrinks dramatically.

Joe Marshall, the chief investment and development officer at Sovereign Network Group, one of the largest housing associations in the country, said the company had purchased 382 new-build flats from UK developers in the last financial year.

That is solely because the builders could not find private buyers to purchase them.

Some of these were in planning or mid-construction, while others were already completed and sat empty.

Mr Marshall said: “When a developer is struggling to sell, they will consider bulk sales to change the tenure, whether that’s approaching someone who will deliver the homes as private rented or someone like us who, as a housing association, can convert the tenure from market sale to either shared ownership or social or affordable rent.”

The appetite among developers to do this has increased in the past year as the sales market has worsened, Mr Marshall said.

“Obviously when we purchase these homes midway through construction, it won’t be for the full market value. The developer will have to take a discount,” he added.

Developers are being forced down this route because all of their traditional buyers are facing a wave of financial challenges.

First-time buyers are struggling with high mortgage rates and the end of the Help to Buy scheme, which until 2023 allowed people in London to purchase new-build properties with only a 5pc deposit and a 40pc government-backed equity loan.

Outside London, this was 20pc.

Overseas buyers, who were traditionally major players in the UK housing market, have also been deterred by rising stamp duty rates, falling prices and tighter anti-money-laundering checks.

Meanwhile, there has been an exodus of buy-to-let investors after they have been hammered by a decade-long crackdown of higher taxes and red tape.

Mr Craine said: “They have been turned from customers into competitors.”

This has become a major factor in parts of the capital that have had a heavy concentration of new-build development in recent decades, such as the Isle of Dogs peninsula in east London, which is home to Canary Wharf.

Mr Craine said: “I did a search there 10 days ago and there were 844 buy-to-let investors trying to sell flats that they had bought between five or 15 years ago.”

Bulk-buying flats is a viable option for housing associations not only because they get discounts but because they can access grant funding from the Greater London Authority and Homes England to convert the flats into affordable tenures.

Both housing associations and build-to-rent operators, who can convert blocks into managed rental flats, are looking for properties that will deliver them long-term rental income over decades rather than the one-off sales.

data.title: Best regular savings accounts for 2026: Today’s latest rates
data.description:
Regular savings accounts are set up on the basis that you’ll be contributing little and often. Some may even require you to commit to paying in a certain amount of money each month – commonly between £10 and £500.

Not only are these accounts good for getting into the habit of saving, but you’ll usually be given a higher interest rate for your efforts.

However, they can come with strict terms. Some accounts may punish missed monthly payments with a lower rate of interest, while others won’t allow any withdrawals for a fixed-term. Many accounts also only last for a limited time, so it’s important to check the terms of each account before you sign up.

In this guide, Telegraph money will cover:

  • How we determine the best rates
  • The best regular savings accounts for July
  • Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a regular savings account
  • Are regular savings accounts worth it?
  • Regular savings FAQs

How we determine the best rates

Telegraph Money’s Best Buy tables show the best savings rates widely available in the market. This means certain accounts are excluded, including those that require minimum deposits of more than £25,000 or are available only to local or existing customers.

The data in these tables is provided by Savings Data Limited and is compiled using automated tracker tools and updates from savings providers. Savings Data Limited then manually checks the information and enters it into a database that feeds the live tables, which update daily.

The savings accounts shown are protected by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS). The information in this article is intended for information purposes and should not be taken as endorsement or advice.

The best regular savings accounts for July

Use the table below to discover the best regular savings accounts for you.

For other savings options that allow you to access your money, see our guides to the best easy-access savings accounts, along with the best notice accounts and best current accounts.

You might be able to find higher rates by locking your cash away – don’t miss our guide to the best fixed-rate bonds.

Plus, don’t miss our guide to the best cash Isas, which shows the top options for easy-access, notice, regular and fixed-rates – where your savings interest is all tax-free.

Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a regular savings account

There are a few things you should look out for when choosing a regular savings account:

  • High headline interest rates: These can look good initially, but given the restrictions on how much you can deposit you may only earn a relatively small amount of interest overall.
  • Restrictive deposits: You might not be able to save as much as you’d like.
  • Requirements to save every month: Missed deposits may mean you’ll lose out on interest.
  • Withdrawal restrictions: Your money may be locked up for a specified term.

Mark Hicks, of investment and savings platform Hargreaves Lansdown, said: “Regular savers can have a very catchy headline rate. But quite often, the maximum amount of money you can deposit is low. Banks may attract you in with a high interest rate and then you realise you can only save £5,000.”

However, if they can be beneficial if you want to get into the savings habit. “They are very good if you want to deposit small amounts of money and if you’re new to saving,” he added.

Caitlyn Eastell, of Moneyfactscompare.co.uk, agreed: “Regular savings accounts are an ideal choice for those wanting to receive a higher rate of interest or wish to develop and maintain a regular savings habit.

“However, many regular savings accounts don’t give much flexibility on the amount that can be deposited each month and often have minimum or maximum monthly payments.”

Are regular savings accounts worth it?

This really depends on the kind of saver you are, and what you want from an account. Regular savings accounts can be useful if you think you can reliably put away some money every month – which you might not be able to access until the account matures.

If this suits you, you can earn a relatively high rate of interest on what you save, and you’ll hopefully end up with a healthy sum if you stick to your savings habit.

On the other hand, depending on your disposable income, some might find the amounts you’re allowed to save restrictive – and they’re also no good if you’re looking for a home for a lump sum.

What’s more, while the headline interest rates might sound high, you might be surprised to see the interest you earn amounting to fairly little, as deposit restrictions will keep your balance low.

Regular savings FAQs

Yes, you can open more than one account – though some providers might have their own restrictions. Regular savers are for making smaller payments, so there is nothing stopping you from opening several to earn more interest on your money.

Some accounts will have a minimum number of payments you need to make each year – often 10 or 11. Some will require you to pay in a certain amount every month.

Yes, money held in regular savings accounts is protected as long as your account is with a UK-regulated bank or building society, the money is protected under the Financial Services Compensation Scheme up to £120,000 per institution.

Yes, you can open a regular savings account for your children. Check our guide to the best children’s savings accounts to see the latest rates.

A regular savings account can help develop good saving habits. If you are only able to put a small amount of money away each month and would prefer a short-term arrangement, then it might be the best situation for you.

Pros:

  • These accounts can help you get into the savings habit, which can benefit you for the rest of your life.
  • A regular savings account gives you considerably better interest rates than standard accounts, rewarding you for making frequent savings over a year or so.
  • They are inclusive – you’re usually limited to saving relatively small amounts, so those with less to save can still benefit.

Cons:

  • There are generally rigid terms and conditions. The amount you can save is often capped and your interest rate might be reduced if you don’t save every month or if you need to make a withdrawal. Many accounts don’t allow early withdrawals whatsoever.
  • You usually need to have another product with the same bank – usually a current account.
  • The rate sometimes lasts for a limited time, often a year, meaning your money is moved to an account with lower interest rates at the end of the term.
  • Some accounts have variable interest rates – meaning the provider can change the rates whenever they want – so it’s important to keep an eye on messages from the provider and switch, if the rates drop too low.

data.title: SNP accused of cutting Scottish islanders off from mainland
data.description:
Islanders will find it far more difficult to visit the mainland after the Scottish Government refused to fund flights.

The SNP has been accused of disregarding the health and wellbeing of tens of thousands of islanders after the Scottish Government refused to provide £1.3m in vital funding to maintain lifeline flights from Shetland, Orkney and Lewis.

Morning services from the islands to Inverness are to end in July following the funding cut, which residents say will make it impossible to fly to the city and back in a day and harder to attend vital hospital appointments.

Eye patients deterred from attending clinics for cataract operations risk going blind as a result of the cuts, health officials warned the Scottish Parliament’s transport committee.

Gordon Jamieson, the chief executive of NHS Western Isles, said spending on extra patient travel and overnight accommodation could cost £1m a year, rendering the SNP’s stance a false economy.

Maggie Sandison, who heads Shetland Isles council, said islanders were “being treated as though we are not contributing to Scotland and those lifeline links are not being valued”.

Loganair, which operates the routes, said services had been unprofitable for five years and that it could not be expected to carry on subsidising them indefinitely.

The airline opened discussions with the Scottish Government-owned Highlands and Islands Airports Limited (HIAL) in December, but announced in June that the flights would be scrapped from July 19 after ministers rejected proposals to make them viable.

Luke Farajallah, the chief executive of Loganair, said losses because of limited demand and high airport fees imposed by HIAL had widened as a result of surging fuel costs.

Mr Farajallah said: “This has been one of the hardest decisions we have had to make, set against one of the most difficult trading periods the airline industry has seen.

“We understand how important these connections are for healthcare, work, education, and family life. Loganair will always play its part in providing air services where they are most needed in Scotland, but we cannot do this alone.”

Loganair’s proposals to make the routes financially sustainable included a reduction in fees and a change to timetables, as well as direct funding.

The carrier questioned the SNP’s refusal to support the routes when ministers announced an £850m procurement programme for ferries to Shetland and Orkney in June.

Loganair plans to end its daily morning flights between Stornoway on Lewis and Inverness, leaving only afternoon services that necessitate an overnight stay. It will also reduce daily operations from Sumburgh on Shetland and Kirkwall on Orkney to weekends only.

While islanders will still have the option of flights to Glasgow, as well as several other cities from the Northern Isles, residents at a meeting with Loganair in Stornoway said they were tied by the NHS to the hospital in Inverness.

The meeting also heard that the cheapest one-way fares to Inverness, priced at £130, were often unavailable because of block bookings by workers at new windfarms.

Graham Simpson, Reform UK’s Scottish transport spokesman, said there would be “huge issues for islanders if they can’t get the medical care they need. These are lifeline routes and we need to find some kind of solution”.

Loganair pledged to restore the flights from October, when demand increases as winter weather makes ferry crossings unreliable, if the Scottish Government reviews its position and offers subsidies for routes that provide a vital public service.

Transport Scotland said meetings continue to seek a “commercial solution” for the routes.

A spokesman said: “The Scottish Government wants to see HIAL and Loganair work collaboratively to ensure that the services can be sustained, recognising their value to local communities and business.”

data.title: South East Water under fire after taps run dry during heatwave
data.description:
South East Water is facing renewed scrutiny from MPs after taps ran dry during the May bank holiday heatwave.

More than a thousand customers across Kent and East Sussex were left without water during the weekend after a series of infrastructure failures.

The crisis has prompted fresh criticism of South East Water just weeks after Dave Hinton, its former chief executive, resigned because of his response to a string of recent supply issues.

Chris Train, the company’s former chairman, also resigned earlier this month.

‘Getting the basics right’

Rosie Duffield, the MP for Canterbury, said: “Despite the recent dramatic change of management at South East Water, we have yet to see any change in acceptable levels of service or visible leadership. Yet again my constituents are left without water in a heatwave, the most basic necessity.

“We need to see proper investment, effective management and urgent intervention by Ofwat and the Government to ensure these incidents stop happening on a regular basis.”

Alistair Carmichael, the chairman of the Commons environment committee, said: “Yet again it is the long-suffering customers of South East Water who bear the consequences of the company’s failure.

“The select committee highlighted the lack of resilience in South East Water and sadly this latest outage provides further proof of that.”

Mr Carmichael, who spearheaded calls for Mr Hinton and Mr Train to resign, added: “They need to focus on getting the basics right, being honest with their customers and to stop always looking to blame some external problem.”

The crisis began on Saturday when a pumping station in Charing, at the foot of the North Downs in Kent, suffered an outage that triggered a significant reduction in water availability.

It hit during the hottest May weekend on record in England as South East Water customers filling up paddling pools and watering their gardens put severe demand on depleted supplies.

The disruption continued for three days, with residents in the Kent village of Challock still forced to rely on a bottled water station on Monday.

South East Water struck a casual tone on social media on Saturday as it urged customers to use water sparingly.

The water provider said: “Sun’s out. We know the drill: nobody wants a water company telling them to save water when there’s leaks. Fair point.

“Our reservoirs are healthy. There is no shortage of water. But if we all use hoses at Saturday lunchtime, the water pressure drops and homes at the top of the hill can run dry.

“Spacing out heavy water tasks over the week means everyone on your street keeps their water pressure.”

South East Water has come under scrutiny because of a string of shortages that left homes without water for weeks.

In December, around 24,000 customers in the Tunbridge Wells area went without water for a fortnight and were forced to rely on collecting water bottles from stations.

Mr Hinton rated his company’s response to the incident as an “eight out of 10”. However, the environment committee said it had no confidence in the utility’s leadership and accused bosses of a “culture of unaccountability”.

South East Water was contacted for comment.

data.title: Despite the gloom, Britain can flourish. Here’s how
data.description:
Looking around Britain today, surely only a fool would be an optimist. The nation slithers towards the abyss. Every Budget announces new punishment beatings for business. The state makes a sarcastic mockery of working hard and being honest.

The utilitarian law of globalisation, that price is the only thing that matters, no longer exerts such a vice-like grip over the fate of nations. For decades we have been told that for a fractional base point of additional GDP growth we should surrender strong communities, open the borders and abandon industry.

But the world has changed since the collapse of confidence in globalisation – and in many ways, it’s changed to our advantage. This is a conversation the UK has been postponing. In this context, I shall offer five reasons why Britain can flourish.

Firstly, we have natural energy resources that Continental Europe envies. 

It is obscene to impose the highest industrial energy prices in Europe when we have centuries of exploitable natural gas reserves, plus coal. Although electricity blackouts are probably now baked into the system, we can ensure they don’t keep recurring into the 2030s. 

I have commended the tiny Social Democratic Party’s plan of an emergency “grid stabilisation” of 60GW of new gas and coal, allowing a Messmer-scale building programme of dozens of new gigawatt reactors to proceed. There is no need to race to nuclear, skimping on safety as some of the ersatz “pro-growth” groups insist. Not when reactors last 70 years. An abundance of cheap energy must be our top priority.

Our second important natural resource is minerals. We have copper and gold in Northern Ireland, lithium and tin in Cornwall, and cobalt and rare earths in Scotland. 

There may be even more: as I reported a year ago, we haven’t conducted a national geophysical survey since the early 1960s. Whitehall is waking up to this with some urgency.

Now the third piece of the puzzle can fall into place: by reviving our refining and processing industries. 

British pragmatism thrives in devising new industrial processes. Our petrochemicals sector was once a world-beater, and our excellent chemistry and chemical engineering departments at universities provide the brains. Alas, thanks to high energy prices and policy indifference, this is a sector on its knees.

“If you don’t have a chemicals industry... then you can’t have pharmaceutical production, aerospace production or car production,” Grant Pearson, chairman of Teesside ethanol company Ensus, told The Telegraph recently.

Fourth, British business continues to command respect abroad. 

When I mention this, some readers assume I am being sentimental or ironic. But I hear it affirmed every week. Our reputation for fair play has endured. 

It is astonishing that successive prime ministers and chancellors, from George Osborne to Rachel Reeves, have looked up to the hucksters of Silicon Valley and American venture capital as our model for how to conduct business. Unlike them, we leave something on the table in a deal. Global customers know this, and like it.

Finally, we can capitalise on the engineering and manufacturing talent that creates world-class technology. 

Dyson and Renishaw and Martin-Baker are world beaters in consumer markets, medical instruments and aviation, respectively. “The Germans fear our engineers,” Marcus Gibson, whose research company monitors the health of our SMEs, likes to say.

Writing on the Dyson company blog recently, Sir James Dyson pointed out that nine out of 10 private sector companies are family firms, and employ more than half of all private sector workers. It shows how Labour’s war on family businesses and private property owners is so spiteful. At such times Labour looks much more like a global lobbying firm for hedge funds than a political operation acting in the British national interest.

These five parts are interlocking gears in a modern industrial society; each benefits from the other. For example, a supply of low-cost novel plastics encourages entrepreneurs to innovate and makes prototyping easier.

An industrial revival is not uncharted territory. None of this requires a Stalinist top-down plan or autarky. Government interventions could be limited to giving refining, processing, manufacturing and engineering companies a 10-year amnesty period from tax. 

Other state measures that could help include getting finance to co-operate, which forces successful companies to sell out to grow; and protecting inheritance and strengthening intellectual property enforcement. Other than that, the state should get out of the way and let markets do their thing.

However, this does require something almost unthinkable today: a shift away from the utilitarian logic of globalisation which the UK has embedded. 

This is what I call “performative laissez-faire”. It is performative in the same way that making international climate change pledges is performative: no one else really cares how virtuous we are.

The overarching utilitarian creed is that the consumer must get the cheapest goods, at the lowest price, no matter what the consequences may be for national security or communities. China boomed, while we hollowed ourselves out. 

Surely this ideology is now broken on the rocks. Some will sneer that reviving industry means “picking winners”, but this is nonsense: the phrase should hold no fear for us anymore. Giving industrial sectors a break from punishing taxes would make a refreshing change from the Government consistently picking losers.

The UK has already tried being the management consultancy to the world. Now it’s time to make stuff again.

data.title: Currys’ turnaround king tasked with reviving tired Boots ahead of flotation
data.description:
When Currys announced that Alex Baldock, its chief executive, was stepping down earlier this year, the response from investors was immediate.

Shares in the electronics chain fell sharply – wiping almost 11pc off its valuation – as markets digested the departure of the man widely credited with engineering the company’s quietly successful turnaround.

Now Baldock appears poised for an even bigger challenge, tasked with reviving a 177-year-old brand that has seen better days. On Wednesday, it emerged that the outgoing Currys chief was being lined up to take the reins at Boots.

The company confirmed his appointment on Thursday, setting the stage for Baldock to try to convince investors that the British chemist could be worth as much as £7bn.

Baldock should be well-suited to the role. He has spent the past seven years proving that legacy retailers can still thrive if they modernise quickly enough.

At Currys, that meant slimming down the store estate, cutting costs, boosting online sales and emphasising its “omnichannel” offering of services, repairs, credit and subscriptions.

Adam Tomlinson, a retail analyst at Berenberg, says that when Baldock arrived at Currys in 2018, “the business was in a very tough place”.

“A major focus initially was restructuring the mobile division, which had become a significant drag on the wider group. He completely realigned it in terms of right-sizing both the store base and the cost base, and turned it from being a problem area into a positive contributor.”

Tomlinson says Baldock’s broader strategy has been to transform Currys from a “product-first retailer into an integrated services business”, encouraging customer loyalty through credit offers, delivery and installation, warranties, repairs and trade-ins.

“What is often overlooked is how much focus he placed on staff and colleagues,” he adds. “He has a mantra that customer experience can never exceed colleague experience.”

Baldock will now have to repeat that success at Boots – a brand which is desperately in need of a turnaround.

Since being folded into Walgreens Boots Alliance in 2014, Boots has endured closures, restructurings and repeated attempts to move away from its old-fashioned chemist image.

Its owners have repeatedly signalled that they would be keen to offload the British business. Walgreens launched a sale process for Boots in 2022.

However, it later scrapped the plans after failing to secure a deal at the valuation it wanted in the face of difficult market conditions and rising borrowing costs. Since then, efforts have been focused on honing the chain, including pushing through further store closures and cost reductions.

Speculation over a sell-off of the British arm was revived last year when private equity firm Sycamore Partners swooped to buy Walgreens Boots Alliance in a $10bn (£7.4bn) deal.

Shortly after the deal was completed, Sycamore split Walgreens into five standalone entities – one of which was Boots. It was seen as the first major step towards ultimately returning the British chemist to the UK stock market.

Baldock is believed by many to have been brought on to get Boots over the line for such a London listing – something insiders claim was “always the plan”. Its owners are looking for a valuation as high as £7bn. Still, some remain unconvinced that any listing would be imminent.

A senior retail industry source says his appointment is the same “as with any private equity-owned business”. “The job is to create as much value as possible for the owners,” the source says.

Given the current state of the London market, they say it is more likely that Baldock has simply been brought in to put Boots on a stronger footing. “I don’t think it’s right that he’s been brought in for an IPO.”

They say Boots’ health and beauty division is now “by far the most important part of the business”, reflecting the growing view inside retail that the future of Boots lies less in pharmacy and more in cosmetics, skincare and wellness.

That shift is already visible inside many of Boots’ larger stores, where beauty halls increasingly dominate the shop floor while traditional pharmacy space shrinks.

Boots has revamped more than 100 of its stores and updated beauty halls in more than 180 sites. It argues that this has helped it to record 20 consecutive quarters of market-share growth.

Analysts expect Baldock to continue pushing the business in the same direction because beauty products offer significantly stronger margins than dispensing medicines or selling low-cost healthcare essentials.

Jonathan De Mello, of JDM Retail, says Boots is “looking at devoting more floor space to beauty rather than distress pharmaceutical purchasing. The margins on beauty products are significantly better”.

At the same time, Boots still faces the challenge of balancing investment in physical stores with the need to boost online sales.

For all the investment Boots has poured into flagship stores and digital infrastructure, analysts argue the business still lags behind rivals on both fronts.

“The Advantage Card is strong, but the website still needs work,” says De Mello.

Former executives claim there are also still swathes of its sprawling estate which are in urgent need of investment. Renovating all of these old stores would be painfully expensive.

Clive Black, another retail analyst, agrees the chain’s estate remains uneven despite years of investment.

“There are some flagship stores where they have clearly invested heavily in beauty, but there are also an awful lot of stores around the country that look pretty tired,” says Black.

If Baldock wants to take Boots public – be it later this year or further down the line – he will have to convince investors that Boots can be a growth business rather than simply a reliable cash generator.

Black says he suspects that “Boots may end up being seen more as an income story than a pure growth story”.

Baldock will also inherit questions over Boots’ international operations.

While the retailer’s presence outside the UK may have formed part of the attraction for him personally – having steered Currys for years in both the UK and overseas – analysts suggest some international businesses could ultimately be sold off as management focuses more tightly on the chain’s strongest markets.

De Mello says Boots’ operations in places such as Germany and Mexico are underperforming. He argues the business would be better concentrating on the UK and Ireland, which he describes as the company’s “engine room for growth”.

At Currys, Baldock managed to convince the markets that physical stores still had value in the Amazon era, provided they worked seamlessly alongside a strong online offer. Between 2024 and 2026, he oversaw a tripling in the retailer’s share price.

Just last week, Baldock told The Times that he loved his role at Currys. His next move would have to be “something properly exciting to take me away from Currys”. With Boots, it seems that Baldock has found that. Now, he said, marked the start of “an exciting new chapter for Boots”.

data.title: Housing development attacked by Burnham ally heading for High Court
data.description:
The developer behind a blocked housing project opposed by one of Andy Burnham’s key economic advisers is to take its battle to the High Court.

Berkeley Homes has launched a judicial review of the decision in May by the local Labour council to prevent the construction of 867 homes on the site of a run-down shopping centre in Peckham, south London.

The FTSE 100 developer said the planner’s decision to block the redevelopment of the Aylesham Centre had undermined Labour’s housebuilding agenda.

The project faced a concerted campaign against it by local groups, including Aylesham Community Action, which argued redeveloping the shopping centre would have meant “gentrification [on] steroids”.

The campaign was backed by Miatta Fahnbulleh, the local Labour MP, who praised the planner’s decision as a “big win” for the areas. She is now one of the key figures shaping Mr Burnham’s economic agenda as he races to create a programme for power.

Rob Perrins, the executive chairman of Berkeley, said on Thursday that the decision to block the scheme would “undermine the Government’s pro-homebuilding agenda unless it is swiftly quashed”.

“This decision has already shaken investor confidence and it is a great shame that we now have to go through the courts to have it overturned,” he said.

Morrisons, which is supporting Berkeley on its judicial review, said: “The cost of the recent decision is not just the loss of a new town centre for Peckham, but the sacrifice of much-needed homes, including a number of affordable homes for local families.”

The judicial review is likely to put pressure on Ms Fahnbulleh to defend her position against the housebuilder’s proposal.

She is set to become a key part of Mr Burnham’s new government, advising him on economic policies, having campaigned for the former mayor of Manchester during his victory in the Makerfield by-election.

After the Aylesham scheme was rejected, she said: “We need new homes and investment – but development must work for local people, protect Peckham’s character and deliver genuine long-term benefit, not just luxury flats.”

Berkeley’s project was set to have replaced the 1980s Aylesham Centre with more than a dozen tower blocks running up to 20 storeys high, served by a new Morrisons supermarket.

The planning inspector refused the project because it would “not tie in successfully with surrounding historic buildings”, blocking the scheme on heritage grounds.

The Government is falling significantly short of its housebuilding targets both nationally and in London. Labour has made housebuilding a key plank of its policy agenda since taking power, pledging to construct 1.5 million new homes by 2029.

To reach its target, the Government needs to build 300,000 new homes per year, but just more than 150,000 a year will be built between now and March 2028, down from 189,000 last year, according to Savills.

London, which has a target to build 88,000 a year, is also vastly behind schedule. Molior data show that construction began on just 5,547 homes in the capital last year.

The judicial review could also serve as an early test case for Mr Burnham’s housing policies, which potentially include an annual land value tax to replace stamp duty and council tax.

Under his leadership as Manchester mayor, Mr Burnham’s office also oversaw a major housebuilding initiative that resulted in hundreds of luxury flats being built in skyscrapers across the city.

However, it was found that a taxpayer-based fund that he presided over produced almost no affordable homes, despite local targets.

Mr Burnham has defended his administration’s decisions, saying that proceeds from the loans will help pay for other housing initiatives such as social and affordable homes outside the city centre.

data.title: What remains unanswered about the Bedford railway crash
data.description:
The number of apparent failures that led to the train crash near Bedford on Friday has left experts baffled.

The collision, in which the driver of the Luton Airport Express service died and at least 90 people were injured, was the first fatal accident involving more than one train in more than 25 years on Britain’s railways, which are regarded as among the safest in the world.

On Friday evening, the late-running 3.50pm East Midlands Railway (EMR) service had stopped just past Bedford on Friday evening when the 4.40pm Luton Airport Express train rammed into it from behind.

It will now be up to a team of investigators to answer the questions over how the accident occurred.

All modern passenger trains have several safety systems fitted and are built into the signalling system as well. Specialists from the Rail Accident Investigation Branch (RAIB) pore over those systems to figure out whether they worked correctly.

Describing himself as “mystified”, Phil Haigh, a former editor of RAIL magazine, said the two causes of the accident could have been “a fault with the signalling system” or if “the driver passed a red signal”.

Investigators will examine black boxes on both trains and at the signalling centre in charge of that stretch of tracks, as well as interviewing witnesses and examining maintenance records.

Mr Haigh said that, historically, RAIB issued an initial update “within a few days” but full reports typically took about a year to complete because of the level of detail within them.

Tony Miles, a rail expert, told Sky News that damage to the trains involved in the collision looked “fairly minimal”.

He added: “Either the train in the rear was already slowing down or they were both going fairly slowly, and something’s happened, but it’s not a high-speed crash, certainly from the damage.”

On the stretch of the Midland Main Line that passes through Bedford, there are four tracks. These are called the Up Fast and Up Slow in the London direction, while the Down Fast and Down Slow lead away from the capital. The accident took place on the Up Fast line, more than two miles south of Bedford.

A warning system failure

Friday’s events began when East Midlands Railway’s 3.50pm Nottingham to London St Pancras service departed three minutes late. During the previous service from London, it had arrived at Nottingham 11 minutes behind schedule.

The Nottingham service, a brand new Aurora-class 810 train with a top speed of 125mph, was put into passenger use in April.

The train picked up more delays en route, running 19 minutes late when it reached Kettering at 4.54pm. There, signallers routed it onto the Up Fast line for a non-stop run into London.

However, after passing Bedford, the Nottingham train stopped. Several insiders who have seen official logs of the event told The Telegraph that the train had suffered a fault with its Automatic Warning System (AWS) installation.

At 5.11pm, the service came to a halt at signal WH152, about two-and-a-half miles south of Bedford at a point with a farmer’s field on one side and an asphalt plant on the other. There, the driver called controllers for technical help with the AWS fault.

AWS installations warn train drivers that the next signal they are approaching is either yellow (meaning “caution, prepare to stop at the next signal”) or red by sounding a warning horn and displaying a bright yellow “sunflower” indication on a gauge in the cab. If the signal is green, AWS sounds a bell and the sunflower disappears.

RAIB investigators will look at whether the AWS failure was linked to the signalling system.

Mr Haigh explained that one possible fault with AWS is that it can wrongly show the indication for a green signal when the signal is actually yellow or red. This can either be a fault with on-board equipment or with track-side signalling gear.

A switch to the fast line before crash

About 45 minutes after the delayed Nottingham train set off, a Luton Airport Express-branded EMR train pulled out of Corby.

This electrically powered 4.40pm service to London was on time. Its journey was uneventful until it switched from the Up Slow to the Up Fast line after a planned stop at Wellingborough.

Ten minutes later, just before arriving at Bedford, the train switched back to the Up Slow.

Official systems recorded that the express then pulled out of Bedford’s Platform 1 about 30 seconds before its scheduled departure time of 5.10½pm.

From Bedford, signallers routed the Luton Airport Express train across Bedford South Junction. However, instead of staying on the Up Slow line leading south from Platform 1, the train travelled across the junction and back onto the Up Fast line.

This manoeuvre placed the express directly behind the Nottingham train instead of on the parallel London-bound track.

RAIB investigators will examine what happened here. A signal called WH356 controls access to Bedford South Junction from the Up Slow line. If it is red, trains must stop and wait until it shows either yellow or green to let them proceed. A set of indicator lights on top tells drivers whether they are continuing straight ahead or crossing the junction onto a different line.

The speed limit across Bedford South Junction is 50mph – compared with 90mph on the Up Slow and 125mph on the Up Fast – for most passenger trains.

Signals split railway tracks into sections. Under rail rules, there must always be an empty section guarded by a red signal in between trains. This keeps a safe gap and ensures that if a signal is accidentally passed at red, there is enough space to stop before a collision. Computer systems controlling the signals are programmed so they do not let trains get too close together.

If there were a malfunction in the Bedford South Junction signalling system, however, it could have been the case that WH356 showed a yellow or green light when it should not have. This could have brought the express dangerously close to the Nottingham train by eroding that safe gap.

Lack of automatic braking system

Once on the Up Fast line after Bedford South Junction, trains pass two signals in sequence. These are known as WH154 and WH152. The Nottingham train had stopped at signal WH152 where its driver was calling for help because of the AWS fault.

Under normal conditions, WH154 – the signal that was behind the Nottingham train – should have been red to ensure that the safe gap was maintained.

Investigators will check black-box recorders at West Hampstead signalling centre, in London, to see whether WH154 was at red or not before the Luton Airport Express passed it.

They will also check whether a key safety system that could have automatically stopped the train was present and working.

Some signals are fitted with safety equipment called Train Protection Warning System (TPWS).

Mr Haigh said: “I’d be very surprised if the signal wasn’t equipped with TPWS. That would automatically put the brakes on if the driver went past it. That’ll be something the investigators will be looking very closely at.”

Satellite imagery reviewed by The Telegraph suggests that TPWS may not be fitted to the key signals involved in Friday’s accident.

While some nearby signals appear to have items mounted in front of them on the tracks – suggesting the presence of a TPWS “grid” to trigger the automatic braking signal – those items do not seem to be present at signals WH154 and WH152 in imagery captured earlier this year.

Mark Budden, Network Rail’s East Midlands route director, said in a statement: “We’re deeply saddened that one of our railway colleagues has tragically died as a result of the collision near Bedford.

“Our thoughts are with their family, friends and colleagues at this incredibly difficult time, and with all those who have sadly been injured or affected.”
