data.title: Retail workers ‘can’t find enough hours’ after Reeves tax raid
data.description:
Hundreds of thousands of retail and hospitality workers are struggling to find enough work as businesses freeze hiring in the wake of Rachel Reeves’s tax raid.

More than 300,000 staff in the sectors, who often work flexible shifts, now find themselves “underemployed”. This means they want to take on more hours but cannot secure the work, according to research carried out by Retail Economics and Legion Technologies.

Labour’s decision to launch a raid on employers’ National Insurance contributions (NICs) has piled an additional £26bn in costs on to businesses, forcing many to cut hiring or offer fewer shifts.

Kate Nicholls, the chairman of UKHospitality, said: “Hospitality businesses are desperate to offer more hours and create more jobs but the current cost burdens are simply so high that businesses regrettably cannot do so.”

Some 8.5pc of workers in the retail and hospitality sectors – or 320,000 people – are now underemployed by bosses, the research found. These staff are now missing out on around £3.6bn in lost earnings.

Prospects for the industries are worsening because of the higher costs of keeping more staff on the payroll.

Nicholas Found, of Retail Economics, said: “Policy and labour costs are definitely part of the backdrop that’s going on.”

Companies have taken to relying on fewer staff during busy periods, piling pressure on existing workers in pubs, restaurants and supermarkets.

Mr Found said: “It comes as no surprise that workers are stretched.”

The report found a mismatch between the hours employees want and the hours they end up working, as well as poor retention of staff, were compounding challenges for the sectors.

High levels of turnover among more experienced staff also risk piling additional training costs on to businesses.

Figures from the Office for National Statistics showed that more than 150,000 retail and hospitality jobs were lost in the 12 months to May.

Tom Ironside, the director of business and regulation at the British Retail Consortium, said: “Retail has long been a vital source of flexible jobs but those opportunities are increasingly under threat.

“Employment in retail has fallen to a record low as higher labour costs and additional regulatory burdens make it harder to recruit and create new roles.”

Mr Ironside warned that Labour’s Employment Rights Act had made it harder for retailers to offer staff additional hours as demand changed.

The law requires employers to offer statutory sick pay and guaranteed hours to those working zero-hours contracts, leading some retailers to curtail hiring.

A spokesman for the Treasury said: “We have the right economic plan – we’re backing the high street by cutting VAT on family attractions and kids’ meals this summer, reforming business rates, with a £4.3bn support package to limit bill rises, capping corporation tax at 25pc, extending World Cup opening hours and taking action on the cost of living to boost the sector.

“Increasing the national minimum wage boosts pay for over 200,000 young workers and employer NICs are lower when hiring under‑21s.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: The £1bn ‘Manc-hattan’ scandal looming over Burnham’s leadership
data.description:
Andy Burnham made his intentions on housing abundantly clear in the run-up to becoming mayor of Greater Manchester in 2017.

No longer would a local taxpayer housing fund support “large companies which should have used their own finance”, nor would it prioritise “city centre and luxury schemes”.

Instead, Burnham vowed to use the fund for the “long-term goal of an affordable home for all”.

Almost a decade later, Burnham’s promises lie broken.

While he has transformed Manchester’s skyline with dozens of towering new developments, his new era of affordability has failed to materialise.

Instead, the so-called “King of the North” stands accused of bankrolling thousands of high-end luxury flats that many of his working-class supporters can’t afford.

For many, this kind of flip-flopping is exactly the reason why no one knows what the future prime minister really stands for.

As one City source puts it: “Is Burnham on the Left of the Labour Party or just a chameleon?”

The accusations against Burnham concern his nine years in charge of the Greater Manchester Combined Authority (GMCA), which fell under his remit as mayor.

In that role, Burnham oversaw the Greater Manchester Housing Investment Loans Fund (GMHILF), a pot to lend money to developers to build homes in the city.

While this fund was originally the source of Burnham’s ire during his first mayoral campaign, it later became one of his main levers of power.

Burnham used GMHILF to transform Manchester into what will likely be Europe’s fourth-tallest city. The revolution was such that the city earned the nickname “Manc-hattan”.

Through a cluster of towering glass structures, Burnham created a skyline to match Manchester’s rapid urban growth.

In doing so, he also cemented one man’s fortune forever.

Renaker, founded by Daren Whitaker, was awarded the bulk of Burnham’s cash to deliver half a dozen high-end skyscrapers in the heart of Manchester.

Whether that be Deansgate South Square South Tower, the tallest building outside of London, or Three60, a cylindrical skyscraper with panoramas overlooking the city, Burnham approved loans for each.

In just seven years, Burnham’s fund awarded more than £500m in public funds to Renaker, which in turn failed to build a single affordable flat.

That is despite targets set by the local council requiring all new developments in Manchester to designate at least 20pc towards affordable homes.

Instead, Renaker’s luxurious apartments were aimed at affluent yuppies and property investors.

One-bedroom flats were sold for £350,000 apiece in skyscrapers that contained dog spas, virtual golf simulators and Mahjong rooms.

It is hard to find many Mahjong players in Manchester, which may well explain why hundreds of Renaker flats were sold to buy-to-let landlords in Hong Kong.

Special seminars were even held by Renaker in the Far East to drum up interest.

For Burnham, who once vowed to tackle the “scourge of absent private landlords” that “bedevils much of Greater Manchester”, it was quite the climbdown.

Even so, controversy over Burnham’s Manchester housing record has so far been largely limited to the North West.

However, as he edges closer to Downing Street, Burnham’s skyscrapers may cast a shadow over his political reputation.

Concerns first emerged in Manchester property circles post-Covid, shortly after Renaker’s first skyscrapers started springing up.

Renaker’s first loan from GMHILF came in 2015, when the business was awarded £23m for a 28-storey development.

Over the next nine years, it received a further £555m in taxpayer cash via a dozen different loans, accounting for three quarters of the entire £983m lent out by the GMCA over that period.

In the process, Renaker’s majority owner Whitaker became one of the wealthiest developers in the country, amassing a personal fortune in excess of £700m, according to the Sunday Times Rich List.

That is up from £140m six years ago.

“We didn’t really know where he’d come from,” says one Manchester developer about Whitaker, who founded Renaker in 2006. “Everybody knew that he was getting a bit of government money, but no one knew it was that much.

In a GMCA report released this year, authority officials admitted GMHILF “heavily skews” towards Renaker but claimed it had been “highly successful in accelerating the delivery of new housing”.

According to its figures, Renaker was responsible for building 6,110 of the 10,974 homes bankrolled by the fund. Just 4pc of the taxpayer-backed flats built by other developers were deemed “affordable”.

And while Whitaker has never commented publicly on the loans, Burnham has sought to defend them.

In true Northern style, he said this year that the GMCA had to support Renaker because “London-based lenders wouldn’t give us money”.

“They had this downer on Manchester,” he said.

However, for one rival developer, the loans were too much to bear.

Aubrey Weis first lodged his legal claim against the GMCA in 2024, aimed at exposing the GMCA’s loans to Renaker as “subsidies”.

According to the property tycoon’s argument in court, Renaker had benefitted from “advantageous treatment” from the local authority, which had not provided any “comparable level of financial support” to other developers.

He told the Competition Appeal Tribunal that the GMCA had “distorted the proper and fair operation of the market in and around Manchester”.

John Leech, a Liberal Democrat councillor who sits on Manchester city council, agrees.

After raising concerns with the council in recent years, he still believes the loans “don’t pass the sniff test”.

“Renaker has always repaid their loans and never defaulted,” he says.

“So I can’t really get my head around why it was necessary for Renaker to keep borrowing money from the GMCA when they had a record that could justify being able to get loans of similar interest from other providers.

“It shouldn’t have been necessary in my view for the Authority to keep lending them such enormous sums of money.”

Scrutiny heightened after it emerged Whitaker moved to Monaco earlier this year, according to Companies House filings, only to boomerang back 24 hours later.

Questions were also raised after The Telegraph revealed Whitaker took £40m in dividends from a development company backed by the GMCA.

That was despite restrictions supposedly blocking any payouts of this nature.

Throughout the process, the GMCA has held firm and denied wrongdoing, albeit occasionally refusing to answer certain questions.

For example, rather than address filings concerning Whitaker’s move to Monaco, a GMCA spokesman said at the time: “All loans to the Renaker group have been made to UK-registered companies.”

The GMCA claimed to have been vindicated last year when the Competition Appeal Tribunal rejected Weis’s claim outright.

A spokesman said: “A case was brought to the Competition Appeal Tribunal about our housing investment loans fund and we won on every count.

“An experienced tribunal said categorically that these loans did not amount to subsidies. They stood behind our due diligence processes and found that we had ‘substantial protection’ against any risk.”

The case has since been escalated to the Court of Appeal, raising fresh allegations over the loans.

In particular, Burnham’s fund has been accused of distributing cash to Renaker without carrying out proper due diligence.

A hearing took place this month and a ruling is expected to be handed down in the coming weeks.

The GMCA spokesman added: “An experienced tribunal said categorically that these loans did not amount to subsidies. They stood behind our due diligence processes and found that we had “substantial protection” against any risk.

“No developer was frozen out and not a single credible or viable proposal was turned down. We have full confidence in the tribunal’s judgment and the expert team behind our processes, and that was the robust case we put forward at the Court of Appeal.”

For Burnham, who declined to comment, the eventual outcome could serve as an unwelcome distraction as he prepares to replace Sir Keir Starmer as prime minister.

In one of his last acts as Manchester mayor, Burnham approved one final housing loan.

Yet rather than partnering with Renaker, he reserved £50.4m for billionaire betting tycoon Fred Done and his development company Salboy.

Confirmed this month, the GMCA approved the payout for a 76-storey tower backed by Oscar-winner Robert De Niro.

Known as Nobu Manchester owing to its partnership with the high-end Japanese restaurant chain, the skyscraper is expected to contain 452 flats, a restaurant and a 160-bed hotel.

The development will also contain a separate 23-storey tower offering 133 social rent homes.

According to some officials involved, taxpayer backing is needed to “deliver a sufficient level of investor return and developer profit”.

Under a government led by Burnham, similar public-private arrangements may soon become the norm if he has any hope of hitting Labour’s goal of building 1.5 million new homes by 2029.

However, whether this is welcomed by anti-capitalists on the Left is another matter.

Even for Burnham, it is a far cry from the intentions laid out nine years ago, when he criticised the way in which GMHILF was being handled.

“While it is clearly building new properties and helping to stimulate regeneration, I cannot see how this approach will solve our housing problems,” he said.

A dozen skyscrapers and a £1bn later, it’s unclear what Burnham the chameleon really stands for.

His free-wheeling approach to housing serves as just one indicator of that.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Builders accused of fixing house prices in £4.5bn legal claim
data.description:
Britain’s biggest housebuilders face a £4.5bn legal claim about allegations they colluded to increase house prices and rip off buyers.

Nine of the UK’s largest construction businesses have been accused of pushing up the price of new-build homes by wrongly sharing information in breach of competition law.

The landmark lawsuit is being brought by Mark McLaren, a consumer champion, on behalf of 700,000 people who bought new-build homes between October 2015 and June 2026.

If the claim succeeds, homebuyers could get compensation payouts worth up to £6,200 each. The case is being brought forward on an opt-out basis, meaning all eligible homebuyers will automatically be included unless they choose not to be involved.

Housebuilders are accused of sharing commercially sensitive information on prices, incentives and sales activity, leading to buyers paying more for new homes during a period of more than a decade.

The case follows an investigation by the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) into whether seven of the UK’s biggest housebuilders broke the law by sharing commercially sensitive information between 2022 and 2024.

The investigation was closed after the housebuilders agreed to implement measures to prevent information sharing and pay £100m towards the construction of affordable homes across the UK.

However, the new legal claim goes further than the CMA’s investigation, alleging the information sharing occurred over a much longer period of more than a decade.

The case has been filed against housebuilders Barratt Redrow, Bellway, Redrow, The Berkeley Group, Bloor Homes, Persimmon, Taylor Wimpey, Vistry Group and Countryside Partnerships.

Mr McLaren, who was formerly a public affairs adviser at Which?, said: “Buying a home is one of the biggest financial commitments most of us will make.

“If, as seems to be the case, housebuilders shared sensitive pricing and sales information with one another instead of competing properly, homeowners across Great Britain may well have been left out of pocket as a result.

“This claim is about standing up for those buyers and ensuring that compensation is delivered to those who deserve it.”

The case will be heard in the UK’s Competition Appeal Tribunal. Mr McLaren will be represented by law firms Geradin Partners and Hausfeld.

Scott Campbell, a partner at Hausfeld, said: “For most homeowners, bringing an individual claim simply isn’t realistic as the cost and complexity put it out of reach.

“That’s why this collective action is so important. It provides a practical route for hundreds of thousands of consumers to seek compensation where they may otherwise have had no way of doing so.”

The case is being funded by Burford Capital, which specialises in bankrolling litigation and has committed up to £29m. Burford puts up capital to fund cases and typically takes a share of any winnings in successful cases to recoup its investment.

Shares in the housebuilders named in the case tumbled on the London Stock Exchange. Persimmon was the worst hit, dropping almost 3pc, while Barratt Redrow, Bellway and Berkeley all fell by more than 2pc.

Berkeley, Bellway, Vistry and Taylor Wimpey declined to comment. The other housebuilders named in the case have been approached for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Union warns factory jobs at risk over Labour’s net zero glass tax
data.description:
Thousands of manufacturing jobs are at risk after Labour’s imposition of a “bottle tax” that penalises glassmakers, a major trade union has warned.

The GMB has accused Labour of punishing Britain’s industrial base through its rollout of the packaging levy, arguing that the policy threatens to send jobs abroad by driving up costs.

It reflects the growing backlash against Labour’s green levies as unions join brewers and manufacturers in warning that higher costs will lead to inflated prices and reduced investment.

The charges form part of the Government’s extended producer responsibility regime, a central plank of its net zero agenda.

The rules force businesses to cover the cost of collecting and disposing of packaging waste, rather than councils. Fees are calculated by weight, meaning heavier materials such as glass incur significantly higher costs than alternatives.

Industry groups say the scheme is distorting the market by making plastic cheaper than glass, despite the latter being “infinitely” recyclable.

The British Beer and Pub Association (BBPA) has said the charges add £124m a year to the beer sector, equivalent to around 6p on a typical bottle, eroding already thin margins and limiting brewers’ ability to invest.

The GMB has warned the policy could put at least 4,750 UK jobs at risk, with higher costs already making overseas production more attractive.

It said the impact would fall disproportionately on regions outside London, undermining Labour’s efforts to boost growth in industrial areas.

Andy Belfield, a regional secretary at the GMB, said: “British workers are seeing their jobs put at risk as providers move away from British glass bottle production.

“These are good, well-paid jobs in our industrial heartlands that Britain can’t afford to lose.”

Emma McClarkin, the chief executive of the BBPA, said the “exorbitant” fees were wiping out the slender profits brewers make on bottled beer, threatening jobs and supply chains.

She said: “The sector is committed to net zero but there is no question that this scheme is punishing brewers, despite them already using a sustainable product that has a high recycling rate.”

Nick Kirk, the director of British Glass, the trade body representing the UK glass industry, said the system placed a disproportionate burden on glass, which accounts for less than 8pc of packaging but more than a quarter of total scheme costs.

He added: “Imports of unfilled glass containers have already risen to 14pc of the total glass placed on the UK market, suggesting that overseas supply is becoming more attractive to brands and retailers as domestic costs rise.”

The criticism comes before a Commons debate secured by Sarah Champion, a Labour MP, who said the policy’s design had been a “catalogue of errors” and warned it was “wholly unworkable and ultimately counter-productive” in its current form.

She added: “It cannot be right that a plastic bottle should be a more cost-effective packaging choice than an infinitely recyclable glass bottle as a direct result of a government policy designed to encourage sustainable and environmentally friendly packaging choices.”

The Government was approached for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: South East Water warns customers will be cut off as reservoirs run dry
data.description:
South East Water has told customers to brace for fresh shortages as the beleaguered utility’s reservoirs start to run dry.

The company warned that some customers would lose supplies on Wednesday and urged them to put water aside while they still can.

A spokesman for South East Water said 18,000 customers are currently affected and urged those who still have access to running water to use it for essential purposes only, meaning drinking, washing and cooking.

Matthew Dean, the head of operations control at South East Water, said: “Extremely high demand for tap water in this very hot weather means our stored water reservoirs are running very low in areas across Kent.

“Yesterday, we pumped 660 million litres of water to customers across our region, which is more than 100 million litres more than the average for a day.

“We are doing everything we can to get water into our reservoirs, but some customers will lose supplies this afternoon.”

The company said that Herne Bay and Whitstable on the Kentish coast were among the worst-affected areas as nearby storage reservoirs had reached a “critical” level.

The disruption comes days after more than 1,000 customers served by the supplier across Kent and the South East were left without water over the bank holiday weekend.

The crisis, which was caused by the failure of a pumping station in Charing at the foot of the North Downs in Kent, coincided with the hottest May weekend on record.

South East Water has blamed recent supply issues on the heatwave, while reservoir levels were also affected by one of the driest Aprils on record, with rainfall standing at 38pc of its average level, according to the Met Office.

But the issues follow severe disruption at the end of last year that left about 24,000 homes and businesses across Kent and East Sussex without water for almost a fortnight. Local businesses are estimated to have lost up to £8m as a result of the crisis.

Rosie Duffield, the MP for Canterbury, said: “I am surprised and disappointed that South East Water find themselves in this position yet again, and rather than lurching from crisis to crisis, it suggests that forward planning for increasingly warm temperatures is vital.”

Dave Hinton, the chief executive of South East Water, and Chris Train, the chairman, have both stood down in recent weeks amid intense criticism.

While Mr Hinton rated his company’s response to the incident as an “eight out of 10”, MPs on the environment select committee said they had no confidence in the utility’s leadership and accused bosses of a “culture of unaccountability”.

Feargal Sharkey, who is campaigning for water companies to be forced to invest more heavily in their infrastructure, said: “The recent outage demonstrates how fragile and on the edge of a precipice the whole public water supply is in southern England.”

South East Water added: “We’re sorry for the inconvenience caused and can reassure customers our teams are out working hard to resolve these issues as quickly as we can.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Romesh Ranganathan’s bakery closed by Labour tax rises
data.description:
Labour’s tax rises have forced the closure of an 89-year-old bakery chain part-owned by comedian Romesh Ranganathan.

Sean Coughlan, the managing director of Coughlans Bakery, said in a post on Instagram that he was “devastated” to announce the closure of the family business.

He said the rise in National Insurance costs and business rates had “absolutely smashed” the company.

Mr Ranganathan reposted the video to his followers, saying he was “gutted”. The comedian became a shareholder in the bakery chain in 2024.

The closure marks the end of a business that was in its third generation of family ownership. Founded in 1937 by Jack and Elsie Coughlan, the company had grown to 31 stores across Kent, Surrey, West Sussex and south London.

The announcement comes after repeated tax raids on the retail and hospitality industry by Labour.

Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, launched a £26bn raid on employers’ National Insurance contributions (NICs) in her maiden Budget and the Government has announced inflation-busting increases in the minimum wage, both of which have hit retail particularly hard. Changes in business rates have also impacted many businesses.

Speaking to The Telegraph, Mr Coughlan said: “I don’t think the Government understands.

“All of these things, just putting them on businesses [and] expecting businesses to get the Government out of a hole, for me is very short-sighted.”

He said many of the company’s sudden cost increases were “stuff that was unseen six months ago”. “Sadly, it’s really hit us,” he added.

Mr Coughlan called on Labour to consider reversing the increase in employers’ NICs and recent changes to business rates relief, which he said had caused the levy to nearly double on some of his stores.

“If you can change that, it would allow people to survive, and then grow,” he said.

He said that the rises in taxes, wages and fuel prices were costing the bakeries an additional £20,000 a week.

Alongside the rise in the employers’ National Insurance contributions, which first came into effect in April 2025, Mr Coughlan added that the recent increase in the minimum wage had piled further costs on to the business.

The British Retail Consortium has previously said that companies faced a “tipping point” on business rates and warned that businesses would be forced to close if the tax continued to rise.

The lobby group estimates that businesses in England pay around £27bn in business rates each year.

Mr Coughlan added that the sharp increase in fuel costs since the outbreak of the Iran war had made it much more expensive for the bakery to run its fleet of vans and called on the Government to step in.

He said: “They can lower fuel taxes and levies when that is so high. Whenever there’s been a hike in fuel it hits retail instantly.”

Mr Coughlan said that soaring energy prices following the war in Iran had also weighed on the business. The bakery’s fuel bill rose from £3,000 a month to £6,000 as oil and gas prices climbed in the wake of the conflict in the Middle East.

The recent heatwave was “the nail in the coffin” for the business, he added, with the bakery making about 50pc less than usual despite outgoings remaining the same.

Mr Coughlan said that the bakery had gone into voluntary liquidation to pay its employees and suppliers.

The bakery chain employs around 170 staff. Many of them have worked for the family business for more than 20 years.

He said: “It’s just heartbreaking. We spent the whole of yesterday in tears with all of our team, because our team is a genuine family. It felt like a funeral yesterday.”

A government spokesman said: “We have the right economic plan. We are reforming business rates, including a £4.3bn support package to limit bill rises, while 900,000 employers – more than 40pc of all employers – are expected to pay no employer NICs in 2030-31. Generous employer NICs reliefs also remain in place.”

data.title: Romesh Ranganathan’s bakery closed by Labour tax rises
data.description:
Labour’s tax rises have forced the closure of an 89-year-old bakery chain part-owned by comedian Romesh Ranganathan.

Sean Coughlan, the managing director of Coughlans Bakery, said in a post on Instagram that he was “devastated” to announce the closure of the family business.

He said the rise in National Insurance costs and business rates had “absolutely smashed” the company.

Mr Ranganathan reposted the video to his followers, saying he was “gutted”. The comedian became a shareholder in the bakery chain in 2024.

The closure marks the end of a business that was in its third generation of family ownership. Founded in 1937 by Jack and Elsie Coughlan, the company had grown to 31 stores across Kent, Surrey, West Sussex and south London.

The announcement comes after repeated tax raids on the retail and hospitality industry by Labour.

Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, launched a £26bn raid on employers’ National Insurance contributions (NICs) in her maiden Budget and the Government has announced inflation-busting increases in the minimum wage, both of which have hit retail particularly hard. Changes in business rates have also impacted many businesses.

Speaking to The Telegraph, Mr Coughlan said: “I don’t think the Government understands.

“All of these things, just putting them on businesses [and] expecting businesses to get the Government out of a hole, for me is very short-sighted.”

He said many of the company’s sudden cost increases were “stuff that was unseen six months ago”. “Sadly, it’s really hit us,” he added.

Mr Coughlan called on Labour to consider reversing the increase in employers’ NICs and recent changes to business rates relief, which he said had caused the levy to nearly double on some of his stores.

“If you can change that, it would allow people to survive, and then grow,” he said.

He said that the rises in taxes, wages and fuel prices were costing the bakeries an additional £20,000 a week.

Alongside the rise in the employers’ National Insurance contributions, which first came into effect in April 2025, Mr Coughlan added that the recent increase in the minimum wage had piled further costs on to the business.

The British Retail Consortium has previously said that companies faced a “tipping point” on business rates and warned that businesses would be forced to close if the tax continued to rise.

Mr Coughlan added that the sharp increase in fuel costs since the outbreak of the Iran war had made it much more expensive for the bakery to run its fleet of vans and called on the Government to step in.

Generous employer NICs reliefs also remain in place.”

data.title: South East Water breaches Ofwat licence after supply chaos
data.description:
South East Water has breached its operating licence just days after it was struck by fresh supply chaos, leaving thousands of customers without water during the heatwave.

The company’s debt has been downgraded by ratings agency Moody’s, meaning it no longer meets licence conditions imposed by the regulator Ofwat.

South East Water confirmed that Moody’s had revised the rating on its senior secured debt from Baa3 – the lowest rung of “safe” investment territory – to Ba1 – which is “junk” status.

As a condition of its operating licence with regulator Ofwat, South East Water is required to hold at least two investment-grade credit ratings at all times.

With the Moody’s downgrade, the company has lost one of those ratings and is now in breach of that requirement.

It puts South East Water at risk of a fine, with Ofwat able to issue penalties of 10pc of a company’s annual turnover if it breaches its operating licence.

An Ofwat spokesman said: “We are aware of Moody’s downgrade, which reinforces our position that a comprehensive financial and operational turnaround in the company’s operations is essential.

“The downgrade puts the company in breach of its licence. We are therefore engaging with them on potential undertakings to secure their compliance by regaining the necessary investment grade rating.”

South East Water said its board was “constructively engaging with Ofwat to agree certain commitments” to restore compliance, and added that it “maintains strong liquidity and a resilient capital structure”.

Henry Tuffnell, an MP on the environment, food and rural affairs select committee, said: “South East Water’s loss of their operating licence is long overdue. For too long the company has acted with impunity and it is right that there are finally consequences for their continued failure.

“I hope with new leadership and a radical cultural overhaul within the company that customers will finally start to get the service they deserve.”

The downgrade by Moody’s came as South East Water warned it was still facing supply issues amid “extremely high demand during the very hot weather”.

On Thursday evening, the company said 8,000 customers in Whitstable, Kent, are still without water, while another 14,000 customers in Tankerton, Ashford, and its surrounding areas were facing low water pressure or patchy supply.

Steve Benton, South East Water’s incident manager, said: “We are continuing to ask customers ... to use water for essential purposes only – for example drinking, washing and cooking.”

Parts of Whitstable are expected to see supply return this evening.

The latest issues follow severe disruption at the end of 2025 that left around 24,000 homes and businesses across Kent and East Sussex without water for nearly a fortnight.

The Drinking Water Inspectorate concluded an investigation into the outage in April, with the findings pointing to “systemic and repeated failings across both operational control and emergency management arrangements, resulting in serious consumer impact”.

Earlier this month, MPs on the environment, food and rural affairs select committee released a critical report of the utility’s handling of the supply problems.

After this report, Dave Hinton, the chief executive of the utility, and Chris Train, its chairman, resigned.

Separately, Ofwat is planning to fine the company £22.5m for repeated supply failures affecting almost 287,000 customers between 2020 and 2023.

data.title: Morrisons to sell dozens of pharmacies in scramble to cut costs
data.description:
Morrisons has put dozens of pharmacies up for sale in a fresh effort to cut costs.

The private equity-owned supermarket has launched a sale process after concluding that many of its in-store pharmacies are not financially viable.

As a result of the review, property agents have been tasked with selling some of the pharmacies on a store-by-store basis, rather than the entire portfolio.

Those affected are likely to remain open in stores, but under the brand of any eventual buyer.

The move to sell dozens of pharmacies comes after Morrisons’ losses plunged to £381m last year, fuelled by steep borrowing costs.

Rising debt pressures have led bosses to reduce borrowings, which fell to £3.1bn from £3.5bn last summer.

The sale of pharmacies is the latest attempt to strip back the business and focus increasingly on traditional retail.

A separate restructuring last year triggered the closure of four pharmacies, alongside 52 cafés, 17 convenience stores, 35 meat counters and 13 florists.

This led to more than 300 jobs being put at risk of redundancy, as bosses identified areas “where the costs of operations are significantly out of line with usage, volumes, or the value that customers place on them”.

Rami Baitieh, chief executive of Morrisons, said the cuts were a “necessary part of our plans to renew and reinvigorate Morrisons”.

Morrisons is pursuing a turnaround strategy after Mr Baitieh said the business had been going through a “challenging period”.

The retail boss blamed weaker consumer spending among poorer shoppers, many of whom have been affected by price rises.

This has led to Morrisons struggling to boost its share of the grocery market, which is at 8.4pc.

That is up narrowly from 8.5pc this time last year, although it is well below the 9.9pc it achieved in October 2021, when it was bought by private equity firm Clayton Dubilier & Rice in a £10bn deal.

This has prompted speculation that Morrisons could lose its position as Britain’s fifth-largest supermarket to Lidl, which now boasts 7.7pc of the grocery market.

Morrisons is not alone in seeking to sell off pharmacies, as the sector has been battered by rising costs in recent years.

Around 1,400 pharmacies have shut in England since 2017, according to the National Pharmacy Association, leaving many households unable to access medicine in their areas.

This has been blamed on rising wage costs and a shortfall in how much the NHS pays pharmacies.

Boots has also closed hundreds of pharmacies in recent years, while LloydsPharmacy, which had 138 sites, collapsed in 2023.

Rachel Reeves has piled more pressure on pharmacies over the past year by increasing business rates and the minimum wage.

Supermarkets have also come under pressure from rising taxes under Labour, most notably by the Chancellor’s decision to increase employer National Insurance rates.

Speaking last month, Mr Baitieh pleaded with the Government to avoid hitting retailers with any extra costs.

He said: “I would say, please, no more. This is very important for the market. We expect inflation to go up.”

Morrisons declined to comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: MPs demand supermarkets tell shoppers if products aren’t British
data.description:
Supermarkets should add warning labels telling shoppers if their sandwiches or sausage rolls have been produced using imported meat, MPs have urged.

A group of more than 40 rural Labour MPs has called on the Government to order retailers to add so-called “food origin labelling” to more meat-based produce to make it clear that the animals were reared in Britain.

Currently, if a person buys a chicken or steak, the country of origin must be prominently displayed, but there is no such requirement for processed foods, such as packaged sandwiches or sausage rolls.

Bacon that is cured and packed in the UK, meanwhile, can be labelled as “British”, even if the pork was originally imported from Europe.

Campaigners have argued that imported meat is farmed to much lower welfare standards than British produce, meaning UK farmers are effectively undercut.

Tim Bonner, the chief executive of the Countryside Alliance, which has backed the campaign, said: “We have long campaigned for clearer food labelling and a fairer marketplace for British farmers, so we strongly welcome this campaign from the Labour Rural Research Group.”

The Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra) has estimated the cost of implementing transparent labelling to be less than £4m. The benefit to farmers’ incomes is expected to be £48m.

Under Sir Keir Starmer, Labour has widely been criticised for going to war with rural Britain because of issues such as the family farm tax and trail hunting.

‘Consumers can play their part’

The Labour Rural Research Group represents some of the last remaining Labour members in the countryside.

Noah Law, the Labour MP for St Austell and Newquay and a member of the group, said: “Honest labelling will ensure that consumers can play their part in backing British farmers.

“If we bring in honest labelling, farmers win, animals win and shoppers win.”

The proposal for honest labelling is modelled on Australia’s long-running “Made in Australia” system.

Australia’s scheme has operated for nearly a decade. The Countryside Alliance claims that the labels are recognised and understood by 99pc of Australian shoppers and have been welcomed by Australian retailers and farmers.

Jerome Mayhew, the Conservative MP for Broadland and Fakenam, told The Telegraph that products often had Union Flags on them when they are actually full of meat and produce that has been farmed abroad.

Mr Mayhew said: “The Union Jack must be a guarantee, not a marketing gimmick. Farmers in my Norfolk constituency produce food to some of the highest quality standards in the world. They are rightly proud of this.”

Labour launched its “farming roadmap” last week, its long-term plan for British agriculture, which was seen as an attempt to win back favour with rural producers.

However, farmers criticised suggestions in the plan that they should cut down on high-emission livestock farming in exchange for growing more “oilseeds and pulses”, such as lentils, to capitalise on rising veganism.

A spokesman for Defra said: “Under existing rules, misleading the consumer is not allowed as food that is not classed as UK origin cannot be sold or labelled as being British. 

“Fundamentally, this Government is putting our full weight behind farmers with £11.8bn of investment to give them the funding, tools and opportunities they need to succeed and grow their businesses to feed the nation.”

data.title: One in eight Britons to take weight loss drugs by 2027
data.description:
One in eight Britons will be taking weight loss drugs by 2027, a new report has predicted.

The number of UK adults taking glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) weight loss medication will more than double from three million to seven million by next year, according to analysis by consulting firm PwC Strategy&, accounting for 13pc of the population.

This uptick will be fuelled by the approval of a new Wegovy pill, which will make weight loss treatment easier and more accessible.

This is expected to trigger a sharp drop in the sale of sweets and crisps, according to PwC, as the rollout dramatically changes consumer spending patterns.

Those already taking the medication cut their spending on snacks and sweet treats by 70pc and spent 60pc less on sugary drinks, PwC Strategy& found in a survey of more than 2,300 adults.

The latest findings have been released just weeks after the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) approved a tablet version of the blockbuster weight loss drug Wegovy.

This is the UK’s first GLP-1 pill for weight loss and can now be prescribed to adults with obesity.

It means that millions of Britons can access GLP-1 drugs, which mimic gut hormones to regulate people’s appetites, without needing to administer them by a weekly injection.

The pills will soon be rolled out across major retailers including Boots, Morrisons and Superdrug.

However, they are not yet available on the NHS. Before this can happen, the pill will need to be evaluated by the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence.

While businesses selling confectionery or fast food will suffer from a drop-off in spending on unhealthy foods, those selling healthy products are likely to see a surge in sales.

GLP-1 users spent 60pc more on fresh food, 54pc more on high-protein options and 40pc more on vitamins and supplements, PwC Strategy& found.

They also spent 30pc more on fitness and an additional 40pc on clothing.

These spending patterns are likely to continue even after they have lost weight and stopped taking the drugs.

More than 80pc of those who had stopped treatment said they maintained at least some of the dietary changes they had made while taking the GLP-1s.

Jacqueline Windsor, head of retail at PwC UK, said: “GLP-1 is the most consumerised medication today. Who your GLP-1 customers are, and how your category changes, will dictate how brands and retailers compete.

“We’ll see consumer businesses reshape their portfolios to manage near-term risk.”

More than a quarter (28pc) of GLP-1 users said they would maintain their current levels of spending on the drugs even if the cost of living rose.

The drugs, which are only prescribed on the NHS for those with severe obesity, have predominantly been purchased by wealthier households, but are becoming increasingly mainstream.

Of current users, 6pc came from lower-income households, compared to 20pc for wealthier households.

Adoption of weight loss drugs has so far been highest among women, who account for 60pc of current users.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Brazil’s jet champion weighs gamble to take on Boeing and Airbus
data.description:
During the century that Christ the Redeemer has towered over Rio de Janeiro, the art deco statue has become deeply symbolic of Brazil’s status as the world’s largest Catholic country.

Yet when it was transformed into a marketing tool for planemaker Embraer earlier this month, residents of the city barely batted an eyelid.

Latin America’s leading planemaker is held in such high regard that a light show projecting its logo on to the 100ft statue was even blessed by a local priest.

Ostensibly a celebration of Brazilian aviation, the publicity stunt highlighted Embraer’s new-found confidence after an avalanche of sales for its regional jets, images of which were also beamed onto the Rio monument.

That swagger could be about to lead Embraer into one of the biggest gambles in the history of aviation as it intensifies plans to take on Airbus and Boeing.

Already the world’s third-largest planemaker, the firm is contemplating a move into the market for narrow-body jets, where Airbus and Boeing have dominated unchallenged for the past four decades.

While Embraer has flirted with such a step before, bosses said pressure had stepped up after Airbus and Boeing struggled to keep pace with delivery schedules.

The delays, driven by supply chain bottlenecks, have stretched waiting times for the most popular versions of the Airbus A320 – the best-selling jet ever – and the rival Boeing 737 to almost a decade.

The two models, seating 150 to 200 people, form the core of airline fleets around the world, with low-cost operators such as Ryanair and easyJet deploying them in their hundreds.

Flag carriers such as British Airways also use them to feed passengers onto intercontinental flights.

Arjan Meijer, the chief executive of Embraer’s commercial aviation business, said carriers have been bombarding him with pleas to commit to building a new narrow-body aircraft to meet the hole left by Airbus and Boeing.

“We are getting inbounds from customers saying it would be great if you built a narrow-body,” he said. “It’s very pleasing to hear because not everyone can build an aircraft like that.”

Meijer said a decision on whether to take on Airbus and Boeing is under “constant review”, with Embraer’s engineering teams stepping up work on the technologies that a new narrow-body plane would require to gain an edge over the A320 and 737.

Embraer’s flagship plane is the E2, best known in Britain as the largest aircraft flying from London City Airport. It has two seats either side of the aisle rather than three seats like an A320 or 737.

While Embraer has never built a full-size airliner like the 737 or A320, the E2 is actually bigger than an A320 – 12 feet longer in fact.

It also makes a military transport plane, the C-390, with a fuselage as wide as the A320.

Company insiders at its São José dos Campos plant near São Paulo say crossing its two flagship products means it already has the building blocks for a challenger to Airbus and Boeing.

Meijer said Embraer would not be bounced into a decision, given the financial risks involved.

“We’re not just going to jump in and do that because customers want us to. It would be a huge undertaking because it’s a completely different market,” he said.

“Our problem is we can only spend a dollar once. My colleagues from defence and from executive jets would love to do something new too.”

Focusing minds at Embraer is the progress made by China in developing the C919 narrowbody.

While the jet has yet to secure clearance to fly in Europe or North America despite first flying more than nine years ago, manufacturer Comac is on course to become an established global player next decade.

Adding to the sense of jeopardy, Meijer said, is the fate of Bombardier, the Canadian firm that was Embraer’s arch rival in the regional jet market.

It was pushed to the brink of bankruptcy by a foray into the narrow-body market.

While lauded, Bombardier’s CSeries model cost billions to develop and struggled to win orders when faced with the sales machines of Airbus and Boeing.

Bombardier was ultimately carved up, handing control of the CSeries to Airbus for one Canadian dollar. Renamed the A220, the plane is now the chief rival to Embraer’s E2, which outsold it last year.

Meijer said that taking a punt on the airliner market would “of course” be risky, and that launching a new aircraft programme was akin to “betting the company” on a single project.

“We had a great example of a company that launched a brand new aircraft, and is not with us any more in the commercial space,” he said.

“You have to look at the whole lifecycle. That’s why this is such an enormous decision that you’re not going to take overnight.”

There is also a question mark about whether Embraer missed its chance to jump into the single-aisle market when Boeing was laid low by the crisis around the 737 Max – a development that also scuppered the US giant’s plans to take over the Brazilian firm.

The latest version of the jet was grounded worldwide in 2019 after two fatal crashes, at which point Boeing had agreed to acquire an 80pc stake in Embraer for $4.2bn (£3.1bn).

With Boeing distracted and Covid sweeping the world the next year, the deal fell apart.

The Max returned to the skies after 20 months, but was idled again after a mid-air door panel blowout that revealed widespread manufacturing and quality-control problems.

Boeing has since recovered to the extent that it outsold Airbus last year, though Meijer said Embraer’s calculations had always assumed that it would come storming back.

“In our business, in every market, you have to accept that you’ll compete with very capable competitors,” he said.

“People thought we couldn’t compete with Airbus, but in the E2, I think we have a magnificent product, and we’ve been able to win a lot of campaigns against the A220 around the world.”

Meijer said Embraer, which has also outlasted storied names including Fokker, Saab and BAE Systems in the passenger aircraft business, has “people who are doing nothing else every day but look at where the market is heading. At the moment we are a Brazilian company with a global presence. But what we are pursuing now is to become a truly global one, wherever that takes us”.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Axel Springer completes Telegraph takeover
data.description:
The Telegraph is at last secure under the ambitious ownership of Axel Springer, beginning a new era at the start of its 172nd year.

Europe’s largest news publisher confirmed it had won all the necessary regulatory approvals and had completed the £575m takeover on Tuesday.

It means that after a testing three-year odyssey The Telegraph is now under the ultimate stewardship of 63-year-old Mathias Döpfner, Axel Springer’s chief executive since 2002 and latterly its controlling shareholder.

In a meeting with staff in the newsroom, streamed worldwide to the publisher’s network of businesses, he revealed that Carolin Hulshoff Pol, the chief executive of the flagship German title Bild, will transfer to the top job at The Telegraph.

Anna Jones, who led The Telegraph’s business during its years without an owner, is to hand over to Ms Hulshoff Pol over the summer and then take up a role representing Axel Springer on the board of a marketing joint venture.

Mr Döpfner said: “Today is a day we have worked towards for a long time, and one we will always remember.”

Ms Hulshoff Pol, 48, who first joined Axel Springer in 1999, said: “The Telegraph is one of the world’s great news brands, with an exceptional journalistic tradition and a clear commitment to serving its readers. It is a privilege to join Telegraph Media Group at such an important moment.”

She will be joined by a new finance chief, Patrick Wehrmann, another veteran senior Axel Springer executive.

Ms Jones said: “My mission over the past three years was to get Telegraph Media Group into the right hands and having Axel Springer as the owners is the very best outcome for this business.”

With the support of Friede Springer, the 83-year-old widow of the publisher’s eponymous founder, Mr Döpfner oversees a portfolio of dozens of digital and print titles, and more than 10,000 staff.

The empire includes the tabloid Bild and broadsheet Welt, and the international outlets Politico and Business Insider. Mr Döpfner tried to buy The Telegraph in 2004 but was outbid.

But after more than two decades of restless expansion, Axel Springer this time had the financial firepower to swoop late and decisively on a protracted sale process. Last year its revenues were €2.2bn (£1.9bn) and adjusted earnings climbed 12.9pc to €364m.

The Telegraph is in line to become the spearhead of Mr Döpfner’s stated ambition for Axel Springer to become a leading force in journalism in the United States, bolstered by enthusiastic adoption of artificial intelligence technology.

Mr Döpfner has discussed plans to target what he believes is a large American audience on the centre-Right of politics that is poorly served by a mostly liberal mainstream media and conservative outlets that tend towards unattractive extremes.

He said: “Axel Springer and The Telegraph share strong commitments to freedom, values, a tradition of embracing and pioneering technological change, and an entrepreneurial will to actively shape the future. This creates a strong foundation for further accelerating our AI-powered digital transformation.”

Chris Evans, the editor of The Telegraph, said: “These are exciting times for The Telegraph. Axel Springer and we have much in common. We share the same values.

“We also share the same vision and the same ambition. We believe there are many opportunities to grow The Telegraph, both in the UK and overseas. After three difficult years without owners, we look forward to stoking up the engines and setting forth on a new voyage.”

Established among the rubble of Hamburg in 1946 under a British press licence, Axel Springer effectively has a written constitution underpinning its political values, which it calls “the essentials”.

It offers explicit support for freedom, free speech, democracy and the rule of law. Political and religious extremism are explicitly rejected, free-market economics embraced and the alliance between Europe and the US considered fundamental.

Axel Springer also supports Israel’s right to exist and strongly opposes anti-Semitism. This element of “the essentials” has become more controversial inside the company as the coalition government led by Benjamin Netanyahu has pursued military action in the aftermath of the terrorist atrocities of Oct 7.

In April, Mr Döpfner rejected a demand from 75 Politico staff that material promoting “the essentials” be removed from their Washington, DC office. They argued it undermined editorial impartiality.

At a political conference in London last week, Mr Döpfner said that Axel Springer titles were editorially independent and their journalists free to criticise the Israeli government and its decisions, as they were any other. However, they should seek other employment if “the essentials” did not appeal to them, he added.

“We have a hiring policy that is not mainly inclusive, it’s rather exclusive,” said Mr Döpfner. “We want to exclude people who don’t feel attracted by these values.”

Axel Springer has pledged to invest in The Telegraph to deliver expansion in the United States, as well as to enhance its journalism and digital products at home.

The commitment has been welcomed by Telegraph journalists after years of financial straitjacketing under the Barclay family and their massive debts. The Telegraph was required to deliver outsized profits to prop up a crumbling business empire, even as Fleet Street faced into the digital hurricane that flattened the traditional pillars of print circulation and advertising.

The Barclay family’s borrowing eventually caught up with them in the shape of Lloyds Banking Group, which seized The Telegraph via the insolvency courts in early June 2023. The business had been pledged as security on a debt of £1.2bn that was more than a decade overdue.

The bank was repaid in full within months after a complex Barclay gambit which aimed to deliver the keys to a proxy of the United Arab Emirates. Instead it manacled The Telegraph in limbo under caretaker administration, a condition made more lively by a parade of stumbling suitors and some operatic twists.

The crisis prompted a fateful campaign by Telegraph journalists against state ownership of newspapers and in defence of free speech.

Ms Jones said the business had “shown remarkable resilience over the past three years and remained both agenda-setting and award-winning”.

She added: “During this period we have also delivered double-digit digital commercial growth and increased digital subscriptions by a third.”

There was also a diplomatic breakdown, extended embarrassment for novice investment banker George Osborne, an unwelcome cameo by Jeffrey Epstein’s top client, tens of millions of pounds of Telegraph revenues shovelled at lawyers and advisers, unwise threats to the editor and no fewer than four announced sale agreements that proved to be worth less than the paper they were written on.

Regardless, The Telegraph continued to produce crucial journalism and make substantial profits. It lives to tell the tale, and any other, freely into the future.

Mr Döpfner said: “Together we can lead the next generation of trusted media.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Weight-loss drug trial results wipe £147bn off Ozempic maker
data.description:
Almost £150bn has been wiped off Ozempic drugmaker Novo Nordisk after a trial of its new weight-loss drug fell short of expectations.

Shares in the Danish pharmaceutical giant plunged by 15pc on Monday, wiping around €169bn (£147bn) off its market value in just 90 minutes.

Trading was briefly halted in Copenhagen as a result of the sharp sell-off, which drove shares to their lowest level since 2021.

It came after Novo revealed that its new obesity treatment had proven less effective than drugs made by US rival Eli Lilly.

The Danish company’s new drug CagriSema, which is delivered via a weekly injection, achieved weight loss of 23pc after 84 weeks, it said on Monday. This compares to 25.5pc for tirzepatide, the active ingredient in Eli Lilly’s weight-loss drugs Mounjaro and Zepbound.

CagriSema – a combination of the hormone cagrilintide and semaglutide, the compound in its existing drugs Ozempic and Wegovy – had been proposed as a follow-up to today’s weight-loss drugs.

However, the disappointing trial is the latest setback for Novo, which is facing fierce competition from Eli Lilly.

The company said the new drug appeared to be safe and caused only mild gastrointestinal side effects.

Novo was once Europe’s most valuable company and was briefly worth more than the entire Danish economy, but its share price has fallen back as copycat drugs grow in popularity in the US.

It is also contending with demands from Donald Trump, the US president, that pharmaceutical companies slash the cost of medication for Americans.

Novo said last year it would cut 9,000 jobs, or around 11pc of its total workforce, as part of an effort to cut 8bn Danish kroner (£930m) in costs. It has warned that profits and sales could drop by as much as 13pc this year as a result of tough competition and falling US prices.

The company’s changing fortunes have had a knock-on effect on the wider Danish economy, which has become increasingly reliant on the drugmaker.

Funds have invested billions of dollars in Novo, meaning its declining value has wiped out large chunks of Danish savers’ pensions.

The OMX Copenhagen 25 index fell as much as 3.6pc on Monday while the krone also weakened, suggesting investors were pulling funds from Denmark.

Per Hansen at Nordnet said: “Novo Nordisk’s downturn is creating negative spillover effects for other Danish stocks. It is likely foreign investors who are collectively pulling out of Danish companies.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Scratch beneath the shiny Manchester surface and a different Andy Burnham emerges
data.description:
“My grandparents came over three generations ago after serving in the British Army. We’re a developed country, we keep telling ourselves. It doesn’t feel very developed to me,” Nasim Ashraf sighed as he leaned on the counter of his charity’s shop on Oldham’s main shopping drag. 

Half an hour ago I had been in glitzy downtown Manchester, all cocktail bars and clanging metal, building sites on every corner. Here the defining sound was the clattering shutters of empty shop units, as the former mayor’s flagship yellow buses careered, empty, down the narrow roads.

I asked Nasim what he made of Andy Burnham’s much-feted state-backed Manchester growth model. He patiently shook his head and explained that Manchesterism was a way of “sidestepping the issues” in places like Oldham. “What we actually need to do is remove ignorance and then instil the ethos of education. When you create critical thinkers, you create great people and you can create great economies. Then you don’t need to rely on government development projects.”

He proceeded to give me a piece of his mind about how the council gave his charity – the UK Education & Faith Foundation – a £10,000 grant to do up its shop front, only to drive away footfall with a new one-way road system. I pressed Ashraf, did the rise of Burnham not give him even the tiniest cause for hope? 

“The politicians at the top level are all the same,” he tersely replied. As a born and bred Oldhamer he was adamant in his warning to the country. Britain’s problems are not “going to be fixed by Burnham”.

Supporters fete the King of the North as the consummate rebel outsider. His party imagines him as a saviour, unsullied by past establishment failures. Labour MPs have impressed upon me that Burnham has spent the last few years engineering Manchester’s spectacular renaissance – a model that could turbo-boost the wider country. 

At the same time, Burnham’s legacy as Manchester mayor is so fragile that Labour could well lose the election for his successor. The Greens are so confident after early door knocking that they genuinely believe it is a two-horse race between themselves and Reform.

This contradiction between Westminster orthodoxy and the view from the ground raises a question. Does the former Manchester mayor’s chequered record raise doubts about his fitness to be PM?

Burnham’s pitch to the country is that he will liberate Britain from “40 years of neoliberalism”. He has presented his Manchesterism blueprint – which uses public cash and state muscle to jump-start infrastructure-fuelled growth – as a way to break the cycle of austerity, privatisation and stagnation. 

Yet the city is rightly up in arms that Burnham provided £800m of taxpayer-funded loans to a single tycoon, who went on a luxury high-rise construction spree but built very few affordable homes. Such bungling raises uncertainties about his ability to grip big projects, negotiate and stick up for ordinary citizens.

Burnham displays a mysterious lack of interest in the country’s trickiest problems, such as intergenerational worklessness – even though they are the kind of issues you’d expect someone of his background to care about. The number of people on benefits in Greater Manchester has surged in recent years, but Burnham has never made it a central part of his mission to tackle economic inactivity.

This is not for lack of local demand. Everywhere I went, people griped that Burnham’s mania for pimping up buildings is not matched by his enthusiasm for investing in people. The main programme to get locals back into work, Working Well, predates Burnham and has largely failed to change attitudes within the most entrenched “benefits families”.

In Bolton, where one in three working-age Boltonians are economically inactive, one local charity – the Bolton Guild of Help – reckons that it has cracked the formula. They coach locals in painfully basic life skills that the self-reliant take for granted – from the difference between regular and payday loans, to the fact that utilities, like water, are not actually free. 

There is a strong case for scaling such a model. Even the most bespoke back-to-work programme costs thousands of pounds less per head than supporting someone on benefits for a year.

Burnham has visited the Guild and is aware of its work, but appears to ignore its lessons. A child of the 1980s, he has spoken powerfully of growing up in a post-industrial world of subtly shrinking horizons, listening to UB40’s Signing Off album and Trevor McDonald read out the weekly job-loss ticker every Friday on the News at Ten. That Burnham emotionally understands the tragedy unfolding better than most politicians yet has failed to act actually makes it worse.

Burnham’s track record on crime is equally contradictory. When he became mayor, he initially watched silently as Greater Manchester Police declined into one of the worst forces in the country. Even when it had ceased to investigate crime meaningfully, keep proper records, or even answer the phone, Burnham sat on his hands until it was put under special measures.

On the other hand, to Burnham’s credit, he made the right calls under pressure. His appointment of no-nonsense Sir Stephen Watson as the new chief constable to turn the ship around was a stellar move. He has backed Watson to the hilt as he has upset progressives with his refusal to bow to Left-wing narratives about institutional racist policing, and massive expansion of stop and search. 

He may be good at making the right call under pressure, but Burnham seems to struggle to get ahead of a problem. This is not reassuring about his ability to govern an increasingly volatile Britain.

Burnham’s handling of Manchester’s grooming gangs scandal points to a lack of moral courage. After the Rotherham report’s explosive findings, Burnham could have insisted on a similarly unconstrained probe for Manchester, putting the safeguarding of vulnerable girls before reputational risk and community tensions. But the review he commissioned was flimsy by design, with no powers to demand access to documents or witness testimony.

But perhaps the most revealing thing about the former Manchester mayor’s tenure is something painfully simple: he may be a bit of a quitter. Whatever one thinks of Burnham’s abortive clean air zone plans for Greater Manchester, the fact that he abandoned a legally mandated £100m cornerstone project in the face of local pushback – even after almost 500 cameras had been installed across the region – says something about his resolve. 

From his pledge to build more affordable housing to his vow to end homelessness, Burnham made a habit of over-promising, failing to grasp ambitious projects, abandoning them, and then blaming the Westminster establishment.

I can’t help but wonder whether Burnham’s impulse to give in when the going gets tough is ingrained. It’s telling that his parents had to twist his arm to go back to Cambridge University for a second interview following the one he fluffed at St Catharine’s – an English tutor cross-examined him on the parallels between the Canterbury Tales and a modern package holiday. It didn’t go too well.

Some might retort that Burnham is the ultimate comeback kid, having run for leader twice before. It’s easy to interpret his stint up North retrospectively as all part of an ingenious long-term plan. But Burnham himself has intimated that he simply left London in a huff. 

In his book Head North, Burnham admits that he fell out of love with Westminster because the job is “not what you think it is when you go in and you become increasingly frustrated the longer you spend there. Put simply you don’t have the power people think you have”.

The Westminster machine hasn’t changed. The question, then, is whether Burnham has.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Revealed: The staggering impact of traffic jams caused by HS2
data.description:
A bill of £100bn is hard to miss, or to forget, especially for the taxpayers footing it.

But those who happen to live near the route carved out for HS2 have already paid another price – hundreds of millions of hours of their time.

Quite aside from the undeniable but unquantifiable damage the project has done to England’s countryside, since preparatory works first began almost a decade ago, data from the Department for Transport (DfT) shows drivers have had to endure more than 160 associated roadworks on major A-roads and motorways alone.

That, in turn, has already caused some 200 million hours of traffic jams, according to Telegraph analysis. And this is a figure that will continue to grow every day until the repeatedly delayed, wildly over-budget project is scheduled to be completed, sometime in the early 2040s.

With a “shovels in the ground” event in Solihull, then prime minister Boris Johnson formally kicked off HS2’s construction in September 2020. But disruption was already well underway.

Any contractor wanting to close or divert a section of England’s backbone of trunk roads must submit detailed plans to National Highways. The first mention of the project in one of these plans came in November 2017: for the best part of two days, the southbound lane of the A46 was due to be closed between Stoneleigh and Kenilworth in Warwickshire.

Over the eight and a half years since, some 163 sets of roadworks have seen traffic cones deployed in HS2’s name. Telegraph analysis shows that, at the worst point, eight works were simultaneously ongoing in early February 2022, and again throughout March 2024.

All told, 14 stretches of carriageway – from the M25 in Buckinghamshire to the A500 in Staffordshire – have been affected to date; roads used by an average of 56,000 vehicles a day.

With such a high volume of motorists, in a country with 185 licence-holders for every mile of tarmac, the time lost behind the wheel to temporary speed limits and diversions quickly spirals. Every planned roadwork comes with an expectation of the delay it could cause: “slight” (under 10 minutes), “moderate” (between 10 and 30 minutes) or “severe” (more than 30 minutes).

Taking the lower estimate in each case, and assuming each vehicle has 1.5 occupants (the average, according to DfT data), HS2-tagged sites have likely caused the loss of roughly 200 million hours for drivers and their passengers.

And this is just from disruption to the strategic road network, England’s major highways. If comparable data for residential streets and country lanes were available, the total would likely be much higher.

In 2020’s Full Business Case for Phase 1, HS2’s London to West Midlands leg was tipped to deliver £1.20 of economic benefits for every £1 spent – described as “low” value for money by the DfT’s own benchmarking at the time. In 2022, analysis of the whole scheme conducted by civil servants predicted benefits of 90p per pound instead, entailing a net loss for the taxpayer. And that was at a time when the overall budget for the project was still £44.6bn – less than half the latest estimate of £102.7bn.

Then, of course, there is the question of how much time the project, aimed at boosting capacity on Britain’s rail network, will actually save commuters. The most generous reports suggest 35 minutes would be clawed back by HS2 relative to existing trains on the West Coast Main Line between Euston and Birmingham, cutting journey times from over 80 minutes today to 45 minutes.

Meanwhile, the most optimistic forecast for passenger numbers envisages 145,000 people travelling between London and the Midlands each day. Combining those estimates, with HS2 trains operating at maximum capacity and full speed every day of the year, those on board would save some 31 million hours a year compared with existing rail journeys.

But even at this rate, it would take more than six years of flawless service to make up for the 200 million hours lost to roadworks connected to the project to date. This in Great Britain, where only two-thirds of trains arrived on time over the past year, and one in 28 was cancelled, according to the Office of Rail and Road.

Regardless, with no stops between Old Oak Common, in London, and the yet-to-be-built Birmingham Interchange, intercity commuters will be the only people to save time because of HS2. For the other residents of Buckinghamshire, Oxfordshire, Warwickshire and Northamptonshire who have sat in traffic for the best part of a decade on account of the project, there will be no such pay-off.

There will be years of pain to come yet. HS2’s website claims to have “350 active construction sites” with work “at its peak”.

Conceived by Gordon Brown’s government in 2009, plans laid out the following March suggested Phase 1 would be up and running by the end of 2026. That deadline has been extended repeatedly in the intervening years. And last month, with the original completion date mere months away, it was pushed back to 2040.

Announcing the delay, Transport Secretary Heidi Alexander revealed HS2 would now likely cost more than Nasa’s Artemis mission to return astronauts to the Moon. She also confirmed the trains’ speed would be reduced from 223mph to 200mph – just 75mph faster than Avanti’s current “Pendolino” stock – in order to save money.

The sorry developments mean it will take all that much longer for HS2 to make up for lost time, when trains are finally running. A DfT spokesman said: “HS2 is primarily about adding vital seats to an overstretched rail network, and its wider benefits are ignored by this methodology.

“This analysis is simply not comparing like with like. Local road delays and intercity train journeys are completely different things and cannot be meaningfully compared in this way.”
data.author: The telegraph
